Facing a button raise from Minni with 5-9 offsuit in small blind position. This is a marginal hand with poor playability. 5-9o has limited potential to improve and is vulnerable to overcards. I am in the Big Blind with Ten of Spades and Five of Clubs. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button. While the pot odds to call are roughly 2.25:1, T5o is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Folding 2h-Ks from the button. While button position is strong in 3-handed play, 2-K is too weak to play profitably. The deuce kicker is problematic if I hit the King. I am in the Small Blind with King-Seven of hearts. Claude has folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against Minni in the Big Blind. King-Seven suited is a strong hand for a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is the standard play here to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately. A 3x raise to 300 is a standard and effective sizing in this position.

Facing a small‑blind open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 3♥8♥. I’ve already posted 100, so calling costs 200 more (300 total). With stacks ~88–105 BB and a wide SB opening range, 3♥8♥ is a cheap speculative, suited hand worth seeing a flop for the price. I raised pre-flop with King-Seven of hearts and was called by Minni in the Big Blind. The flop comes Five-Six-Deuce rainbow, which is a low board that could hit a Big Blind's calling range. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should continue with a standard continuation bet to take down the pot immediately. King-high still holds significant equity and can improve on many turns. A bet of 300 (half pot) is appropriate to maintain initiative.

I called preflop with 3♥8♥ as a cheap speculative hand. On this flop (5♦6♣2♦) I have no pair, but I do have a gutshot to a 4 (2-3-4-5-6 straight) and backdoor heart possibilities. Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~25% break-even equity (300/(900+300)=25%). My combined chance to improve to a meaningful hand by the river (hitting a 4, pairing a 3 or 8, or completing a backdoor heart) is in the neighborhood of the pot‑odds, and Flash’s small bet is consistent with a wide CB range. Plan: call one street to see the turn and fold to significant raise unless I improve (pair/straight/clear draw).

The turn card 3d is highly coordinated, completing potential straights for any hand containing a 4 (which is very much in Minni's Big Blind defending range) and completing a diamond flush. While I have an open-ended straight draw (any 4 makes a straight), my King-high has lost significant relative value on this board. Since Minni checked, I will check behind to see a free river card and potentially realize my equity or showdown value. Betting here is risky as it's difficult to represent a stronger hand than what Minni might have connected with on this board.

I called preflop and the flop with 3♥8♥ and the turn (3♦) paired my trey — I now have bottom pair. The board (5♦ 6♣ 2♦ 3♦) is wet: a 4 completes a 2-3-4-5-6 straight and three diamonds on board make a flush for anyone holding two diamonds. Flash’s line (preflop raise, flop bet, turn check) is ambiguous — could be pot‑control with a marginal made hand or a slowplayed strong hand/complete draw. With a vulnerable bottom pair I don’t want to inflate the pot or get check‑raised off my hand; checking controls the pot, allows me to realize my showdown equity, and see the river for free. Plan: check the turn, re‑evaluate on the river (call small bets, fold to large pressure unless I improve to two‑pair/trips).

On the river, the board is 5d-6c-2d-3d-Ac. I have King-high (Kh 7h), which has zero showdown value. However, the Ace on the river is an excellent scare card for me to represent as the pre-flop aggressor. The turn was checked through, which might lead Minni to believe I was pot-controlling with a marginal hand or trapping. By betting now, I am representing that the Ace improved my hand (like Ax) or that I have a completed straight or flush. Given Minni's history of folding to pressure on coordinated boards (as seen in Hands #3, #4, and #20), a well-sized bluff here has a high probability of success against her likely range of mid-pairs or missed draws.

I need 750 to call into a 1,950 pot (≈38% break‑even equity). I have only bottom pair (3♥) on a very wet board (5♦ 6♣ 2♦ 3♦ A♣). Flash’s line (SB open, flop c‑bet, check turn, river bet) and the river sizing are highly consistent with value hands that beat me: any Ace (now paired on the river), A‑3/A‑5/A‑6/A‑2 two‑pair combos, pocket 5s/6s/2s, the one combo of 3♣3♠ (very bad for me), and multiple two‑diamond hole combos that completed a flush on the turn. I block very few of those combos and my hand has little showdown value versus this sizing. My prior plan was to check the turn and fold to big river pressure unless I improved to two‑pair/trips — this is that big pressure. Raising would be reckless (bloats the pot with a marginal hand). Fold and preserve chips for a better spot.

I am on the button with 8h 3h. While the button range in a 3-handed game is wide, 8-3 suited is significantly below the threshold for a profitable opening hand, especially against active opponents like Minni and Claude who have shown a willingness to defend their blinds and play aggressively post-flop. I have K♠J♠ in the small blind heads-up with ~82bb effective. This is a strong, playable hand that I want to take the initiative with rather than just completing to 100. I’ll raise to 300 (3x BB) to build fold equity, isolate the big blind, and avoid giving him cheap odds to see the flop. If Claude calls I’ll c-bet on many favorable flops and play aggressively; if he 3-bets a small sizing I will consider calling to play postflop, but I will fold to a very large 3-bet to avoid bloating a pot out of position.

I'm in the big blind with 9h-Tc, a marginal hand at best. While the cost to call is only 200 chips into a 400-chip pot, several factors make folding the correct decision:

1. **Position disadvantage**: I'm out of position as the big blind. This is the worst position in poker, and playing marginal hands out of position is a long-term losing strategy.

2. **Hand strength**: 9-T offsuit is below my calling threshold here. It's not strong enough to profitably play out of position against Minni's raise.

3. **Opponent tendencies**: From the hand history, Minni is showing aggression from the small blind, raising frequently. This confirms the aggression I'm facing.

4. **Stack health**: With 20,275 chips, I have a healthy stack and can be selective. There's no urgency to play marginal hands.

5. **Tournament stage**: At hand 44 of 150, I should prioritize discipline and playing strong hands in position rather than marginal hands out of position.

The fold is disciplined and preserves my chips for better opportunities where I have position or stronger holdings.

I'm on the button with T4o. With two players left and position on both blinds, I should open a wide but constructive range — T4o is marginal to limp but fine to raise for a steal. A standard 3x BB open to 300 (total) applies pressure, keeps the initiative, and defines ranges. If I face a significant 3-bet I'll fold; if called I'll use position to c-bet dry boards and pot-control or fold on wet/connected boards. Raising to 300 balances aggression and pot control with this holding.

Folding 7-9 offsuit from the small blind. This is a weak, marginal hand with poor equity against Minni's raising range from the button. Key factors: (1) Out of position post-flop - I'd be playing from small blind throughout, which is a significant disadvantage; (2) Hand strength - 7-9o has poor equity against most of Minni's raising range; (3) Stack depth - while I have a healthy 20k stack, there's no need to get involved with weak hands when we have good fold equity; (4) Flash is still to act from big blind, creating further complications. This is a standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Six suited (A6c), which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Minni opened from the Button for 3x, a standard play she has made frequently. With 112.5 big blinds, I have plenty of space to play. I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (Six) is weak, and the board is extremely coordinated (Ace-King-Jack). This board favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni), which likely contains many Broadway cards, sets, or even a made straight with Queen-Ten. Leading out into the aggressor on this texture is risky. I will check to see how Minni proceeds. If she bets, I will likely call one street to see if the turn improves my hand or safe-guards the board, but I need to be cautious of her potential two-pairs or straights.

Flop K♦ J♠ A♦ — a very high, connected board that hits the big blind’s calling range hard. My T♣4♠ has no pair, no real backdoor flush, and negligible showdown value. As preflop aggressor I c-bet dry boards, but this one has poor fold equity (BB can comfortably call with many A/K/J/top-pair, broadway and pair combinations), so a continuation bet will often get called and put me in difficult decisions on the turn. With ~80BB effective and a 650 pot, I’ll exercise pot control and use position: check and reevaluate. Plan: fold to a sizable bet, consider a delayed bluff or small pressure on safe-turn cards if villain shows weakness, but avoid bloating the pot with air.

On the turn, the board has paired with a second King (Kd Js Ad Kc). My hand is Aces and Kings with a 6 kicker. On the flop (Kd Js Ad), both Minni and I checked. Her check-back on the flop suggests she likely doesn't have a very strong hand like a King, a strong Ace, or the straight (QT), although she could be trapping. With the board now pairing Kings, any K in her range has me crushed, and most of her Aces (AQ, AJ, AT) also have me out-kicked. Since I am out of position and my hand is essentially a bluff-catcher or a marginal value hand on this extremely coordinated board, checking is the best play to control the pot and see what she does. If she checks again, it reinforces that she is weak; if she bets, I will have to evaluate her sizing and the likelihood of a bluff versus a King or better Ace.

Board: K♦ J♠ A♦ K♣. I have T♣4♠ — no pair, no diamond or heart draw, essentially air. I was the preflop aggressor but the flop was very hostile to my range (A/K/J heavy) and I checked it. The turn pairing the king further reduces my fold equity and makes a bet here likely to be called by Kx/Ax/top pairs and some QJ/TT type hands — hands that beat me and will put me in difficult spots with ~80BB effective and only a 650 pot. Poker + pot control: keep the pot small with air and take a free river. Plan: check and see river; fold to any significant river bet, and only consider bluffing on a very safe river or thin value betting if I actually hit a ten.

On the river, the board is Kd-Js-Ad-Kc-2d. I have two pair, Aces and Kings, with a Six kicker. The action has been extremely passive, with both Minni and I checking on the flop and the turn. Her pre-flop raise from the button usually suggests a wide range, but her checking back on such a coordinated flop (AKJ) and a turn that pairs the King (AKJK) suggests she either has a very weak hand or is potentially trapping with a monster. Since my kicker is weak (6), betting for value is risky because I'm unlikely to be called by worse, and better Aces (AQ, AJ, AT) or any King has me beat. Checking to realize my showdown value is the most prudent play.

I opened preflop with T4o as a steal, then checked the very hostile K♦ J♠ A♦ flop to exercise pot control. A river bet is unlikely to fold out Kx/Ax/Jx hands that beat me and will more often be called by worse or better hands, so bluffing has poor expected value. Calling with As2c on the button in 3-handed play. Position is a significant advantage, and the call of 100 chips is minimal relative to my 20k stack. I’m in the big blind with J♦2♦. Flash (SB) raised to 450 and Claude (button) called, so calling would cost me 350 more into a 650 pot — I’d need roughly 35% equity to justify a call. Calling Flash's SB raise in 3-handed play. Position on the button is crucial, and Flash's raise from the small blind in a 3-way game likely represents a wide opening range. I have flopped a set of tens, which is an extremely strong hand (the second nuts, trailing only pocket Aces). Heads-up flop decision with As2c on board Th 6s Ah. I have top pair with weak kicker after calling Flash's pre-flop raise. Flash now raises to 600 on the flop. I have a set of tens on a board of Th-6s-Ah-Qc. This is a very strong hand. Folding on the turn with A2o. My hand analysis:

SITUATION: I have top pair (Ace) but with the worst possible kicker (2). The board is Th 6s Ah Qc, and Flash has aggressively raised on the flop (600) and again on the turn (1200).

FLASH'S LINE ASSESSMENT: Flash's sequential aggression (preflop raise to 450, flop raise to 600, turn raise to 1200) strongly indicates a premium hand. In 3-handed play, while Flash could have a wide range, the turn raise after I called the flop raise is a reliable strength indicator. Flash likely holds:
- Overpairs (JJ, KK, QQ, AA) - beat me badly
- AK/AQ/AJ - better kickers dominate my A2
- Two pair (AQ, Q6, A6) - now possible with the Queen
- Straight draws are less likely given the aggressive turn action

EQUITY CALCULATION: Against Flash's probable range, my A2 has approximately 20-22% equity (mainly the 2s out on the river for two pair, which only beats underpairs and weak hands). I need ~26% equity to justify the 1200 call against 3400 in the pot. This is a slight negative EV spot.

RISK MANAGEMENT: With 19,175 chips and only one card remaining, folding preserves chips and avoids compounding a questionable A2o call. I already invested 550 chips preflop and 600 on the flop into a weak hand. No need to further chase with inadequate equity.

FOLD is the mathematically sound play.

I am on the button with 6c 7d. While 3-handed play allows for a wider opening range from the button, 67 offsuit is at the bottom of that range. Heads-up vs the big blind with K♦6♦. In the small blind I don’t want to just limp and play OOP; K6 suited is a reasonable opening hand heads-up. I have pocket queens, a premium hand that should definitely re-raise here. Claude 3-bet me to 600 after I opened to 200. Check. Flopped set of queens on a 9-Q-8 board. Minni checked after calling my pre-flop 3-bet, indicating weakness. I should build the pot while maintaining balance. A 600 bet (50% pot) generates value from her mid-pair and broadway card holdings while protecting against draws. I checked the flop with K♦6♦ on 9♥ Q♠ 8♠ and Claude (who 3‑bet preflop) bets 600 into a 1800 pot. Calling 600 gives me 600/(1800+600) = 25% pot odds. My hand has roughly 6 clean outs (~24% to pair by the river) and only a very small chance to pick up a backdoor diamond flush — so my raw equity is marginally below the required break‑even. Worse, Claude’s 3‑bet/c‑bet range contains lots of Qx, spade hands and overpairs that both already beat me and reduce the value of my outs (many pairs I make will still lose). Raising as a bluff is unattractive: I don’t block spade or many strong Q/K combinations and would be over‑leveraged without good equity. With ~73BB effective and plenty of hands left in the tournament, preserving chips is the correct, lower variance play here. Fold.

Button with 4♦8♠ — a marginal, unsuited, disconnected hand but I'm in position in a 3-handed game. If I face a sizable 3-bet I'll fold this hand; only a very small squeeze would be considered as a speculative call with intention to play only with clear equity. Overall: open to seize position and leverage fold equity.

Fold. 8c3s is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand with poor post‑flop playability. I’m in the small blind and will be out of position to the button raiser with the big blind still to act. Three-handed on the button with As3h — open-raising is standard. I have the Ace blocker and position on both blinds, and both opponents have shown a tendency to call rather than 3-bet heavily. K8o is a marginal hand that doesn't justify calling from the small blind position. With Flash still to act and both opponents present, I'm out of position post-flop. I have a pocket pair of sixes on the button in a three-handed game. This is a strong starting hand. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and build value, as pocket pairs play well in short-handed situations.

I'm on the button with 4h3h — a small suited connector with position against the small and big blinds. Stacks are deep (~76BB) so speculative hands have post‑flop value, but multiway pots hurt 4‑3s. I am in the Big Blind with 8c 4s, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the button and Claude 3-bet from the small blind. I am facing a raise and a 3-bet with garbage cards, so there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

I opened to 300 on the button with 4h3h and Claude in the SB 3‑bet to 850. With AK on a dry flop (3d-6c-2h), I should apply pressure with a standard continuation bet. I have 4h3h on a 3d-6c-2h board: a weak pair (a trey) plus a backdoor heart draw and I'm in position. Turn card is Td, bringing board to 3d-6c-2h-Td. I have AK which is king-high and the strongest non-pair holding. Minni is critically short-stacked at 5,925 chips and has been calling down my aggression, suggesting a mid-strength hand. Flash hasn't engaged. I have 4♥3♥ on 3♦-6♣-2♥-T♦: just a weak one-pair (pair of 3s) with very limited improvement possibilities. Claude was the preflop 3‑bettor, c‑bet the flop (900) and now barreled the turn for 1,800 — his line skews towards overpairs/top pairs and strong broadway/AX hands, though he will have some air in his range. The pot is 5,400 and calling 1,800 gives me a post‑call pot of 7,200, so my break‑even equity is 1,800/7,200 = 25%. Naively I have ~14 river outs (two 3s, three 4s, three 10s, three 6s, three 2s) → 14/46 ≈ 30% to improve to two‑pair/trips, so on pure pot‑odds grounds a call is defensible. Several of those outs (2,6,T) can make trips for Claude if he already holds that rank, so some outs are “dirty,” but being in position I can control the size on the river and fold to heavy action when I miss.

Raising here is unattractive: the minimum raise to 3,600 would commit almost my whole stack (would push my total committed to ~5,350 and leave me only ~575 behind) and is a very thin bluff versus a 3‑bettor’s range. Folding surrenders the equity we bought cheaply on the flop. Plan: call to realize equity, see the river, and fold to significant river aggression unless I hit two pair/trips (in which case I’ll extract value). Therefore I will call the 1,800 on the turn (total committed this hand = 1,750 pre/flat + 1,800 = 3,550).

This is a critical river decision with AK (king-high) on a final board of 3d-6c-2h-Td-6d. The river paired the 6, which is significant given my previous aggressive line. Minni is critically short-stacked at 4,125 chips and has been calling my aggression throughout this hand, strongly suggesting a real holding rather than a draw. Key considerations: (1) The river 6 could have significantly helped Minni if they hold a pocket pair (especially TT which now makes two-pair), AQ/AJ (which I'm ahead of), or a 6 (which I'm well behind). (2) My king-high is respectable but vulnerable to the range of hands Minni would call with across multiple streets. (3) Minni's consistent calling pattern (pre-flop 3-bet call, flop call, turn call) indicates genuine hand strength rather than a marginal holding. (4) Given the action and Minni's stack size, they're likely shoving any reasonable hand if I bet, meaning I'd be risking significant chips against an uncertain situation. (5) Checking allows me to see Minni's action and preserve chips if they have a made hand. This is a pragmatic decision prioritizing risk management with a showdown-ready but non-premium hand.

I have 4♥3♥ — only a single pair of threes on 3♦-6♣-2♥-T♦-6♦. Claude was the preflop 3‑bettor and barreled flop (900) and turn (1,800), so his line is heavily weighted toward overpairs, Tx, sets, or otherwise strong hands. The river pairing a six actually increases the likelihood he has a hand that beats me (or that he’s cautious checking a vulnerable value hand).

Betting here would be a pure bluff. The pot is 7,200 and my stack is 4,125; a realistic bluff size (≈1/3 pot ~2,400) needs substantial fold equity (~25%) to be break‑even. Given Claude’s earlier aggression and tendency to call down, his fold rate is likely lower than that, so a bluff is +EV only rarely. Raising is nonsense — it would commit a large portion of my stack with almost no chance. I also don’t have thin value to get called by worse — most hands that check behind will beat me. 

So I check back, preserve chips, and fold to any meaningful river bet.

I'm on the button with Td8d, a suited connector with decent potential. My position is strong (button/late position), and I need to apply pressure here. 

Looking at opponent patterns: Minni has been extremely aggressive, raising from most positions but also folding to pressure. Flash is more selective and conservative. 

With only 100 to call and a speculative but playable hand, I have several options. A raise makes sense here because:

1. Position advantage: I'll act last post-flop if called
2. Blind stealing value: Minni and Flash are both short-stacked relative to mine
3. Hand playability: Td8d plays well in position against likely folding ranges
4. Aggression: I've been selective in recent hands, so my action carries weight

A raise to 250 (putting in 250 total) accomplishes:
- Applying immediate pressure to fold the blinds (good outcome)
- Building a pot with position if called (favorable outcome)
- Using my button position to maximum advantage

This is a standard button raising strategy against relatively weak opposition.

I am in the Small Blind with Queen-Deuce suited. Claude has opened from the Button for 250. While the hand is suited, it is very weak and playing out of position against a deep-stacked aggressive button is not ideal. Calling often leads to difficult post-flop spots, and there is still the Big Blind (Minni) to act who could squeeze. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Facing a button open to 250 (SB folded) I’m heads-up out of the big blind with Ad3c. This is a marginal but defendable hand heads-up: the opener’s range is wide and the price is cheap — 150 to call into a 400 pot (~27% equity needed). With deep stacks I don’t want to 3-bet OOP with a rag Ace and weak kicker; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me realize equity or capitalize on favorable flops. Plan: call to see the flop (250 total committed). On an Ace or strong draw I’ll proceed; on dry boards I’ll be cautious and often check-fold to heavy pressure.

I have top pair (Ad) with a terrible 3-kicker and one club (backdoor) on Ah 6c Tc. Claude was the preflop raiser and his range is wide from button — lots of Axs, broadways, pocket pairs and club hands. Out of position I want thin value and to charge the club draws while keeping the pot controllable with a weak kicker. A half-pot c-bet (~275 into 550) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse Aces, some tens and broadways and makes draws pay, but isn’t so large that I’m committed when I face significant aggression. Plan if Claude raises big: fold most hands (my kicker is poor); if he calls, re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously for value or pot-control depending on the card.

I have Td8d and flopped top pair (tens) on Ah 6c Tc, though with a vulnerable 8-kicker against the ace on board. Minni's flop raise of 275 is relatively small compared to the pot, suggesting she may have a medium-strength hand or be semi-bluffing with a draw. Her pre-flop calling range from the BB is wide, and while she could have an overpair or Ax, I can't fold top pair to a small raise when I'm getting nearly 3:1 odds. The call is also minimal relative to my 15,675 stack (1.75% of chips). I'll see another card to gather more information about the strength of my hand. A raise would be premature without more conviction, and folding would be unnecessarily weak here.

Check for pot control. I have top pair (A♢) with a horribly weak 3-kicker and the turn 4♦ closes the realistic backdoor club-runout, so I don’t want to build a bigger pot and get raised by better Aces or two-pair. Checking keeps the pot small, allows me to call reasonable-sized bets from worse hands or bluffs, and fold to big aggression; if Claude checks behind I can re-evaluate the river and pursue a thin value bet if appropriate.

Minni checked the turn after her small flop raise, which signals likely weakness or a hand that didn't improve. She's given me a free opportunity to apply pressure.

I'm holding Td8d with a pair of tens and an 8 kicker - top pair but with a vulnerable kicker given the ace on board. Minni's pre-flop calling range from the big blind is wide (could be small pairs, weak aces, broadway cards, or draws), and her small flop raise of 275 suggested a medium-strength hand rather than a strong overpair.

The turn check is a weakness indicator. She either:
1. Has a weak ace or middling hand and is slowing down
2. Had a draw that didn't complete
3. Is setting a trap (less likely)

Key factors favoring a bet:
- I showed strength pre-flop with my raise, so continuing aggression is consistent
- Top pair is still likely the best hand against her likely range
- The pot is small (1100), and the turn is a good spot to build it when ahead
- A free river card could hurt me (any King, Queen, Jack, or 9 improves her position)
- Her turn check weakens her hand representation

I'll bet 400, which is about 36% of the pot - a standard pressure bet that shows confidence without overcommitting. This bet accomplishes protecting my hand equity, applying pressure on what is likely the best hand, and gathering information for the river decision.

Call 400. I have top pair (A♦) but the worst possible kicker (3♣) and the turn 4♦ killed my backdoor club draw, so this is a hand I want to keep the pot controlled with. I checked the turn for exactly this reason — to deny action and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal kicker — and Claude's small bet (400 into a ~1500 pot) looks like a probe that can include worse Axs, tens, pairs, club/straight attempts and bluffs. The price is cheap (≈400 to win ~2300, so I need only ~17% equity), so calling preserves showdown equity and lets me fold to clear strength on the river if necessary. I won't inflate the pot by raising OOP with this weak kicker; I'll call and re-evaluate on the river (fold to large turn/river aggression, consider thin value if he checks and a safe card arrives).

Pot is 1,900 and I hold A♦3♣ — top pair but the worst kicker. Claude opened pre, called my flop bet, then fired a small probe on the turn (400 into ~1,100) and I called. That line is consistent with a wide range: worse A‑x, tens, medium pairs, missed club draws, and some bluffs — but it also contains two‑pair/sets and A‑kicker combos that beat me. The river 9♠ doesn't complete any obvious club draws and mainly adds a few 9x combos to his range; it doesn't improve my kicker situation. With a terrible 3‑kicker and deep stacks I don't want to bloat the pot and face a raise from AT/TT/66/44 or a better A. Betting for thin value will fold out a lot of worse hands and get raised by my dominated Aces, so it's a losing line long‑term. Best line is pot control: check and go to showdown (and if Claude bets a small probe I can call; fold to large river raises). I check to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand and to realize my showdown equity.

Minni's turn and river checks strongly indicate weakness. Her pattern (small flop bet → check turn → check river) suggests a medium hand that didn't improve, likely a weak ace or small pair. I hold top pair (pair of tens) which is almost certainly the best hand. Betting 500 (26% of the 1,900-chip pot) extracts value from weaker holdings while maintaining the aggressive tempo I've established in this hand. If she has a strong ace she'll call anyway; if she has nothing, I win the pot; if she has a weak hand, she might call or fold either way I gain maximum value for my likely best hand.

Claude opened from the button, called my flop bet, barreled a small turn and now makes a small river probe of 500 on A♥‑6♣‑T♣‑4♦‑9♠. I hold A♦3♣ — top pair with an awful kicker. His line is wide and includes a lot of A‑x (many of which beat me), some medium pairs (TT, 66, A4 two‑pair), and missed club/straight bluffs. Calling 500 to win ~2,900 (pot after his bet) gives me ~14.7% break‑even equity, which is cheap against a mixed value/bluff river sizing. Raising would be negative EV (I get called by better Aces and fold out worse), folding surrenders too many worse A‑hands and bluffs. Best play is to call and take my (small) showdown equity.
Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong hand for opening on the button in a three-handed game. I'll raise to 300 to take control of the hand and put pressure on the blinds.

Flash opened to 300 on the button. I hold Q♥T♥ in the small blind — a hand with good playability, suitedness and useful blockers to broadway combinations. Calling 250 keeps me out of position and often leads to multiway pots where QTs loses value; a tiny reraise (to 500) is too weak and invites easy flats/4-bets. I want to take the initiative and apply pressure, isolate the button, and fold out weaker speculative hands from the big blind. I’ll 3-bet to a sensible, medium size (900): large enough to gain fold equity and extract value from worse Qx/Tx/suited broadways, but not so large that I’m automatically committed vs a shove/4-bet. Postflop plan: c-bet often heads-up on favorable textures, proceed cautiously if Claude cold-calls (or if I face a sizable 4-bet I’ll reassess and likely fold without strong reads).

I'm facing a 3-bet to 900 with AK in the Big Blind. This is a premium hand that should be played aggressively. With 38% pot odds and strong hand strength, a 4-bet raise is appropriate. I'm raising to 2400, approximately 2.7x Minni's bet, which clearly represents strength, builds the pot in my favor, and puts maximum pressure on both opponents. This is optimal with one of poker's best starting hands.

I opened with a standard button raise of 300. Minni 3-bet from the small blind, and Claude then 4-bet from the big blind. While Ace-Eight offsuit is a strong button opening hand, it is significantly behind the range of a cold 4-bet in this spot. Claude's 4-bet after Minni's 3-bet suggests extreme strength (typically JJ+, AQ+). To protect my stack and avoid playing a dominated hand out of position in a bloated pot, folding is the most disciplined and professional move.

I 3-bet to 900 to apply pressure and isolate, but Claude’s reraise to 2400 is a sizable response and represents a very strong range (QQ+/AK, maybe AQs at the margin). The pot is 3600 and calling requires 1500 more, so I need ~1500/(3600+1500) ≈ 29.4% equity to break even. Q♥T♥ OOP vs a typical big blind 4‑betting range does not have that equity or the post‑flop playability advantage (and a min 5‑bet to 3900 would commit too much and still won’t fold out the majority of his value hands). I had planned to fold to a sizable 4‑bet without strong reads, so I’ll conserve my stack and fold here.

I have A♠6♥ on the button in a 3-handed pot — a clear spot to open with position and an ace-blocker. The pot is 150 and it’s 100 to call; I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB). This size gives good fold equity against the blinds, matches table raising tendencies, and still keeps the pot manageable with a marginal but playable hand. Plan: if called I’ll play aggressively in position (c-bet favorable Ace/dry boards, pot-control or check/call on coordinated boards and backdoor draws). If I face a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold most of the time; I may call a very small squeeze depending on exact sizing and opponent, but won’t inflate the pot out of position with A6o.

With pocket Queens in the small blind, I should re-raise against Minni's button raise. QQ is a premium hand that's likely ahead of most of Minni's opening range. A re-raise to 750 (2.5x the original raise) builds the pot while maintaining control, applies pressure to both opponents, and leverages my larger stack. This is more effective than calling, as it takes initiative and keeps Minni's positional advantage from being exploited postflop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Eight offsuit. Minni opened from the Button and Claude 3-bet from the Small Blind. T8o is far too weak to call a 3-bet, especially out of position against the aggressors. Claude has shown significant aggression recently, and I have no reason to believe this is a light 3-bet. Folding is the only sensible play to preserve my stack.

Call to 750 total. A6o on the button is worth defending in position — I have an ace-blocker and good postflop playability. Claude’s 3-bet to 750 (after my 300 open) is a relatively small squeeze that can contain a lot of broadways/bluffs, and the pot odds to call are acceptable. With ~116bb effective I prefer to keep the pot manageable and use position: c-bet or apply pressure on dry Ace-high flops, control or check-call on coordinated boards, and fold to decisive heavy action on later streets. I will not 4-bet here with A6o for value.

With pocket queens on a dry flop (5d-Jc-Th), I'm following up on my pre-flop 3-bet with a continuation bet. QQ is a strong hand likely ahead of Minni's calling range, which probably contains AK, AQ, KK, KQ, or smaller pairs. The board texture doesn't hit most of these hands strongly. Betting 800 (roughly 50% pot) maintains aggression, builds the pot when ahead, and keeps initiative from my position while staying out of position.

I call to keep the pot manageable and use position. I have A♥6♠ on J♣ T♥ 5♦ — marginal but playable in position. Claude’s line (SB 3‑bet preflop then a ~½‑pot c‑bet) can include a lot of broadway hands, medium pairs, and some bluffs; he also has many hands that currently beat me, but I get ~25% pot odds to call (800 to win 3,200) and I block many A‑x value combos because I hold an ace. The flop is relatively dry and I have a backdoor heart plus the chance to pair up on the turn, so calling is the prudent play. I will fold to heavy, polarized turn aggression if I miss; if I pair the Ace (or hit a favorable blank and he checks) I’ll play more aggressively. I will not raise here — raising with A6 would be overcommitting and out of line with my hand.

With pocket QQ on a 5-5-J-T board, I'm continuing my aggressive line established pre-flop and on the flop. The second 5 doesn't significantly improve Minni's likely calling range (AK, AQ, KQ, smaller pairs like 88, 99), and I remain ahead of most hands. I've been dictating the action throughout this hand with strong bets, and the turn is an appropriate spot to maintain that pressure. A bet of 1,500 (roughly 47% of the current pot) keeps the pot manageable while extracting value from my strong hand and maintaining initiative. This sizing is consistent with my continuation bet sizing and puts Minni in a decision, likely to fold hands weaker than JJ or AJ.

Pot is 4,700 and I must call 1,500, which gives me roughly 24% pot odds. With A♥6♠ on J♣ T♥ 5♦ 5♥ I currently have only Ace-high and only six clean outs (three Aces + three Sixes) to make at least a pair on the river — about a 13% chance. Claude’s line (3‑bet preflop, firm flop bet, sizable turn bet) is strongly value‑oriented (Jx/Tx, 55, TT+, two‑pair/overpairs) and likely contains too few bluffs to justify calling. Raising would be committing a large portion of my stack as a pure bluff with very poor equity. I fold to preserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

I'm on the button with T7s in a heads-up pot against the blinds. This is a favorable situation:

1. **Position**: Button is the strongest position - I act last preflop and have maximum information
2. **Hand strength**: T7s is speculative but playable. It's suited (improves to ~15% equity vs random hands) and has good potential for straights and flushes
3. **Pot odds**: 150 in the pot, 100 to call = 2.5:1 odds, which easily justify calling with a hand that can flop draws and sometimes pairs
4. **Stack sizes**: All stacks are healthy (17K+), allowing for postflop flexibility
5. **Opponent tendencies**: Both Flash and Minni have been relatively aggressive, but having position gives me significant edge in marginal situations

With a decent stack in position, T7s is perfect for a call. I can win pots post-flop with intelligent play, and the hand has decent equity when I do flop something. Folding would be too tight; raising would be overvaluing a marginal hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 2s 4h, which is a very weak hand. The Button (Claude) has limped for 100. Even though it's only 50 more to call, this hand plays very poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand against two opponents. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here to avoid unnecessary losses with a trash hand.

Claude just limped (called 100). I’m in the big blind with K♠5♣ — a marginal offsuit king with a weak kicker and no flush/backdoor synergy. I’m out of position post‑flop against the button, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand by raising. Better to check and see a flop cheaply: I’ll proceed cautiously, calling small c‑bets for pot odds or continuing only if I hit a king/5 or get favorable draw texture; fold to large aggression on dry boards without improvement.

Flop: 5♦ A♣ 3♦ — I have K♠5♣, so I’ve paired the board but it’s a marginal low pair with an Ace on board and I’m out of position to the limper. Claude’s limp indicates a wide range (Ax, small pairs, suited connectors), so I’m often either ahead of small holdings or behind Ax/overpairs. I don’t want to inflate the pot with a vulnerable pair OOP. Checking controls the pot, lets me call reasonable c-bets for pot odds, and re-evaluate on the turn (raise only with a clear read or improvement). I’ll check and call small bets; fold to large, polarized aggression.

Minni (big blind) has checked to me on the flop showing weakness. I'm holding T7s (completely unimproved) on a 5d-Ac-3d board with no made hand currently. While I have 8 outs to make a straight (any 4 or 6), betting air here is risky. Even if Minni's check indicates weakness, betting with zero current value could lead to difficult situations if called. Since I'm in excellent position (button, acting last), I can afford to take a free card and see the turn. If I hit my straight or improve, I'll have a strong hand to work with. If the turn is blank, I can reassess. Checking preserves my equity with my gutshot draw while maintaining position for future streets. This is a balanced line that doesn't overcommit with a speculative hand.

I have K♠5♣ — a marginal pair of fives. Board on the turn is 5♦ A♣ 3♦ 6♦, so the turn completes three diamonds on board (any two‑diamond hand now has a flush) and an Ace is present. Claude limped preflop and checked the flop, indicating a wide range that can include Ax, small pairs, and diamond draws. I’m out of position and the pot is only 250, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a vulnerable pair that’s frequently behind. Checking controls the pot, keeps weaker holdings and bluffs in, and allows me to call a small turn bet for pot odds or fold to large, polarized aggression. Plan: check here; call small bets (~≤1/3 pot) to see a cheap river, fold to larger bets, and re-evaluate if checked to on the river.

I'm holding Ts7s on a flop of 5d-Ac-3d-6d with Minni checking to me on the turn. 

**Hand Analysis:**
- I have a gutshot straight draw (any 4 makes me a 3-4-5-6-7 straight = 4 outs)
- Additionally, I have 3 outs each for pair of 7s and pair of Ts (6 more outs)
- Total: approximately 10 outs to some improvement

**Board & Position Analysis:**
- Minni has now checked twice (flop and turn), indicating weakness
- I'm in position on the button
- Pot is 250, and stacks are healthy (10250 for Minni)
- Two free cards (flop check, turn check) suggest Minni has a marginal or missed hand

**Action Rationale:**
Checking again and taking a free river card would be passive here. Given:
1. Minni's demonstrated weakness with back-to-back checks
2. My position advantage
3. My 10+ outs providing equity
4. The opportunity to apply pressure and take down the pot immediately

A bet is the aggressive play. This is a semi-bluff that accomplishes multiple things:
- **Fold equity:** Forces Minni to make a decision; many weak hands will fold
- **Value:** If I'm called by a draw or marginal hand, I have significant equity
- **Positional advantage:** I'll act last on the river regardless

Betting 150 is a reasonable size—enough to create meaningful pressure without overcommitting, and establishes betting flow for the river if called.

I have K♠5♣ — a marginal pair of fives with a weak kicker on a wet board (A♣ 5♦ 3♦ 6♦). Claude limped preflop and checked the flop, so his range is wide (Ax, small pairs, one- or two-diamond draws). His small turn bet of 150 into a 400 pot is a blocking/half-value size that’s cheap to call and consistent with both made hands (Ax, small pairs, completed flushes) and bluffs. Raising OOP with this vulnerable pair is risky — I have little fold equity versus Ax/flushes and would bloat the pot when behind. Folding is too conservative because I still beat many holdings in his range and can realize pot equity. Therefore I’ll call the 150 to see a cheap river and re-evaluate; fold to large river aggression unless I improve or pick up a favorable read.

Board: 5♦ A♣ 3♦ 6♦ A♠. I hold K♠5♣ — only a single pair of fives with a weak kicker. Claude limped pre, checked the flop, then raised a small 150 on the turn (I called). That line is wide — it can include Ax (which now makes trips with the paired ace on the river), small pairs, and diamond draws (the turn already put three diamonds on board). The river pairing the ace drastically increases the portion of his range that beats me (any Ace = trips; flushes/strong made hands already beat me on the turn). With such a vulnerable, thin value hand OOP, bluffing has low fold equity and betting for value is very thin (I only get called by worse occasionally). Therefore I should control the pot and take the free showdown: check. Conditional plan: if Claude leads a small stab (≈≤1/3 pot ≈175–185), I can call; fold to larger river bets because they are strongly polarized and likely beat me.

RIVER ANALYSIS - Ts7s on 5d-Ac-3d-6d-As:

**Hand Strength:**
I have absolutely no made hand:
- No pair (T-7 unimproved against board of A-A-6-5-3)
- No straight (would need 4 or 8; neither came)
- No flush (Ts7s gives only one spade)
- This is complete air

**Opponent Assessment:**
Minni's line is revealing:
- Checked flop: weakness indicator
- CALLED my turn bet (150): This is critical. Minni didn't fold, suggesting they have something—likely a pair or draw that improved
- Checked river: Can indicate either a marginal hand checking it down, or weakness seeking free showdown

The key factor is that Minni CALLED my turn bet. This means they have a hand worth defending, not just air folding to pressure.

**Why Not Bet:**
- Zero equity with a total bluff
- Minni has demonstrated willingness to call by calling the turn
- The river Ace is a blank for me but potentially helps hands Minni might hold (any Ace in their range)
- Additional chips here are just losses; I cannot make Minni fold a hand that's already willing to call bets

**Why Check:**
- Minimize losses with a complete whiff
- Preserve chip stack for better opportunities
- Respect Minni's call; they showed strength
- River is a natural place for both players to check it down with marginal holdings

This is a simple decision: you don't double-barrel into a caller with zero equity. Take the showdown loss and move on.

I have Queen-Ten offsuit on the button in a three-handed game. This is a strong opening hand. Standard play is to raise to 3x the big blind (300) to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. My stack size is healthy at 122.5 big blinds, and I want to maintain an aggressive profile.

Holding 2♥6♦ in the small blind versus a button open to 300. This is one of the weakest hands — highly dominated by a typical raising range. Calling 250 more would put me out of position against the raiser (and the big blind who can still squeeze/call) for a marginal price; raising as a bluff risks facing a shove or a call from better hands. Preserve the 10,600 stack for better spots — fold.

Folding 2-6o (the worst starting hand) against a button raise in the big blind. Despite heads-up dynamics and reasonable pot odds, the hand quality is too poor. With ~30% equity against Flash's range and needing ~31% to break even on pot odds, this is an easy fold. Preserving chips for premium hands is more valuable in tournament play.

Button with T♠6♣ against only the blinds — marginal hand but excellent position and good opportunity to steal. Pot is 150 and calling 100 is passive; I want to apply pressure and define ranges. A 3x open to 300 gives reasonable fold equity versus the SB/BB, reduces the frequency of light 3-bets, and keeps the pot manageable relative to my deep stack (10,600). Plan: if I get called, play post‑flop aggressively with continuation bets on favorable boards; fold to a sizable 3‑bet since T6o has poor playability out of position. Raise to 300 to take the initiative.

K5o from the small blind is a weak, easily dominated hand. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (250 to call into ~450 pot), I face significant disadvantages: I'm out of position post-flop and will have to act first on every subsequent street against Minni's aggressive button raise. K5o lacks both strong equity and playability in a 3-way pot. As the chip leader with 16950, I can afford to be selective and wait for stronger holdings. Folding here is the disciplined, profitable long-term approach.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Ten offsuit. The button (Minni) has raised to 300. This is a standard opening range for a button. King-Ten is a very strong hand to defend my big blind with against a button raise. I am getting good pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 450). While I could 3-bet to try and take the pot down now, calling allows me to see a flop and realize my hand's equity against what is likely a wide button opening range. I will call and see how the flop develops.

I am out of position on a paired board (Jc-Js-7h) with King-high. While King-Ten has some back-door potential and decent high-card value on this board, leading out into the pre-flop aggressor is generally not ideal. I will check to see how Minni proceeds, as this board hits both ranges but checking allows me to control the pot size and potentially catch a bluff or see a cheap turn card.

I opened preflop and the BB (Flash) just checked to me on a paired, relatively dry J♣ J♠ 7♥ flop. My T♠6♣ has no showdown value, but as the preflop aggressor I have a range advantage and can credibly represent a jack or a strong pair. A half‑pot c-bet (~325 into 650) applies pressure to missed overcards and small pairs, preserves fold equity, and keeps the pot manageable if I get called. If I face a check‑raise I’ll fold — T6o has poor equity vs that line. If called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously unless I improve. Betting now maximizes immediate fold equity and keeps initiative.

I have King-high on a paired board of J-J-7. While King-Ten has some theoretical equity against a wide button opening range, I am out of position and have completely missed this flop. Minni is an aggressive player who frequently continuation-bets and often follows up on later streets. Calling here without even a backdoor flush draw would put me in a difficult position on the turn if I don't improve. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Raising with Ah8h from the button is a standard play. I have good position, a reasonable hand (ace with 8-kicker, suited), and the opportunity to apply pressure to the blinds. A 3x BB raise to 300 is appropriate sizing - not too aggressive but establishes my aggression and maintains fold equity. This hand has good potential both pre-flop and post-flop with flush draw possibilities.

I have been dealt the best possible starting hand, Pocket Aces. Claude has opened from the button, a position where players typically open a very wide range of hands. My goal here is to maximize value and build a large pot immediately. While calling can be used as a trap, 3-betting is the standard and most profitable play to extract value from Claude's range and protect my hand from the big blind. I will raise to 1000, which is a standard 3-bet sizing from the small blind against a 3x open.

Claude opened to 300 on the button and Flash (SB) 3‑bet to 1000. I’m in the big blind with Q♦4♠. Pot is 1400, to call 900 (min‑raise to 1700). Q4o is a weak, often‑dominated hand with very poor post‑flop playability — especially out of position and likely in a multiway pot if Claude calls. Calling 900 here requires ~39% equity to break even preflop; Q4o has far less against typical button + 3‑bet ranges. A 4‑bet is also a bad option (no fold equity and I’d be committing with garbage). Preserve chips for better spots and fold.

Calling Flash's re-raise with Ah8h. Key factors: (1) I have excellent position on the button with an aggressive opponent in the SB - this is where I can extract maximum value post-flop; (2) I'm getting 3:1 odds (2100 to call 700), and Ah8h has solid equity (~40%+) against Flash's wide re-raising range shown throughout this tournament; (3) Flash has demonstrated extremely aggressive play (raising with 2c-6h, 7h-Qs, 5c-Th, etc), so their re-raise range is very wide and vulnerable to position-based exploitation; (4) My hand has good playability with flush draw and broadway card potential; (5) At 16650 chips, I can afford to see a flop and leverage position advantage - re-raising risks getting all-in with a marginal hand, while calling lets me control the pot and win through post-flop play where my position is decisive. The flop will define the hand's direction, and my button position allows me to react optimally to Flash's action.

On a flop of 10c-9h-5s, I have an overpair with Pocket Aces. This board is relatively coordinated, meaning it could have connected with Claude's 3-bet calling range (hands like JTs, T9s, or 89s). However, my hand is still significantly ahead of most of his range. I want to bet for value and to protect my equity against straight draws. A bet of around 40% of the pot (850 into 2100) is a standard continuation bet that keeps his range wide while extracting value from tens, nines, and draws.

Calling Flash's 850 flop bet with Ah8h. I have excellent position on the button and a strong semi-bluff with 7 flush outs plus overcard equity against Flash's wide, aggressive range. The 3.5:1 pot odds are favorable for a flush draw with additional equity. Calling keeps the pot controllable while allowing me to leverage position advantage on the turn - any heart, broadway card, or straight card (7, K, J) will significantly improve my hand and give me aggressive options. Flash's aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament suggest they may be semi-bluffing or overplaying mid-strength hands, making position-based exploitation more valuable than committing extra chips now with a marginal holding.

The Jack of clubs on the turn is a dynamic card that connects the board significantly (10-9-5-J). It completes potential straights for hands like KQ and makes two-pair combinations like JT possible. Given that I am out of position against the chip leader, Claude, and the board has become much more coordinated, I will switch to a pot-control line. Checking allows me to see how Claude responds to the scare card. If he checks back, I can confidently value bet most rivers. If he bets, I have a relatively easy call to evaluate the river, while avoiding getting into a massive pot where I might be drawing thin against a straight or a set.

Flash's turn check after aggressive flop betting suggests weakness or caution - the neutral Jack didn't improve most hands significantly. I have 10+ outs with Ah8h (7 flush outs + 3 straight outs via 7s, plus Ace overcard), positioning this as a strong semi-bluff. Betting 1500 (40% pot) applies real pressure while maintaining upside if called - my draw has two cards to complete with excellent equity. Button position and Flash's check give me strong fold equity here. This is optimal extraction of my positional advantage against Flash's wide, aggressive range.

On this turn, the Jack of clubs is indeed a card that hits many parts of Claude's calling range (JT, QJ, AJ, and even the straight with KQ). However, I cannot fold Pocket Aces to a single sub-half-pot bet. My check was intended to control the pot size and induce bluffs or thinner value bets from hands like Tx, 9x, or semi-bluffs. By calling, I keep his range wide and give myself the opportunity to see a river card. If the board doesn't get significantly worse on the river, I can likely look to check-call again or even value-bet if the river is a blank. I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a King) and two outs for a set as well, providing some backup equity.

The river card is a Queen, making the final board 10c-9h-5s-Jc-Qh. This is a very dangerous board for pocket Aces. There is now a four-card straight on the board (9-10-J-Q), meaning any King or any Eight makes a straight. Given the action, Claude's range (calling a 3-bet pre-flop and a continuation bet on the flop) contains many hands that have now improved to a straight, two pair (like QJ, JT, or QT), or sets. My Aces are now purely a bluff-catcher. Betting would be a mistake because I will likely only be called by hands that beat me and fold out the few bluffs he might have. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or evaluate his action if he bets.

I have a Queen-high straight (Q-J-T-9-8), the strongest possible hand available given the board composition. Flash's river check after aggressive flop/turn betting indicates weakness—likely a marginal made hand like top pair or a missed draw. No better straights are possible (K not on board), no flush is possible (only 2 hearts on board), and trips/full houses are extremely unlikely given Flash's betting pattern. A 2500 chip value bet represents approximately 37% of the pot, which is optimal sizing to extract value from weaker made hands while avoiding overcommitment. If Flash has a pair, they'll likely call this bet; if they have air, I win the pot. This is straightforward value extraction with a premium hand in position.

The board runout of 10-9-5-J-Q is extremely dangerous for pocket Aces. The presence of a four-card straight (9-10-J-Q) means any King or any Eight in Claude's range has now made a straight. Given the action—Claude calling a 3-bet pre-flop, calling a continuation bet on the flop, and then betting the turn when I checked—his range is heavily saturated with Broadway cards and suited connectors. Many of these hands (like KQ, KJ, KT, J8, T8) have made straights, while others (like QJ, QT, JT, J9) have made two pair. Even though I am being offered excellent pot odds (calling 2500 to win a total pot of 11,800), the board is simply too connected. There are very few hands Claude would bet on this river that I can actually beat; even a hand like AQ is now a marginal value bet for him. I will make a disciplined fold to preserve my stack.

I am on the button with 2d 4s, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, the hand has almost no value and very poor playability. Raising as a steal attempt is possible, but against these opponents, it's safer to wait for a better hand to preserve my stack for more profitable situations later in the tournament. I will fold.

Heads-up with a deep stack (~108bb) — I can open very wide from the small blind. T7o is a reasonable heads-up opener (broadway/connected card with decent playability) and in heads-up I act last postflop, so I want initiative. A standard min-open to 200 (2bb total) applies pressure, gains fold equity, and lets me continue with a wide c-bet on many flops. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and play cautiously on obvious disasters. Raise to 200.

Calling from the big blind with K-3o. Getting 3:1 pot odds (300:100) which is sufficient to defend. I have position post-flop and Minni's small blind raise is a wide range. My King has showdown value, and I can evaluate the flop before committing further chips. This is a standard big blind defense against an aggressive small blind raise.

I opened wide to 200 preflop and Claude just called a very wide range (pairs, broadways, suited connectors). The flop 8♦-5♠-2♣ is very dry and misses most of his calling range, so as preflop aggressor I have a lot of fold equity. I want to c-bet frequently but keep the pot controllable if called — a small/medium c-bet ~1/3 pot is best. Betting 150 into the 400 pot pressures his overcards and unpaired holdings while limiting losses if he calls. Plan: take the pot now if he folds; if he calls I will re-evaluate the turn and generally check back bricks or proceed only when I pick up real equity or a favorable card. If he raises I will fold T7o — it has poor equity vs a raising range on this board.

K3o is a weak starting hand that doesn't improve significantly on this flop (8d-5s-2c). I'm facing a bet from Minni, who has shown consistent aggression throughout the tournament. While I'm getting 3.67:1 pot odds (550:150), my hand equity against her likely range is too low. She could have overcards (AK, AQ), paired the board, or be betting with broadway cards. K-high without any kicker strength or draw potential is difficult to play out of position in a multi-way pot. The best play is to fold and preserve my chip stack for premium situations. My 20,200 stack is healthy enough that I don't need to marginally defend this hand.

Button with 7c6c — a playable, suited connector with great postflop potential and position on both blinds. Pot is 150 and I only have to call 100; rather than limp, I want to take the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds while building a pot I can play in position. I’ll open to a standard 3xBB (300 total). That size gives decent fold equity vs the blinds, keeps weaker hands from seeing cheap flops, and isn’t so large that I’m committed if I face a big 3-bet — I’ll proceed cautiously or fold to strong 3-bets and play aggressively postflop if called.

Ts Ad is a premium hand (roughly 10th best starting hand). From the small blind against a button raise, this is an excellent spot to 3-bet. Minni has been raising frequently from the button with a wide range throughout this session, indicating exploitable aggression. A raise to 900 (approximately 3x the original raise) accomplishes several things: (1) it puts pressure on both Minni and Flash to fold or commit more chips, (2) it narrows their ranges considerably, (3) if called, I'm in a strong position postflop with an ace-high hand that plays well both in position and out of position. With a healthy stack of 20,150 and premium cards, aggression is warranted here. The math favors re-raising since I'm well ahead of Minni's button-raising range and can win the pot immediately or get value from weaker hands.

I am in the Big Blind with 5d 2c, which is a very weak hand. The action has been a raise from Minni (Button) to 300 and a 3-bet from Claude (Small Blind) to 900. Given my position, the strength of my hand, and the significant action ahead of me, there is no reason to continue. I will fold.

Button with 7♣6♣ facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 900 after my 300 open. Pot is 1300 and I need to call 600 more (900 total). With ~107BB effective, 7♣6♣ is a strong hand to play in position: good equity, great flop playability and large implied odds. The 3‑bet size is standard/wide enough that calling is cheap (600 into a 1300 pot ≈ 32% immediate odds) and lets me use position to realize equity or apply pressure post‑flop. A 4‑bet here would be a thin bluff and needlessly inflates the pot out of position on future streets; folding is too tight. Plan: call, see a flop, and proceed based on texture (continue with draws/top pairs, fold to heavy action on dry boards or strong 4‑bets).

Betting with Ts Ad on 7h 2d 2h flop. This is a standard continuation bet in a heads-up pot where I was the preflop aggressor. My ace-high is strong on this dry board, and I need to define the hand rather than check and give free cards. A 2/3 pot bet of 1,200 applies appropriate pressure while maintaining good bet sizing discipline. This bet wins many pots immediately against hands like AJ, KQ, and weaker pairs, while getting value from Minni's continued aggression if she has a premium hand. The bet also keeps my line consistent with my strong preflop 3-bet.

Flop: 7♥ 2♦ 2♥ — I have 7♣6♣: top pair with a weak kicker. Claude 3‑bet preflop, so his range is value‑heavy (JJ+/AK/AQ) but will also contain some bluffs/suited broadways. His flop sizing (1200 into 3100 ≈ 28% pot) looks like a standard probe/c-bet rather than a polarized, huge value shove. In position with a hand that plays well versus his wide 3‑betting range, I want to keep his bluffs and worse hands (AK, AQ, KQ, some medium pairs) in the pot and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand by raising. Calling is cheap and preserves flexibility: I can control pot size, extract value from worse, and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call the 1200, fold to significant raise/jam, call smaller turn barrels and extract value or check back as appropriate.

With Ts Ad on a 7h 2d 2h 6d turn board, I have ace-high with a gutshot to the nut straight (4 outs to any 9). The turn card (6d) didn't significantly improve or hurt my hand. I've already taken an aggressive line preflop with a 3-bet and continued on the flop with a 1200 bet. At this point, I'm out of position and holding a marginal hand. Checking here makes strategic sense because: (1) I'm out of position and my hand strength is uncertain - it could be best, but it could also be vulnerable; (2) a check allows me to see Minni's action and gives me information about her hand strength; (3) if she checks, I get to the river cheaply with my draw; (4) if she bets, I can make a value decision on the river with more information; (5) with a gutshot (marginal equity), continued aggression out of position is less profitable than controlling pot size. This gives me the flexibility to see the river and evaluate based on community response.

I have two pair (7s and 6s) on 7♥ 2♦ 2♥ 6♦ after calling Claude’s flop probe. Claude 3‑bet preflop and barreled the flop, then checked the turn — that line is ambiguous (could be pot‑control with overcards or a slowplay with a deuce). Two pair is strong here but vulnerable to any hand containing a deuce, so my objective is to extract value from worse hands (single 7x, Kx/Ax hands that will call) and to charge any turn/river draws while keeping the pot manageable relative to my stack. Betting gains fold equity and simplifies future decisions; checking lets him realize equity or pot‑control with a wide range. I’ll bet about half the pot (2150 into 4300) — big enough to get value and deny free cards, but small enough that I can continue against reasonable aggression. If Claude check‑raises very large (polarized/commitment), I’ll strongly consider folding to a shove since that line is consistent with trips/full house; if he just calls, I’ll re‑evaluate and extract further on the river when appropriate.

With Ts Ad on a 7h 2d 2h 6d board, I'm holding ace-high with a gutshot to the nut straight (4 outs). Minni's turn raise to 2,150 after I checked needs careful evaluation. Looking at the pot odds: I'm risking 2,150 to win 6,450 + Minni's raise, giving me roughly 3:1 odds. I only need 25% equity to break even mathematically. My actual equity is likely 25-35% against Minni's range here - she could have a weak pair, a semi-bluff with broadway cards, or even air given her aggressive button-raising profile throughout this session. The turn card (6d) didn't dramatically change the dynamics. Calling achieves several objectives: (1) It keeps me in the hand to see the river card which could be a crucial 9 for the nut straight; (2) I get to showdown with a reasonable hand; (3) Against an aggressive opponent like Minni, folding ace-high would be too passive; (4) My stack (18,100) easily accommodates this call. The river decision will be clearer with complete information. This is a profitable call given the odds and my opponent's tendencies.

Checking the river with ace-high on a paired board. I'm out of position with a marginal hand after an aggressive turn from Minni. The 9d river card likely improved her range more than mine - she may have connected with broadway cards or made trips/a set. Checking achieves: (1) Controls pot size with my weak holding; (2) Allows me to face her river action and fold if she shows strength; (3) Gets to showdown cheaply if she checks behind; (4) Avoids speculative betting that would bloat the pot with poor equity. This is the standard defensive play against an aggressive opponent when holding marginal strength out of position on the river.

I hold 7♣6♣ on 7♥ 2♦ 2♥ 6♦ 9♦ — two pair (7s & 6s). Claude 3‑bet preflop and then bet the flop and called my turn bet; that line is consistent with a lot of overpairs/broadway and medium pairs that missed this board, though a small portion of his range (hands with a 2, pocket 6s/7s, or two diamonds) now beat me. Overall the majority of his 3‑betting/calling range is still behind my two pair. I want to extract value from one‑pair hands and missed broadways while charging any turn diamonds or hands that picked up pairing outs. A half‑pot river value bet (~4,300 into 8,600) is the right sizing — large enough to get calls from Kx/Ax and medium pairs, but not so large that I’m auto‑committed vs a polarized check‑raise. If Claude check‑raises shove (or very large), I’ll treat that as a polarized line (trips/full house/flush) and fold rather than committing my stack. Therefore I’ll bet for value now.

Calling on the river with Ts Ad (ace-high) against Minni's aggressive raise. I'm getting 4:1 pot odds, needing only 20% equity to break even. Against Minni's mixed range (which includes frequent semi-bluffs and aggressive plays throughout this session), I estimate roughly 20-25% equity, making this call mathematically justified. Her aggressive pre-flop, post-flop, and river betting pattern, combined with her limited remaining stack (2,500), suggests a mixed range rather than only premium holdings. Folding ace-high would be overly passive against her demonstrated tendencies. The pot size (17,200 total) is manageable relative to my stack (15,950), and calling allows me to reach showdown and evaluate her actual holding.

3-7 off suit is statistically one of the worst hands in poker. While I'm in good position on the button and the call is only 100 chips, this hand has minimal equity against most holdings and poor playability post-flop. Even though position is valuable, 3-7o lacks the potential to justify entering the pot. I should preserve my stack (11,650) and wait for stronger opportunities. With 86 hands remaining in the tournament, fold equity and proper hand selection are critical to maintaining stack health.

I am in the Small Blind with King of Spades and Jack of Clubs, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Claude has already folded from the button. I am facing a 50 chip call to complete the 100 big blind. Given my stack size and the strength of KJo, I will raise to take the initiative and potentially win the blinds immediately or play a pot in position if Minni calls.

I’m in the big blind with Q♦T♦ facing a 300 SB open. It costs me 200 to call into a 400 pot (~33% pot odds). QTs is a well-priced defend here — suited, connected, lots of post‑flop playability and good equity versus a wide SB opening range. Flash has shown frequent preflop aggression but not an ultra‑tight calling range, so calling preserves equity and keeps the pot manageable out of position against his likely wide range. A min‑raise would bloat the pot and can get me 3‑bet or committed OOP with a hand that’s better played post‑flop. Plan: call, see the flop; continue for value if I pair or have strong draws, otherwise control the pot and fold to heavy pressure on dry boards.

I have middle pair (Kings) with a Jack kicker on a very coordinated Ace-high board. I also hold a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (a Ten gives me the straight). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will lead with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot. This allows me to represent the Ace and gather information about Minni's hand. If I am called, I still have significant equity with my straight draw and showdown value with my pair.

I have Q♦T♦ on K♣ A♦ Q♥ — middle pair with a decent ten kicker and some backdoor equity (gutshot to Broadway if a J arrives, and backdoor diamonds). Flash opened from SB and is c-betting a small 300 into a 900 pot (≈25% pot odds to call). That sizing is consistent with a wide continuation range, so my pair has good showdown value versus many of his hands. Raising OOP with QTs would bloat the pot and can be punished by A/K or a 3‑bet; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call 300, keep worse hands/bluffs in the pot, and re-evaluate on the turn — continue for value if I improve or if he shows weakness, and fold to sustained, large aggression on obvious A/K boards.

The turn card (Jack of Spades) has given me two pair (Kings and Jacks), but the board is now extremely dangerous with four cards to a straight (A-K-Q-J). Any Ten in Minni's hand now completes a Broadway straight. While I have a strong hand in a vacuum, the board texture is very 'wet,' and I am vulnerable to any Ten. Betting here likely only gets called by straights or hands with significant equity, and I don't want to build a large pot when I could be drawing dead. I will check to see how Minni reacts and control the pot size. If she checks behind, I can evaluate the river; if she bets, I will have to consider her sizing and the likelihood of her holding a Ten.

I have Q♦T♦ on K♣ A♦ Q♥ J♠ — a pair of queens (vulnerable to A/K) but real showdown value versus Flash’s wide SB c-bet range. He bet small on the flop (wide range) then checked the turn — a sign of weakness or pot control with a top pair. I want to extract value from worse Qx/Jx and deny a free river while keeping the pot manageable OOP, so a small value bet (~1/3 pot) is ideal. I will size to 400: enough to get called by worse hands and fold pure air, but small enough that I can fold to a sizable check‑raise. If he flats I’ll re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises big I’ll let this marginal hand go.

I have improved to Two Pair (Kings and Jacks), but the board is extremely dangerous with four cards to a straight (A-K-Q-J). Any Ten in Minni's hand now holds a Broadway straight. However, Minni's bet of 400 into a 1200 pot (offering 4:1 pot odds) is relatively small. Given the strength of my hand in a vacuum and the favorable pot odds, folding would be too tight. I may still be ahead of other two-pair combinations or semi-bluffs, and I have small equity to improve to a full house on the river. I will call and re-evaluate on the river.

On a board of Kc-Ad-Qh-Js-Kd, I have improved to a full house, Kings full of Jacks (KKKJJ). However, this board is extremely dangerous. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my continuation bet on an A-K-Q flop, which strongly suggests she holds an Ace or a Queen. If she holds an Ace, she has Kings full of Aces (KKKAA); if she holds a Queen, she has Kings full of Queens (KKKQQ). Both of these hands beat my full house. While I beat a straight (any player holding a Ten), the likelihood that Minni called the flop with an Ace or Queen is high. I will check to control the pot size and hopefully reach a cheap showdown, as betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands and puts me in a difficult spot if raised.

I actually made the Broadway straight on the turn (T‑J‑Q‑K‑A using my Q♦T♦ and the J♠ on turn). The river K♦, however, pairs the board and massively increases the number of hands that now beat me (any K in Flash’s hand becomes trips or can make a full house with an Ace/Queen; pocket Aces/QQ also become full houses/quads). Flash’s line (SB open, small c‑bet on the flop, check on the turn, call my small 400 turn bet, then check river) is ambiguous — could be a medium A‑hand pot‑controlling, a trap K, a turned straight (T‑x), or just a showdown‑value Qx. If I bet now I’ll fold out many worse hands but also get called (or check‑raised) by the hands that actually beat me, so a value bet is thin and can be negative EV. Checking behind preserves my equity, avoids getting inflated into a losing pot vs trips/full houses, and lets me realize my equity against weaker Qx and missed hands. Plan: check to showdown; call a small river bet but be prepared to fold to a large shove/check‑raise that strongly represents a full house/quads.

I am on the button with Queen-Seven offsuit. While I have positional advantage, this hand is quite weak to open-raise with, especially considering both opponents (Minni and Claude) have shown significant aggression in previous hands. Q7o does not play well post-flop, and trying to steal the blinds against active players at this stage of the tournament is unnecessarily risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up from the small blind with Qc4d. Pot is 150 (I’ve posted 50, BB 100) and it’s my decision to act for 50 more. In heads‑up play I should open a wide stealing range — Q4o is marginal but acceptable to raise for fold equity, especially with deep stacks and the positional advantage postflop (I act last after the flop). A min‑raise to 200 (200 total committed; +150 more than my posted 50) applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable, and lets me play postflop with initiative if called. If Claude 3-bets large I can fold and preserve stack; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet appropriate textures and proceed cautiously when I miss. Therefore I’ll raise to the minimum to steal or realize equity in position.

K-10 is a decent hand for heads-up play in the big blind after Minni's small blind raise to 200. Throughout the tournament, Minni has shown an aggressive playing style with frequent raises and significant chip accumulation. In this heads-up situation, I should defend my big blind with a hand that plays well. Rather than just calling 100 more, raising to 600 (approximately 3x Minni's raise) applies real pressure and has multiple benefits: (1) It generates fold equity to win the pot immediately, (2) It maintains the initiative, (3) It's sized appropriately relative to both stacks - using only ~5% of my 11,550 chips while being meaningful relative to the current pot, and (4) K-10 is a hand that plays well both in a fold situation and if called. This aggressive line fits the tournament dynamics and my objective to build my stack while managing risk.

I opened light to 200 as a steal, but Claude responded with a sizable 3‑bet to 600. Calling 400 into the 800 pot would require ~25% equity to break even (pot ~1600 after my call). Qc4d offsuit is a weak, poorly‑dominating hand versus a typical 3‑bet range (pairs, A/K broadways, etc.) and has very limited playability in a deep‑stacked 3‑bet pot. A 4‑bet would have to be highly polarized and is not +EV here. Preserving my 18,500 stack and folding to this 3‑bet is the lowest‑risk, highest‑EV play — I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

Three-handed on the button with 9♦5♥. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call; with deep stacks (~185bb) and position I should open a wide range to steal the blinds and take the initiative. 9-5o is marginal but playable in this short-handed spot — I’ll size up to a standard 3x BB open to 300 total to apply pressure and gain fold equity. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; if called I’ll play postflop aggressively in position (c-bet good textures, control pot on dry boards).

Folding K5o from Small Blind facing a Button raise. Weak unpaired hand with poor kicker, out of position post-flop, no edge against Minni's range. Stack is healthy enough to fold marginal hands and wait for better spots. Clean fold.

In Hand #66, I am in the Big Blind holding Qc 8d. Minni, who has been consistently aggressive and holds a large chip lead, raised to 300 from the Button. Claude folded in the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me pot odds of 2.25:1. Given that Minni opens a wide range from the Button and my hand has reasonable equity against such a range, defending the Big Blind is a standard and profitable play. I will call to see a flop.

On the flop of 2d-9s-Ts, I have a gutshot straight draw to the nut straight (needing a Jack) and one overcard with my Queen. As the Big Blind defending against a Button raise, it is standard to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction and potentially see a free card or evaluate their bet size. I'm checking with the intention of seeing how Minni proceeds.

I have top pair (9♦) with a weak 5 kicker on 2♦ 9♠ T♠. Against a BB calling my button open, his range is wide — tens, weaker nines, spade draws and broadways — so I want to take the initiative, get value from worse pairs and charge spade draws. A half‑pot c-bet (~50%) accomplishes that without bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. Plan: bet to extract/protect; if called, re-evaluate on the turn (continue on blanks, exercise caution on spades/overcards); fold to a large check‑raise unless reads justify calling.

On the flop of 2d-9s-Ts, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) and one overcard (the Queen). Minni, the button aggressor, has bet 325 into a pot of 650. This is a half-pot continuation bet, which is standard for her. To call, I need 325 to win a total pot of 1300, requiring 25% equity. A gutshot provides about an 8.5% chance to hit on the turn and a 16.5% chance by the river. Even if I include my Queen as three additional outs, my total equity is roughly 28% for the whole runout, but only about 14% for the turn. Being out of position against an aggressive chip leader with a weak draw and no backdoor flush potential makes this a difficult call. While the implied odds of hitting a Jack are present, the overall weakness of my hand and the likelihood of facing another bet on the turn lead me to fold and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm on the button with 4c 8c, facing 100 to call. While position is favorable and I'm getting decent pot odds (2.5:1), 4-8 offsuit is simply too weak to play profitably in this 3-handed scenario against two active opponents (Minni with 18,750 chips and Flash with 9,300 chips). Both have been aggressive throughout this session. The hand lacks playability post-flop if called, and the risk/reward doesn't justify committing chips with such a marginal holding. I'll preserve my 11,800 chip stack and wait for a stronger opportunity to apply pressure from the button.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Deuce offsuit. It is a three-handed game. Claude has already folded from the button. I have 9,300 chips and the big blind, Minni, has 18,750.
