Ace-high is a strong starting hand in shorthanded play, especially blind-vs-blind. Since I am out of position for the rest of the hand, I prefer to take the initiative or win the pot immediately rather than just calling. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on Minni.

Facing a 3x SB open to 300 from the small blind, I only need to add 200 to call into a 400 pot (pot will be 600 after my call). That gives me ~3:1 pot odds (~25% equity required). 2♥6♥ is a weak hand but it's suited and has backdoor flush/straight potential; versus a likely wide SB stealing range my hand has reasonable equity and I also gain the postflop positional advantage as the big blind. A shove/raise to the minimum (500) is too much to risk with this holding — I have little fold equity and would be inflated into a bloated pot out of position preflop. Folding is too tight given the cheap price to see a flop. Plan: call to 300, see the flop, proceed cautiously (fold to heavy aggression unless I pair or pick up good draw equity).

I have bottom pair (Deuces) with the best possible kicker (Ace) on a low, connected board (5-4-2). Since I was the pre-flop aggressor, I will follow through with a continuation bet. This bet aims to take down the pot immediately, protect my vulnerable pair from overcards, and extract value from worse hands or straight draws that Minni might be defending with in the Big Blind.

Call 300. I flopped a weak but real hand — a pair of deuces (2h with 2c on board) plus some improvement potential (three 6s, four 3s and two remaining 2s = ~9 outs, plus remote backdoor heart chances). Flash's 300 into a 900 pot is a small c-bet and consistent with a wide SB continuation range, so folding would be too nitty given the price. Raising is unattractive with a vulnerable bottom pair and deep stacks (SPR ≈7) — I have little fold equity and would be out of position in a bloated pot. Plan: call to see the turn, realize equity and keep the pot controlled; if I miss and face heavy turn aggression I will fold, if I improve I'll extract value.

On the turn, the board pairs with a second Five. I currently have two pair (Fives and Deuces) with an Ace kicker. While my hand has improved, the board is quite connected, and a Five is well within Minni's defending range from the Big Blind. By checking, I control the pot size and can evaluate Minni's action. This also allows her to potentially bluff with straight draws like 3x or 6x, which I can then decide to call to get to a showdown.

Turn analysis: I now have two pair (fives and deuces) with 6h2h, plus a four-card heart draw (4h on board + my two hearts). Flash’s line (SB open pre, small flop c-bet then check the turn) looks more like a wide/weak/float range or a draw rather than a slow-played boat/trips. I’m in position and want to both extract value from worse pairs/floats and charge the heart/3-straight draws while denying a free river card. Plan: value/protection bet about half-pot. If Flash check-raises large I’ll give credit to trips/full-house and fold; I’ll be willing to call a small raise to see the river and re-evaluate.

On the turn, the board has paired Fives, which is a card that could reasonably be in Minni's Big Blind defending range. However, my Deuces have now become part of a two-pair (Fives and Deuces) with the best possible kicker (Ace). Minni's bet of 600 into a 1200 pot is a standard sizing that could represent trips, a straight draw, or even a pure bluff after I checked. Given the pot odds of 3:1 and the fact that I have a decent hand in a shorthanded game, folding is too weak. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me. Calling allows me to control the size of the pot and see a river card while keeping her bluffs in her range.

On the river, the board has completed with a King. I have Two Pair (Fives and Deuces) with an Ace kicker. While my hand is decent, the King is an overcard that might have improved Minni's range if she was defending the Big Blind with any Kx hands. Since I checked the turn and only called her bet, leading now would likely only be called by hands that beat me (trips, straights, or a King). By checking, I can potentially reach a cheap showdown or induce a bluff from missed straight draws like 6-7 or 6-3. I have enough showdown value to check-call a reasonable bet, but checking is the best initial move to control the pot.

I hold two pair (Fives and Deuces) on 5s‑4h‑2c‑5h‑Kd. Flash’s line (preflop open, flop c‑bet, check‑call on my turn bet, then check river) looks like a lot of floats/missed draws and medium pairs rather than a locked trips/monster. Very few hands beat me — a 5 (trips/quads) or a King (giving K+5 two‑pair) — and a river check makes trips slightly less likely. Checking behind surrenders thin value. I’ll bet ~1/2 pot (1200) to extract value from worse pairs (4x, single deuces, turned pairs and missed draws that will call small) and to deny a free showdown. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll give credit to K/5 combos and fold; I’ll tolerate a small raise but won’t bloat the pot unnecessarily.

On a board of 5s-4h-2c-5h-Kd, I have two pair (Fives and Deuces) with an Ace kicker. Minni has bet 1200 into a 2400 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds (I need to be right 25% of the time). While the King on the river is a scare card, Minni's line could represent a variety of hands, including missed straight draws like 67 or 63. Given the shorthanded nature of the game and the fact that I block some of her strongest value hands (A5, A3) with my Ace, my hand is a reasonable bluff catcher. Folding two pair here would be overly exploitable, so I will call to see the showdown.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack suited (Ac Jc), a premium starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds. A standard raise is the correct play here to build value and take initiative. Throughout the tournament, the typical opening raise has been 300, so I will stick with that sizing to put pressure on the blinds and potentially win the pot pre-flop or play a heads-up pot in position.

I’m in the small blind with 8♠3♦ — a very weak, unsuited, unconnected hand with no blockers. Flash has raised to 300 from the button and Claude is still to act in the big blind; calling 250 would be a multiway, out-of-position spot with very poor post‑flop playability and low equity. A light 3‑bet bluff here is undesirable — I have no clean blockers and both opponents have shown willingness to defend/call. Better to conserve chips for a clearer spot later. Fold.

This is a straightforward fold. I hold 4-5 offsuit, one of the weakest possible hands in poker. While I'm getting reasonable pot odds (2.25:1, needing ~31% equity), several factors make folding correct: (1) I'm in the worst position post-flop (Big Blind, out of position), (2) 4-5o has minimal equity against Flash's opening range and will be difficult to play out of position, (3) we're at a relatively early stage of the tournament (hand 68/150) with healthy stacks, so I can afford to wait for premium spots, (4) Flash's raise from button is standard aggression, and I shouldn't get stubborn defending marginal hands. Preserving chips for better opportunities when I have better cards is the correct long-term strategy.

Button with A♣4♥ — marginal but playable Ace and position is a big advantage in 3-handed play. I want to seize the initiative and apply pressure to the blinds rather than limp and play a multiway pot with a dominated Ace. A standard 3xBB open (raise to 300 total) gives good fold equity, is small relative to my deep 164bb stack, and keeps postflop options open. Plan: if everyone folds I pick up the blinds; if called I play postflop in position and c-bet appropriate textures; if faced with a sizable 3-bet I’ll fold A4o rather than get committed out of position.

I'm holding A7 of diamonds in the small blind. Minni has opened from the button to 300, and I'm facing a call of 250 to win a pot of 450, giving me 1.8:1 odds. A7 suited is a playable hand in this spot - it has good connectivity and is strong enough to contest against a button raise. While Flash still needs to act from the big blind, calling allows me to see the flop in position with a hand that plays well post-flop. A7s can make strong hands (nut flush draws, top pair, etc.) and has reasonable showdown value. Given the pot odds and hand strength, calling is more appropriate than folding a decent hand or 3-betting too wide.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. Minni opened from the button and Claude called from the Small Blind. While I am getting attractive pot odds (200 to call into a 700 pot), this hand performs poorly multi-way and is difficult to play out of position against two active opponents. I will fold to preserve my stack for more favorable opportunities.

Checking the flop with Ad7d on 6s5dJc. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) plus a backdoor flush draw, giving me ~40% equity. Without a made hand, checking keeps the pot manageable and allows me to realize my draw equity efficiently. This also keeps Minni in the hand and lets me see if she bets, giving me more information to make decisions on the turn.

I opened preflop and have position; the flop (6♠-5♦-J♣) is relatively dry with only weak backdoor possibilities and a single overcard. Claude checked, which often indicates weakness or a marginal call preflop — many hands in his calling range (Kx, Qx, small broadways, off-suit Ax) will fold to pressure. I hold A♣4♥ which blocks some Ax combos and gives me decent fold equity. I will continuation-bet about half the pot (350) to win the pot now or charge worse hands; if Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn, and if he produces a large check-raise I’ll give it up with A4o rather than get committed out of position.

I'm holding Ad7d on a 6s5dJc flop with an open-ended straight draw (8 outs) plus a backdoor flush draw (approximately 6 additional outs accounting for overlap). Against Minni's bet of 350 into a pot of 1050, I'm getting approximately 3:1 immediate pot odds (1050:350). With roughly 14 total outs, I have approximately 28-30% equity, which justifies the call mathematically. My hand has significant upside potential - I can make a straight, a flush, or even an ace-high with continued brick runouts. Calling keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to realize my draw equity on the turn. I remain in position for future streets, and if the turn improves my hand, I can apply pressure. Folding here would be too tight given the pot odds and hand equity.

I'm holding Ad7d on a board of 6s5dJcJs. Minni has checked to me on the turn, meaning she's given me a free card. At this point, I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs with 4s and 8s), which gives me approximately 32% equity against most made hands. The turn pairing the jacks has reduced the attractiveness of my holding, but I still have meaningful draw equity. Since Minni checked on the turn despite having bet the flop aggressively, this suggests either her hand isn't strong enough to continue applying pressure, or she's slower playing a made hand. Checking is the optimal play here because: (1) I get to see the river for free if she has a medium-strength hand, (2) betting with a draw out of position invites either a fold from hands I'm beating or a call/raise from hands that beat me, (3) I maximize my equity by seeing another card without risking additional chips. If the river bricks and she bets, I'll have the information needed to make a fold decision. If the river makes my straight, I'll be well-positioned to extract value.

I opened preflop and c-bet the dry 6♠-5♦-J♣ flop; Claude called. The turn pairs the board (J♣-J♠-6♠-5♦) which makes things a bit trickier (if Claude started with a J he now has trips), but he checked to me — that's either weakness or a slowplay. I have A♣4♥: little showdown value but I block some A-x combos and I have position. Betting here preserves fold equity and lets me take the pot down now against hands like 6x/5x/underpairs and some marginal Ax. I'll make a medium-size bet (~50% pot) to pressure those hands while risking a manageable amount of my deep stack. If Claude check-raises or jams, A4o is not worth a big confrontation so I'll fold; if he calls I'll re-evaluate on the river and proceed cautiously.

Calling the turn bet with Ad7d on 6s5dJcJs. I have an open-ended straight draw with 8 clean outs (any 4 or 8 makes me the nuts), giving me approximately 32% equity. Pot odds are 3:1 (2100:700), requiring only 25% equity to break even. My hand is mathematically profitable to call. Additionally, I have strong implied odds—if I hit the straight on the river, Minni's continued betting pattern suggests she'll likely pay me off. With one card remaining and this level of draw equity, calling is the correct play.

Betting 1400 (half the pot) for value with my pair of Jacks. Minni's aggressive play through turn followed by a river check indicates weakness - likely missed draws or marginal made hands. This bet extracts value from her range while testing if she has a stronger hand. If she raises significantly, I can fold. If she folds, I win a pot I'd likely take anyway. If she calls, I win with my strong hand most of the time.

I have A♣4♥ — no pair, just Ace-high. Line: I c‑bet the flop and bet the turn; Claude called both streets. That line heavily favors a made hand (Jx, 6x/5x, maybe a turned 8 or a slow‑played pair) rather than frequent bluffs. On the river he bets 1,400 into a 4,200 pot: calling 1,400 gives me pot odds of ~25% (1,400 / (4,200+1,400)). My actual equity with Ace‑high against his calling/river‑betting range is well below 25%, so a call is -EV. A raise would be an ambitious bluff that is likely to be called by any pair and is unnecessary when I have position and a deep stack to wait for better spots. Fold and preserve chips.

I'm holding 4c2c, the worst starting hand in poker (also known as 'deuce-four'). While position is favorable on the button, this hand has poor equity against any reasonable opponent range. Even accounting for positional advantage, 4-2 is fundamentally a weak hand that will lose value long-term. With a stack of 13,150 chips and being well-positioned in the tournament (hand 70 of 150), there's no justification for investing chips with this hand. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities where I can exploit my button position with actually playable cards.

Action is folded to me in the Small Blind. I have an Ace (A3o), which is significantly stronger than the average hand in a heads-up situation against the Big Blind. Raising here is standard to either win the blinds immediately or take the lead in the hand with a range advantage. I will raise to 300, consistent with the table's standard opening size.

Call. Facing a 3bb open from the small blind, T5o is marginal but defensible from the big blind heads-up. Calling costs 200 into a 400 pot (about 33% pot odds) and with deep stacks (~150bb effective) I have implied odds to realize equity on favorable flops (top pair, two pair, or occasional straights). I don't want to 3-bet this weak offsuit ten — a min 3-bet to 500 won’t fold enough hands and the opener will keep postflop position, so 3-betting is spotty and unnecessarily bloats the pot. Plan: call, see the flop, continue when I connect (or have good equity) and keep pot control / fold to heavy aggression on dry misses.

I have Ace-high on a very dry King-high board (Kd-8h-2s). As the pre-flop aggressor, this board favors my range more than the Big Blind's. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 300, which is half the pot, to put pressure on Minni's weaker holdings like small pocket pairs or unpaired hands that missed the flop, and to protect my Ace-high's showdown value.

Facing a 300 bet into a 900 pot on K♦ 8♥ 2♠, the c-bet is small (≈33% pot) and likely includes a wide range from the SB. Calling 300 to potentially win a ~1500 pot requires ≈20% equity. T♠5♦ has limited raw equity, but the price is cheap and I can float to see a turn, look for a pair or backdoor diamond/straight cards, and fold to heavy turn aggression. Raising OOP with this weak hand is a losing play and folding immediately concedes good implied odds to the preflop raiser. Plan: call the flop, re-evaluate on the turn — continue if I pair or pick up meaningful equity, otherwise fold to meaningful pressure. This controls the pot while exploiting a likely wide c-bet range.

The 7c is a brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. Minni's call on the very dry flop suggests she likely has a piece of the board, such as a pair of 8s or 2s, or possibly a better Ace. Since my Ace-high is unlikely to fold out a pair with a second barrel, and I still have some showdown value, I will check to control the pot size. This allows me to see a river card for free if she checks back or decide whether to fold/call based on her betting sizing if she leads.

I have T♦5♠ on K♦-8♥-2♠-7♣ — no pair and no realistic draw. Flash leading the flop then checking the turn looks like weakness or pot control with a made Kx; betting out of position with this airball would bloat the pot and get called by better hands (or fold only the very worst). Pot control is correct: check back the turn, keep the pot manageable, and reevaluate on the river. Plan: if I pair on the river I can consider a small value bet; otherwise fold to significant aggression or pick a river-bluff only if the board and opponent's line show clear weakness.

On this very dry board, my Ace-high has some showdown value, especially after the turn went check-check. Minni's turn check suggests she might have a marginal made hand (like an 8 or 7) or a hand she's looking to take to showdown cheaply. Betting now likely only gets called by better hands. By checking, I can potentially win a small pot if Minni checks back with a weaker hand or a missed high-card draw, and I can evaluate her bet sizing if she decides to lead out.

I have T5o and missed the board K♦‑8♥‑2♠‑7♣‑4♦ — no pair and no usable showdown value. Flash’s line (open preflop, small c‑bet on the flop, then check the turn and check the river) shows clear weakness: his flop stab failed to push me off and he relinquished initiative. The only way to win this pot now is a bluff. Pot is 1,200 — a half‑pot river bet (600) gives a good balance of fold equity vs. risk: it’s large enough to fold many medium pairs and showdown‑canditates but not so large that I overcommit with air. I will bet 600 to try to take it down; if he raises I’ll fold because I have no showdown value.

I currently hold Ace-high (Ac3s) on a board of Kd-8h-2s-7c-4d. After I checked the turn and river, Minni has led out for 600 into a 1200 pot. My Ace-high has some showdown value, but Minni's line of calling the flop and then betting the river after the turn went check-check often represents a marginal made hand (like a pair of 8s, 7s, or even 4s) looking for thin value. While the pot odds are 3:1, it is difficult to justify a call with only Ace-high here, as most of Minni's 'floats' on the flop that would now be bluffing (like 65s or 54s) have actually improved to pairs or were already beating me. I will fold and preserved my stack, as I still have a healthy 112 BBs.

I am on the button in a three-handed game with Ten-Six suited. In this position, I have a wide opening range to take advantage of the positional edge. A standard 3x raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and allows me to see a flop in position or take down the blinds immediately.

I have pocket 3s (3c3d) in the SB facing a standard button open to 300 (pot 450, I must put in 250 more). Stacks are deep (~156BB effective), so this is a textbook set‑mining spot: calling is cheap relative to the stacks and I can realize large implied odds if I hit a set (≈11.8% to flop a set). 3‑betting with a small pair is unattractive — I’d be OOP vs the raiser, risk a 4‑bet, and lose the implied‑odds value. Folding surrenders too much equity. Plan: call to see the flop (300 total). If I hit a set I’ll extract value; if I miss I’ll proceed cautiously and fold to heavy, coordinated aggression.

Folding 7♣J♣ in the big blind facing a raise from Flash and a call from Minni. This is a weak hand that's easily dominated by two opponents' ranges. Out of position with marginal equity doesn't justify the call. Preserve chips for better spots.

I called preflop with 3♣3♦ for set‑mining. Flop is J♦ 4♣ A♣ — I’ve missed and I’m an underpair with one club (no real draw). Standard play is pot control and to realize equity: check and keep the pot small. If BTN c-bets a small size (≤ ~1/3 pot ≈230) I’ll call to see a cheap turn (≈8.5% to hit trips by river). I’ll be willing to call medium-sized C-bets given deep stacks because of implied odds, but I’ll fold to large/overbet sizing that strongly polarizes to Ax/Jx/sets. If he checks back I’ll re-evaluate the turn and only invest more if I improve or get clear bluffing action.

On the button, I have 10-6 of clubs on an Ace-high flop with a backdoor flush draw. After raising pre-flop and being called by the Small Blind, a continuation bet is standard to represent the Ace and put pressure on hands that missed the flop or middle-pair hands like a Jack. The board is relatively dry, making it a good candidate for a half-pot C-bet to take the pot down immediately.

I called preflop to set‑mine with 3c3d. Flop Jd 4c Ac misses me — I’m an underpair with only a very weak backdoor club. Flash’s c‑bet of 350 into a 1,050 pot (~33% pot) is small enough to continue: calling 350 gives me ~25% pot odds and, with deep stacks, strong implied odds if I hit my set. Plan: check‑call to realize equity and see a cheap turn; I’ll fold to heavy turn/river aggression unless I improve to trips or get a clear bluffing line. Raising/bluffing OOP with this hand is suboptimal.

Check. I have pocket 3s (3♣3♦) — an underpair on J♦-4♣-A♣-K♣. The turn K♣ makes the board three clubs, massively increasing the chance the preflop raiser holds a two‑club flush. I have almost no showdown value or additional equity (only two outs to trips on the river), so my priority is pot control and preserving my deep stack. Checking keeps the pot small and lets me fold to any meaningful turn aggression. If the BTN checks back I can re‑evaluate on the river; if he fires a very small probe (uncharacteristic) I might consider a call, but I will fold to medium/large bets or raises. Raising/bluffing OOP with this hand is unattractive.

On the turn, the King of Clubs improves my hand significantly. I now have a flush draw to go along with my backdoor potential from the flop. The King is also a card that hits the range of the pre-flop aggressor. Since Minni checked again, I will continue to apply pressure with a semi-bluff. This bet keeps the initiative, can fold out weak Jacks or underpairs, and provides me with a great price to draw to my flush if called. I will bet 700, which is half the pot, to maintain a consistent sizing while building the pot for when I potentially hit my draw.

Preflop I called for set‑mining, and I checked the turn to control the pot. The K♣ on the turn completed a three‑club board; Flash’s line (open, small c‑bet, turn bet) now heavily favors club flushes, Ax/Kx with clubs, or other made hands. I have only two outs to make a set on the river (3♥/3♠) — roughly 4–4.5% equity — and even a river set can still lose to a club flush. The pot is 2,100 and the bet is 700, so I’m being offered ~25% pot odds (700/(2100+700)). My equity (~4.3%) is far below the required ~25%, and there are no realistic implied‑odds to justify calling or raising OOP against this line. Raising is also a poor bluff (out of position, deep stacks, little fold equity vs made hands). Fold and preserve the stack for better spots.

I’m on the button with K♥4♦ (a marginal offsuit king) and a healthy stack (≈150BB). With only the small and big blind left to act I want to leverage position and fold equity rather than limp. A standard 3xBB open (to 300 total) pressures the blinds, is consistent with the table’s sizing, and gives me the initiative post‑flop. If I face a sizable 3‑bet I’ll fold K4o; if I get called I’ll c‑bet dry flops and play cautiously on coordinated boards, using position to control the pot. Calling 100 is too passive and folding is too weak, so I raise to 300.

I'm in the small blind with pocket deuces (2s-2c) facing a button raise from Minni to 300. While small pairs have value for set mining, several factors make folding the right play here: (1) I'm out of position post-flop, (2) The big blind (Flash) hasn't acted yet and could 3-bet, making the pot odds less favorable, (3) This is hand 72 of 150, so I have ample opportunity to play premium hands. Small pairs are most profitable in position with good implied odds when both opponents are committed. This situation doesn't meet those criteria. Folding preserves my stack for better opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding 3s 5c. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. With 3-5 offsuit, I have a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Although I am getting 3.25-to-1 odds to call, the hand is frequently dominated and rarely hits strong enough to realize equity against a persistent opponent like Minni. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

With 6s 2h (one of the worst hands in poker), folding is the correct play despite having good position on the button. While the odds appear attractive (3:1 to call 100 into 150), 6-2 offsuit lacks playability postflop and wins rarely in multi-way pots. With 77 hands remaining in a 150-hand tournament, I don't need to force weak holdings. I should preserve my 13000 stack and wait for better spots where my positional advantage can be used with stronger hole cards.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Seven offsuit. The Button has folded, leaving only me and Minni in the Big Blind. T7o is a marginal hand, but in a three-handed game and especially in a blind-vs-blind battle, it is strong enough to open for a raise to attempt to steal the blinds or play the hand with initiative. Minni has been active, but applying pressure from the SB is a standard play here to maintain an aggressive image and pick up the pot uncontested.

Facing a SB open to 300 heads‑up with 3c3s and ~150bb effective. This is a classic set‑mining spot: calling 200 more (total 300 committed) gives good pot and implied odds to continue with plans to extract value if I hit a set. I’m out of position postflop so I don’t want to 3‑bet and bloat the pot unnecessarily; calling keeps weaker hands and enables me to fold to heavy action when I miss. Plan: call, evaluate flop — play aggressively if I flop a set or strong equity, otherwise proceed cautiously and fold to large pressure.

I am the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind situation. The flop is very dry (Kh-6d-2s), which favors my perceived range as the raiser, as I can easily have strong Kings (AK, KQ, KJ) or pocket pairs. Minni's calling range from the Big Blind is likely wide, containing many hands that missed this board entirely. A standard continuation bet here should be effective in winning the pot immediately. I will bet 300, which is 50% of the pot, to apply pressure without over-committing with just Ten-high.

I called preflop as a set‑mining spot with 3c3s. The flop (6d Kh 2s) completely misses my hand — I only have a low pocket pair. Flash bets 300 into a 900 pot; calling 300 to win 1200 gives me ~25% pot odds. My chance to make a set by the river is only ~8.4% (2 outs), far below the required equity. I’m out of position and many of Flash’s continuing hands (Kx, 6x, 2x, overpairs, strong broadways) already beat me or will apply pressure on later streets. A bluff raise is too risky with deep stacks and can put me in difficult spots if reraised. Fold and preserve my stack for better spots.

On the button with Queen-Ten suited in a 3-handed game, this is a premium hand that I should open for a standard raise. Raising to 300 is consistent with previous play and maximizes value while putting pressure on the blinds.

7♦9♣ is a marginal, offsuit connector and I’m out of position in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300 with the big blind still to act. Pot is 450; calling 250 requires ~35.7% equity to be correct. Versus a button open-range (and given Claude’s tendency to defend/call), my realized/implied odds are poor and this will likely become a multi-way OOP pot. A minimum 3-bet to 500 would be too small to generate reliable fold equity and a larger 3-bet would commit chips with a weak holding. With a healthy 14,800 stack I should conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

Flash raised to 300 from the button, Minni folded from the small blind, and I'm in the big blind with 4d Jc. 

Hand Analysis: J4 offsuit is a weak hand, particularly out of position. While I have pot odds of approximately 2.25:1 (needing to call 200 into a 450 pot), my hand lacks playability post-flop. Even in a heads-up situation against a wide button raising range, J4o will struggle significantly.

Position Consideration: I'm out of position (big blind) facing a button raise, which compounds the difficulty of playing this hand profitably.

Stack Consideration: At 12,900 chips with 55 hands remaining (75 of 150), I have a healthy stack and should not feel pressured to play marginal hands. Preserving my stack for premium opportunities is the correct long-term strategy.

Decision: Folding is the optimal play. The hand is simply too weak to defend with, and folding weakly here is better than calling and playing a difficult post-flop scenario with J4o out of position.

Button with A♣9♠ versus only the blinds: this is a standard open-raise spot. A9o plays well postflop in position (good ace-blockers and decent broadway/straight potential) and we have deep stacks so we can apply pressure. Table dynamic has many 300 opens, so sizing to 300 (3x BB) achieves fold equity and keeps sizing consistent — it’s big enough to discourage loose calls from the blinds but small enough to allow profitable postflop play. Plan: open to 300; if we face a large 3-bet we’ll fold A9o out of position, but we can continue against small 3-bets or single calls and use position to take control of the pot.

Folding with Js5s from the small blind. This is a weak, unplayable hand in this spot. I'm facing a raise from an aggressive player (Minni) on the button, Flash is still to act in the big blind, and I'm out of position. Js5s has poor equity against Minni's raising range and cannot win without improvement. Even if I call and Flash folds, I'd be heads-up with a weak hand in the worst position at the table. This is a clear fold - I should conserve chips for premium hands where I have positional and equity advantages.

I am in the Big Blind holding 7h 5h, which is a decent speculative hand in a three-handed game. Minni raised to 300 from the button, and Claude folded. I need to call 200 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 650. The odds are favorable (2.25 to 1), and with my stack size, I can afford to see a flop to try and hit a straight, flush, or two pair. I will play defensively out of position and see how the board develops.

I am first to act on a flop of Ah-Qs-Th. This board is very dangerous as it connects well with the button's opening range (high cards and pairs). I have 7-high with a backdoor flush draw. I have very little equity here and no reason to lead out. I will check and likely fold if Minni bets, as this board is much better for her range than mine.

We raised preflop and hit top pair on a fairly dry A‑Q‑T board. Flash checked from the big blind so we have position and a wide calling range from him (worse Aces, Qx, Tx, some pocket pairs and broadways). Betting extracts value from worse Aces and queens, charges any backdoor hearts/straight draws, and keeps control of the pot. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (325) — big enough to get value and deny cheap equity, but not so large that a check‑raise commits the stack without a stronger read. Re‑evaluate on the turn if he check‑raises or gives action.

On a flop of Ah-Qs-Th, I have a flush draw with my 7h and 5h. Minni has bet 325 into a pot of 650, which is exactly a 1/2 pot bet. I need to call 325 to win a total pot of 1300, giving me 4-to-1 odds (25%). With 9 outs to a flush, I have approximately an 18-19% chance of hitting on the turn. While the direct pot odds are slightly below what I need, the implied odds if I hit my flush against an opponent who likely has a strong piece of this board (Aces, Queens, or Tens) make this a profitable call. I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate; if I don't hit a heart or if the board pairs, I will likely fold to further pressure.

I am out of position against Minni on a very connected and now paired board. The Ten of Spades on the turn is a significant card, as it gives anyone with a Ten three-of-a-kind. Since I am holding only a flush draw and seven-high, I have no reason to lead out and build a pot against a range that likely contains Aces, Queens, or Tens. I will check to see how Minni responds; if she bets, I will have to decide if the price is right to continue chasing my flush, keeping in mind that the board pairing increases the risk of being against a full house.

I have top pair (A♣9♠) in position on A‑Q‑T‑T. Flash called my flop half‑pot c‑bet and then checked the turn, which looks like either pot‑control with Qx/Ax or weakness — not a clear check‑raise line from a monster. The turn pairing the Ten does introduce risk of trips/full houses for hands with a Ten, but those strong combos are relatively rare and would often have shown more aggression earlier. I want to extract value from worse Aces, Queens and some marginal Tens while keeping the pot controllable, so I’ll bet about half‑pot. Size chosen (650) is big enough to get value and deny free cards but small enough that I can fold to a large check‑raise given my mediocre kicker; if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the board has paired with the Ten of Spades, creating a board of Ah-Qs-Th-Ts. While I still have a flush draw with my 7h-5h, the board pairing is problematic. It significantly increases the probability that Minni (the original raiser) has improved to a full house or at least three-of-a-kind, given that her range includes many Tens, Queens, and Aces. Minni is betting 650 into a 1300 pot, offering me 3-to-1 pot odds (needs 25% equity). My chance of hitting a flush on the river is approximately 19-20%. Because the pot odds are slightly unfavorable and the board pairing makes a flush potentially 'not the best hand' even if it hits, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Folding Q9o on the button. While I have position, this hand is too weak to profitably invest in a 3-way pot against two aggressive opponents (Minni and Flash). Q9 offsuit needs significant equity that I don't have here, and both opponents are likely to contest the pot. With a healthy 128BB stack, folding marginal hands to wait for premium opportunities is sound strategy in this aggressive field.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Trey offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has already folded from the Button. While I only need to commit 50 more chips to see a flop, playing such a weak hand out of position against Minni, who has been active and aggressive, is unlikely to be profitable in the long run. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a 3-handed game. T6o is a very weak starting hand, typically outside of the standard opening range for this position even in a short-handed format. While I have been active from the button in previous hands, Minni and Claude have shown they are willing to defend their blinds and play post-flop. Given the stack depths and the low value of the hand, folding is the most prudent play to wait for a better opportunity.

I'm in the small blind with 8♠9♣ (off-suit) and only the big blind to act. In a 3-handed (effectively heads-up vs Claude after Flash folded) spot this hand is playable and it's better to take the initiative than to limp/call out of position. A standard 2BB open (total to 200) achieves fold equity, isolates the BB and lets me play the flop on my terms. If Claude 3-bets large (a significant 3-bet) I'll fold this marginal, out-of-position holding; if he calls or makes a small 3-bet I'll proceed cautiously — c-bet dry flops, check/float or call on coordinated boards and try to realize equity. Therefore I raise to the minimum open of 200.

I'm in the Big Blind with Th3h against Minni's raise to 200 from the Small Blind (Flash folded). I need to call 100 more chips into a 300 chip pot, giving me exactly 3:1 odds. 

T3o is a weak hand, but the pot odds are compelling. Against Minni's fairly wide Small Blind raising range, I have approximately 30-35% equity, which exceeds the 25% equity required to break even at 3:1 odds. Additionally, I'll have position post-flop after Minni acts, which provides flexibility.

Given the reasonable pot odds, this is a profitable call in the long run. My stack is healthy enough (12,750) to take this spot and see a flop. A fold would be overly tight and sacrifice positive expected value spots.

I call the 100.

I have 9♣8♠ on 9♦8♦3♣ — top two pair heads-up. This is a very strong hand but the board has two diamonds, so there are some dangerous combos (two-card diamond combos with a flush draw, and the occasional set like 9-9 or 8-8). I want to get value from worse pairs/straight draws and charge diamond draws while keeping the pot manageable. Betting ~60% of the pot is the right balance: it extracts value from single-pair hands and draws and makes hunters pay to chase. Plan: bet for value/protection now; if I'm called I'll re-evaluate on the turn (continue on safe cards, be cautious and check/defend or fold to large aggression if a diamond or a card that completes obvious strong combos appears).

Analyzing this flop situation with T3h on 9d-8d-3c:

**Hand Strength:**
- I have bottom pair (threes) plus a gutshot straight draw (four clean 7s make me a straight)
- This gives me approximately 5-8 total outs depending on how the hand develops

**Pot Odds:**
- Current pot: 650
- To call: 250
- Odds: 2.6:1
- Breakeven equity needed: ~28%

**Against Minni's Range:**
- Her SB raise preflop was wide (shown by her frequent raising patterns)
- Her flop raise to 250 could represent:
  - Continuation bet with various holdings
  - Semi-bluff with draws
  - Overpairs or top pair/top kicker hands
  
My bottom pair likely has decent equity against a significant portion of her range, and my gutshot gives me additional outs.

**Risk-Reward:**
- Pot odds are favorable for a call with my equity and outs
- Stack sizes allow me to continue (12,650 remaining after call)
- Calling keeps my hand disguised and maintains flexibility on the turn
- Being OOP is a drawback, but the odds justify continuing

**Decision:**
Calling allows me to see another card, maintain position flexibility, and keep the pot manageable. This is a profitable long-run spot that exceeds the breakeven equity threshold.

I have 9♣8♠ on 9♦8♦3♣6♣ — top two pair vs Claude who called my flop bet. The board has two diamonds, so diamond draws and the occasional set (99/88) are the main dangers, but many of Claude’s calling hands (single pairs, worse two-pair combos, and diamond draws) will pay to continue. I want to extract value from worse pairs and charge diamond/straight/backdoor draws while denying a free river. Pot is 900; a bet of ~2/3 pot (~600) balances protection and value without overcommitting my stack. Plan: bet 600 now to charge draws and get value; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river (value-bet on blanks, proceed cautiously or check/fold to large aggression if a diamond completes a flush or an obvious crushing card appears). If he raises large on the turn I’ll narrow his range to sets/strong made hands and respond based on sizing (fold to an overcommitment, call smaller raises to get to showdown).

Folding with bottom pair and a gutshot on the turn. Pot odds of 2.5:1 require ~29% equity, but I likely have only 18-22% equity with Th3h against Minni's range (which likely includes top pair, overpairs, or stronger draws after her turn aggression). The math does not support continuing. Minni's flop raise followed by turn re-raise indicates strength, making my weak pair vulnerable. This is a mathematically negative expected value call despite earlier favorable odds on the flop.

Button with KQo — a strong, standard open-raise hand. With three players and deep stacks I want initiative and fold equity to isolate the blinds and avoid playing a marginal hand passively out of position. A 3x BB sizing (raise to 300 total) is conventional here: it applies pressure, keeps the pot manageable if called, and matches the table’s common raise sizes. If I face a large 3-bet I’ll fold; I’ll consider calling a small 3-bet to realize equity and leverage position postflop. Plan postflop: c-bet on dry/advantageous flops, exercise pot control or check-back on heavily connected/overcard boards and use position to extract value or fold when strongly resisted.

Folding 9d5d from the small blind. This is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Minni has been an aggressive raiser throughout the tournament, frequently opening from the button. With only 250 to call but facing a likely continuation bet post-flop while out of position, this hand doesn't justify continuing. The equity of 9-5 offsuit doesn't warrant building a pot from the SB against aggressive play. I'll preserve chips for stronger opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Spades and Six of Hearts. Minni, who has been quite active from the Button, raised to 300. I have 200 more to call into a total pot that would be 650. This hand is generally too weak to defend profitably against a standard Button raise, even with the discount. I have a healthy stack and don't need to force a play with a mediocre-to-poor hand out of position. I'll fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the Button with Q8s (Queen-Eight suited), which is a playable hand in position. The pot is 150 chips and I need to call 100. With two opponents and my stack at 12,350, I have excellent position for post-flop play. The odds are favorable (150:100 on a 1.5:1 call) and button position allows me to act last throughout the hand, giving me information advantage. While Q8s isn't a premium hand, the combination of position, pot odds, and potential to outplay opponents post-flop makes calling the right decision. I can fold easily if the flop misses, or leverage my position if I hit top pair, a flush draw, or connect with the board.

I am in the Small Blind with Ten-Six offsuit. The Button (Claude) has limped in. While the price to call is only 50 chips, Ten-Six is a very weak hand to play out of position against two opponents, one of whom (Minni) has shown significant aggression in previous hands. Folding here avoids potentially losing more chips in a marginal post-flop situation.

I have A♠T♣ in the big blind facing a single limp from the button. With deep stacks and only one opponent this is a clear isolation spot — ATo plays well heads-up and I want to take the initiative rather than give the button a cheap, positional flop. A standard 3x isolation raise (to 300 total) will fold out weaker speculative hands, give me fold equity, and allow me to play a pot with a strong Ace against a single opponent. Checking hands over gives the button free odds and keeps multiway possibilities; folding is unnecessary. If Claude 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a large 3-bet, call or 4-bet against smaller sizings), but preflop the correct action is to raise and isolate.

Calling 200 more chips (300 total) with Q8s on the button. Excellent pot odds of 2.25:1, button position advantage for post-flop play, and reasonable hand potential with flush/straight draw possibilities. Minni's raise is standard-range from BB, and my stack depth allows for good post-flop dynamics. Position and odds make this a clear call.

I raised preflop to isolate Claude and I still have initiative heads-up. The flop J♣ 3♥ 9♦ is dry and rainbow — I have A♠T♣ (no pair) with only a very weak backdoor Broadway (needs two running cards). My hand has modest showdown value but I block a lot of the strongest A‑x combos (reducing some two‑pair/slowplay combos) and the opponent’s limp/call range contains many missed hands and small pairs. A standard continuation bet here will pick up the pot often or define Claude’s range while keeping the pot controlled. I’ll size for about half the pot to generate fold equity but not overcommit with just Ace‑high. Plan: bet ~1/2 pot (325). If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he raises a large amount I’ll fold (I don’t want to get stacked with merely Ace‑high).

I'm calling Minni's 325-chip flop bet with Q♠8♠ on a J♣3♥9♦ board. Hand analysis: I have an inside straight draw (need a Ten for 8-9-10-J-Q), plus a spade flush draw. With 11 clean outs (4 tens + 7 non-ten spades, with T♠ counting both), I have approximately 44% equity. Pot odds are favorable at 3:1 (975 to call 325), well above my break-even point of ~2.2:1. Key factors supporting this call: (1) I'm in button position with information and last action advantage on turn and river, (2) Minni is an aggressive player who continuation bets frequently, and her bet could contain many weaker hands or missed holdings, (3) My drawing equity combined with position allows for profitable post-flop decisions on turn/river based on board development and opponent action. This is a classic position and equity-based call with strong post-flop potential.

I just improved to second pair (Ten) with A♠T♣ on the turn (board J♣ 3♥ 9♦ T♥). Claude cold‑called my isolation preflop and my half‑pot c‑bet on the dry flop, so his calling range is wide (Jx, 9x/3x, pairs, some broadways and floats). The turn gives me decent showdown value and blocks some A‑x hands. There are very few realistic single‑card straights and only a two‑heart backdoor flush possibility, so betting to extract value and deny equity is appropriate. I’ll take the initiative and bet about half the pot (≈650 into a 1,300 pot) to get value from worse pairs/Tx/9x and to charge any backdoor draws. If Claude raises large I’ll give up (second pair is vulnerable); if he calls I’ll reevaluate on the river.

I have a made Queen-high straight (8-9-10-J-Q) on Q♠8♠. This is a strong holding that beats the majority of Minni's likely range. She could have an Ace-high straight (AK), but that's only one component of her likely holdings. Pot odds are excellent at 3:1. Even accounting for reverse implied odds on the river, calling is clearly profitable. The river card will likely maintain or improve my hand strength.

I have second pair (T) with the Ace kicker, but the K on the river significantly improves Claude’s calling range (any Kx or many broadway combos now beat me). Claude has been passive (limp/call pre, call on flop and turn) so his line contains a lot of made pairs and few bluffs — a river bet is likely to be called by better (Kx/Jx) or fold out only the very weakest holdings. Checking keeps the pot controlled, lets me see a showdown vs his calling line, and avoids bloating the pot where I’m often behind. Plan: check to river, call a reasonable small-to-medium bet, but fold to a large shove or polarizing raise.

I hold a King-high straight (K-Q-J-T-9) on a J♣3♥9♦T♥K♠ board, which is the second-strongest possible straight and beats almost all of Minni's likely holdings except AK. Minni's river check after aggressive pre-flop and flop/turn play suggests either marginal value, uncertainty, or a missed holding. I should bet to extract value from hands like QJ, QT, premium pairs (TT, 99), and ties like AQ. A bet of 1200 (roughly half the 2600 pot) is appropriately sized to maximize value while remaining proportional to the action. This is a straightforward value bet with a premium made hand.

Claude has been passive (limp/call pre, call flop and turn) and his small river bet (1,200 into a 3,800 pot) looks like a thin value/probe. Calling 1,200 gets me ~24% equity to win the 5,000 pot — a cheap price to see showdown with second pair (T) and the Ace kicker, which also blocks many A‑x bluffs. Raising would bloat the pot with a likely inferior hand and folding gives up too much with decent pot odds. I will call the 1,200 and fold only to a large shove or a polarizing raise.

I am on the button with Q4o. While opening ranges in 3-handed play are wider, Q4o is at the very bottom of the range and often a fold against aggressive or sticky opponents. Minni and Claude have shown consistent activity and resistance in recent hands. Folding here is the safest and most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up from the small blind with 4♥5♦ — a marginal, low connected hand. Pot is 150 and I need 50 to call (pot odds ~3:1), so a call requires ≈25% equity to break even. Versus a wide BB defending range this hand has reasonable equity, so completing to see a flop is +EV. I’m out of position and Claude has shown willingness to defend and call/raise, so I don’t want to inflate the pot with a weak hand by min-raising to 200. Plan: call 50 to see the flop, then proceed cautiously — fold to heavy aggression on dry boards, but continue/raise when I hit a pair, two pair, or strong draws (OESD/close straights) or get favorable runouts.

Heads-up against a small blind limp with 3h9s - an extremely weak hand. Checking to see the flop cheaply is the correct play. No need to build the pot out of position with such a poor holding. Minni's limp suggests weakness, and I can evaluate the flop before committing more chips. My goal is to minimize losses on this hand and preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Check. I’m OOP on A♦ T♦ 6♣ with 4♥5♦ — no made hand, just an open‑ended straight draw (3s and 7s = 8 clean outs, ~31% to hit a straight by river) and a backdoor diamond possibility. Counting pair-outs (4s/5s) gives ~14 outs to improve by river (~52%), but many of those will still lose to Ax or Tx. Betting OOP here would be a thin bluff on an Ace‑high flop against a defender (Claude) who calls down fairly often, so my fold equity is limited and I’d be pricing myself into tough spots. Best EV line is to check and realize equity: call small turns to chase or fold to significant pressure, and only invest more if I hit a 3/7 (straight), pair with good redraws, or pick up a strong draw. Action: check.

Minni limped pre-flop and checked the flop, both signals of weakness. With 3h9s, I have zero showdown value, so I need to win the pot immediately rather than check it down. A bet of 125 takes advantage of her passive play and should win the pot against her weak range. This is a standard aggression spot against weak limpers.

Claude's raise to 125 into a 325 pot gives me ~27.8% break‑even equity to call. With 4♥5♦ on A♦ T♦ 6♣ I have an open‑ended straight draw (8 clean outs ≈31.5% to hit by the river), plus a small backdoor diamond/paired‑card equity — enough to make a call +EV. Raising OOP with only a draw would be thin (limited fold equity and it bloats a pot I don’t want to play out of position), so I’ll call to realize equity and keep options open. Plan: if I hit a 3/7 (the straight) be aggressive; if I miss and face heavy turn pressure, fold.

Check. The turn K♦ gives me a four‑diamond situation (Ad, Td, Kd on board + my 5♦) so my primary realistic way to make a strong hand is the diamond on the river (9 clean diamond outs ≈ 19.6%). Counting non‑diamond pair outs (the remaining 4s and 5s) gives ~14 unique outs to improve to a pair or a flush (~30.4%), but those pair outs are often still second‑pair versus an Ace/Ten/King. Claude bet the flop and I called; he checked the turn — that line looks like pot control with a wide/value range or a disguised made hand. I’m out of position and don’t want to bloat the pot with a thin semi‑bluff that will be called by Ax/Tx/Kx or run into a made flush if Claude holds two diamonds.

Checking preserves equity and keeps the pot manageable while I realize my draws. If Claude checks back I get a free shot at the river; if he bets I can call a reasonable size to chase my outs (or fold to extreme pressure if the sizing makes the math negative). If I hit the flush on the river I’ll play aggressively for value; if I only pair a 4/5 I’ll proceed cautiously against continued aggression from Claude.

Minni checked the turn after calling my 125 flop bet. The King of Diamonds is a very bad turn card for my 3h9s - it adds another broadway card that likely improved her hand significantly if she has any K, broadway cards, or diamond holdings. I have zero showdown value with this hand and cannot win at showdown against most of Minni's likely holdings (any pair, any K, any diamond draw that completed). The aggressive flop bet was reasonable against her weakness, but now that I'm called and the board has improved, I should control pot size and avoid putting more chips into a pot where I'm almost certainly behind. Checking allows me to see the river cheaply and only invest further if there's clear weakness. If I bet again here with no equity, I'm just burning chips. Let's check and evaluate the river.

I missed the river — 4♥5♦ has no pair, no straight and no flush on A♦ T♦ 6♣ K♦ 7♠. Claude’s line (flop raise, then turn check) looks like a value/pot‑control range (Ax, Tx, Kx) or a turned draw that checked down; he’s not folding to a small river stab very often. With zero showdown value and limited blocker value from a single diamond, a river bluff here has poor fold equity and a high chance of getting called or raised off the hand. Pot is only 450; I’m not going to bloat the pot or risk a raise with nothing. Check and let him show his hand — fold to meaningful aggression on the river if he bets.

Minni's checking line from pre-flop through river indicates significant weakness. With zero showdown value on 3♥9♠, I must attempt to win the pot immediately. A small bet of 150 (1/3 pot) is appropriate sizing for a river bluff that exploits her apparent surrender. She's given multiple weakness signals: limping pre-flop, check-calling flop, then checking turn and river. This is a spot where I win regardless of her holdings - if she has anything worth showing down, I lose at showdown anyway, so a bluff is my only path to the pot.

Board: A♦ T♦ 6♣ K♦ 7♠. My hand 4♥5♦ — no pair, no straight, no flush. Pot = 600, to call = 150 → break-even equity = 150/(600+150) = 20%. Claude raised the flop (shows a strong or value-heavy range), checked the turn (pot control or disguised value) and now makes a small river bet of 150. That line is far more consistent with thin value (A/T/K, made 2p/trips, or a turned draw) than with frequent bluffs. I don’t block his likely value hands and have essentially zero showdown equity, so calling requires him to bluff ≥20% of the time — unlikely given his line. Raising OOP as a pure bluff from air is too thin and just bloats the pot or gets forced off. Fold and conserve chips for better spots.

Button with A♣4♥ — an ace with a weak kicker but a very good hand to raise for position and fold equity. I’ll open to 300 (3x BB): that sizing is consistent with my table image (I’ve been raising ~300) and gives enough fold equity vs the SB/BB while keeping postflop play manageable in position. Plan: if called, C‑bet thin on dry or A‑high flops and play for value when I pair the ace or a 4; check/ pot‑control or fold to heavy action on coordinated/flushy boards. If I face a sizable 3‑bet, I’ll fold (hand is dominated vs standard 3‑bet ranges); I’ll only consider continuing vs a very small 3‑bet from the BB to see a flop in position. Action: raise to 300.

Folding 3♣6♦ in the small blind. This is one of poker's weakest hands - low, gapped, and offsuit. Against Minni's button raise, I have poor equity and will be out of position post-flop. With a healthy 15,050 stack and 69 hands remaining, there's no reason to take this marginal spot. The correct long-term strategy is folding weak hands and waiting for positive EV situations.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s 9h. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. Although the pot odds are roughly 2.25 to 1 to call, my hand is extremely weak and plays poorly out of position. Given the stack depths and the fact that I have over 110 big blinds, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand against a standard button open.

7♥9♠ is a marginal hand, but I'm on the button in a strong position to act last preflop. I'm facing a small 100-chip call into a 150-chip pot (2.5:1 odds), which is reasonable for a speculative hand in position. Both Flash and Minni have shown aggressive preflop tendencies but can be outmaneuvered postflop. With a healthy stack of 15,050, I can afford to take a flop here and use position to control the action. 7-9 has decent potential to hit draws and has some playability when executing position-based aggression if the flop is favorable. This is a standard button call against two aggressive blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with Jc 2d. Claude, on the button, has limped in for 100. The pot is currently 250 and it costs me 50 to call. While J2o is a weak hand, getting 5-to-1 odds to see a flop against a limper is acceptable in a three-way dynamic, especially with the current stack depths. However, Minni in the big blind is aggressive. If I call, I risk a raise from the big blind or playing a weak hand out of position. Given the tournament context and my stack size, folding such a weak hand is safer to avoid being outplayed post-flop in a multi-way pot with a sub-optimal holding.

I'm in the big blind with J♥6♥ facing a limp from the button (Claude). Heads-up his limp is a wide, often weak range; J6s has decent heads-up playability (backdoor hearts and straight potential). I prefer to take the initiative and isolate rather than check and let him see a cheap flop; a standard 3x BB raise (to 300) applies pressure, folds out the very weakest holdings, and keeps the pot a reasonable size with a marginal hand. Postflop plan: if he calls I'll c-bet on dry textures and pursue backdoor equity; if he 3-bets large I'll fold to avoid playing a bloated pot out of position. Raise to 300 to isolate and seize initiative.

I'm on the button with 7♥9♠ facing Minni's raise to 300 from the BB. Flash has folded, leaving me heads-up in position against an aggressive opponent. I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds (450:200) to call. 7♥9♠ is a speculative but playable hand with straight potential (any 5, 6, 8, or T makes me a strong draw). Key factors supporting a call: (1) excellent position as the button - I act last postflop and can control the action; (2) Minni has shown consistent aggression throughout the tournament, making her susceptible to position-based play; (3) healthy stack (14,950) allows me to take controlled risks; (4) decent equity and playability postflop - the hand improves frequently on most flops. While 7♥9♠ isn't premium, the combination of position, pot odds, and opponentProfile makes this a standard profitable call in this spot.

I was the preflop aggressor vs a limp-caller with a wide, often weak range. Flop Ah-3s-Kd has an Ace and a King that hit his calling range, but it's otherwise pretty dry and has few real draws. My J♥6♥ has almost no showdown value (only remote runner-runner heart/straight outs), so I need to use fold equity rather than check/call. I will make a standard half-pot continuation bet (325) to apply pressure to his unpaired hands and small pairs while keeping the pot manageable. Plan: if he folds we take it; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to a sizable raise since my equity is low.

7♥9♠ completely misses the A♥3♠K♦ flop. I have zero made hand strength and only marginal straight draw potential (needing A-4 or K-2 connections that are unlikely). Minni has shown aggression preflop and on the flop, indicating a strong hand (likely pair or premium ace). While pot odds appear favorable at 4:1, my actual equity is minimal. Even if I hit a 7 or 9, I'm easily dominated by her likely holdings. This is a standard fold—speculative hands require much stronger equity profiles and draw potential to justify calls against showing strength. Position cannot overcome fundamental hand weakness in this spot.

Holding J3o on the button is a clear fold. While it's tempting to steal the blinds in a three-handed game, this hand is at the very bottom of any reasonable range and has almost no post-flop playability. Given that both Minni and Claude have been active and willing to defend their blinds or raise, I will wait for a stronger hand to engage.

We’re heads-up after the button folded, holding 4♠3♣ — a weak, low unsuited connector but playable in heads-up because I’ll have position post-flop. The pot is 150 and calling 50 is cheap, but raising to the min (200 total) gives me initiative and fold equity vs Claude’s wide calling range while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold this hand; if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and exercise pot control on dry boards. Raise to 200 to try to steal the blind and play the hand in position.

Calling 100 chips with Ah3s from the big blind against Minni's small blind raise. Excellent pot odds (3:1), any ace has decent equity against her aggressive raising range, and I can play post-flop aggressively if I connect. Folding would be too tight here.

I raised preflop with 4♠3♣ to take the initiative. Flop Q♦ J♣ 2♥ is a very dry, high-card board and I have no pair or meaningful draw. Heads‑up and in position I should continue aggression to leverage fold equity. Claude will have a lot of hands that miss this flop (Ax, Kx, small broadways) but he can be sticky; so I choose a modest c‑bet to apply pressure without bloating the pot. Bet half‑pot (200 into the 400) — if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I exercise pot control on the turn and only continue with real improvement; if he check‑raises big I will fold as I have negligible showdown equity.

I hold Ah3s on a Qd-Jc-2h flop. This is ace-high, which is a solid but not premium holding on this board. Minni's 200 bet into a ~350 pot gives me 3:1 pot odds, which is favorable for calling with any ace and reasonable kicker. Given Minni's aggressive small blind raising pattern throughout the tournament, her bet could represent a very wide range: pairs (Q, J, 2, pocket pairs), strong aces, or even bluffs. My hand has decent equity against most of this range. Out of position, I want to keep the hand alive without over-committing at this early stage. A call allows me to see the turn action, gather more information, and potentially take the hand down if the turn allows. The pot odds and hand equity both support this conservative approach versus a premature raise.

Check. I opened preflop and c-bet the dry Q♢ J♣ 2♥ flop and Claude called, making the pot 800. The turn A♣ is a very bad card for my 4♠3♣ — I have no pair and no realistic draw (with only one club in my hand I cannot make a 5‑card club flush by the river). The A significantly improves Claude’s flop‑call range (Ax, A‑X broadways, many hands that now have at least pair or strong showdown equity), so a double‑barrel bluff is low EV and will face many calls or a check‑raise. I’ll control the pot and see a river by checking; fold to any substantial lead/raise and re-evaluate on the river if checked to me.

Turn value bet with top pair (pair of aces). Minni's flop bet followed by turn check indicates a marginal hand or a miss. The Ace that hit is excellent for my holding and I should build the pot by betting approximately 56% of the current pot. This sizing allows me to get value from weaker aces, queens, jacks, and drawing hands while not overcommitting if I'm behind. Minni's aggressive small blind pattern suggests she could have a wide range including KQ, small pairs, or weaker aces that would call this bet.

I checked the turn with 4♠3♣ as planned. Claude now bets/raises to 450 into the pot (pot shown 1250), so a call of 450 would risk 450 to win 1700 total — I need ≈26.5% equity to make a call break-even (450/1700). My hand has essentially only six clean outs (the three remaining 3s and three remaining 4s) — ~6/46 ≈ 13% to pair on the river — well below the required equity. Worse, many of those hits still lose to hands his line represents: Aces, Qx/Jx, sets or two-pair combos (he cold-called preflop, called my flop c-bet, now barrels the Ace). I have no realistic backdoor draw and very little fold equity, so calling is negative EV and raising is unjustifiable. Fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

I open‑raise to 300 (3x BB). On the button in a three‑handed game A♦4♥ is worth raising: I have position, an ace blocker and good fold equity versus the blinds. The pot is only 150 and I’m deep, so a 300 sizing keeps the pot controllable if called while pressuring both SB and BB to fold weaker holdings. Plan: if called I’ll continuation‑bet favorable flops (ace‑high or dry boards) and proceed cautiously versus strong resistance; fold to a large 3‑bet since A4o plays poorly against committed 3‑betting ranges. Overall this play maximizes steal equity and preserves stack flexibility.

Calling with JT from small blind against button raise. Good pot odds (2.2:1) and the hand plays well postflop with straight and pair potential. Better to see the flop and use postflop skills than raise with a non-premium hand out of position. Avoiding bloating pot preflop while maintaining flexibility for the big blind's decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. Minni opened for 3x from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot is currently 700 chips, and it only costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5:1 (or I need about 22% equity). Against two wide ranges, Q6o typically has enough raw equity to justify a call, even out of position, especially in a multi-way pot where I can see a flop cheaply. I will call to see the flop and play conservatively thereafter.

JT on a K-5-2 board is a marginal hand with good potential rather than current strength. I have a gutshot straight draw (any Q makes me a straight) and two overcards to the low cards on board. Being out of position with three opponents in the hand makes checking the prudent play. Betting here would commit chips with a hand that's currently behind most reasonable ranges these opponents would hold. Checking allows me to: (1) keep the pot manageable with a speculative hand, (2) see how the original raiser (Minni) acts, (3) potentially get a free card if everyone checks through, or (4) make an informed decision on future streets. The equity of JT (roughly 35-40% in multi-way pot) improves significantly if I hit the straight, so staying cheap to see more cards is optimal.

I have missed the flop completely with Queen-high. The board features a King, which is very much in the range of the pre-flop raiser, Minni. Claude has already checked, and I will do the same. Given I have no pair and no real draw, I plan to fold to any significant bet from the Button.

I opened to 300 on the button and both called; the flop K♠? (K♥) 5♣ 2♠ is very dry and checks to me. I have A♦4♥ — a weak ace but an important blocker to A‑x and a good hand to c‑bet for fold equity. Both opponents’ checks show weakness or medium single‑paired holdings; this is an ideal spot to steal the pot or charge hands with little equity. I size to ~1/2 pot (450): large enough to force folds from unconnected hands and protect my range, but small enough to keep the pot controllable if called. Plan: if called I proceed cautiously on the turn (check/control unless I improve or see weakness); if I face a large check‑raise I’ll fold since my hand has poor equity versus strong ranges (Kx/sets).

Calling Minni's flop bet of 450 on K-5-2 with JT. I'm getting 4:1 pot odds for a hand with ~8 outs (gutshot straight + overcards = 32-40% equity). This is profitable immediate odds for a drawing hand. Flash checked weakly, suggesting minimal strength. Minni's bet is consistent with button aggression on a dry board. Deep stacks allow me to see the turn card where hitting a Q gives me a very strong hand. Overcalling maintains a reasonable pot with position for the turn decision.

I am in the Big Blind with Queen-Six offsuit. On a flop of K-5-2, I have completely missed. Minni (the pre-flop aggressor) bet 450 into a 900 pot, and Claude (the Small Blind) called that bet. This call from Claude is significant as it suggests he has at least a piece of the board, a pocket pair, or a draw. I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw. Even though the pot odds are roughly 4:1, I have almost zero equity in this three-way pot and no profitable way to continue. The best play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Checking the turn with pair of tens on 2s 5c Kh Td. While the Ten improved my hand from a draw to a made hand, I'm still vulnerable to Minni's likely strong holding given Minni's preflop raise and flop aggression into two checkers. Minni probably has top pair or better. Checking allows me to: (1) see what Minni does, (2) control the pot out of position with a marginal made hand, (3) get information to make a better river decision. If Minni bets, I'll have decision point with decent pot odds for a made hand. If Minni checks back, I see the river for free or can value bet. This is superior to betting and forcing Minni to make a decision out of position.

I check back. I c-bet the K52 flop for fold equity and was called, which narrows Claude’s range to a lot of medium/strong hands (Kx, mid pocket pairs, some Tx/overcards and occasional sets). I didn’t improve on the T♦ turn — my A4o is essentially a busted hand against any made pair and has poor bluff-equity versus a caller. The turn is a neutral/relatively scary card for my perceived range and the pot is already 1,800 with deep stacks, so barreling here would often force a call from hands that are way ahead and fold out only the weakest holdings. Checking controls the pot, preserves stack flexibility, and keeps bluffs in Claude’s range so I can make a more informed decision on the river (call small/medium bets with Ace-high, fold to large bets or raises, consider thin value only if I improve). This is the prudent, balanced line after my flop bet was called.

Betting the river for value with pair of tens. Minni's bet-check-check pattern is a classic weakness tell. The flop bet followed by checks on turn and river suggests Minni either missed a draw or has a marginal hand. My pair of tens with Jack kicker is likely ahead of most of Minni's checking range. A 600 chip bet is appropriately sized to extract value from weaker hands while not overcommitting with a marginal made hand. Deep stacks support this value extraction approach.

Claude called my flop c‑bet and checked the turn, which weights his range toward a lot of made hands (Kx, mid pocket pairs, some tens) and only occasional bluffs. His river shot of 600 into ~1800 is a small, 1/3‑pot bet — it gets called by worse aces and smaller pairs and is also the size a player would use for thin value. I hold A♦4♥: the ace is a decent blocker and the best non‑paired showdown hand, but I’m behind any pair. The price is right — I’m being asked 600 to win 2400 (≈20% equity needed). Folding is too exploitable here and a raise is thin and likely to be called or re‑raised by value hands. I’ll call to realize my showdown equity and avoid folding out bluffs.

Pocket 77 is a premium hand in early-to-mid tournament play. On the button with positional advantage, I should raise to apply pressure and take control. A standard 3.5x raise to 350 builds the pot, creates fold equity with the blinds, and allows me to play profitably postflop with position if called. This is a strong value raise with excellent implied odds.

I am in the Small Blind with 9s 3c, which is a very weak starting hand. Claude has raised from the button, and with Minni still to act in the Big Blind, this is an easy fold as the hand has very little equity or playability.

Claude opened to 350 from the button and I’m in the big blind with K♦7♥. I’m getting 250 to call into a 500 pot (making it ~750 after my call) which requires ~33% equity — K7o has roughly that versus a wide button opening range and is worth defending. With deep stacks and many hands left, I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal hand by 3-betting; a flat-call keeps pot control and lets me realize equity or fold to strong action postflop. Plan: call to 350 total, evaluate flop texture (continue with a king or strong draws, fold to heavy aggression on dry boards).

Claude opened from the button and I defended with K♦7♥. The flop (A♣ 9♣ J♥) is an Ace-high with two clubs — I have only king-high with no club or meaningful draw. Claude’s button range contains a lot of Aces, broadways and club draws, so I'm often behind and have little fold equity. With deep stacks and a marginal hand I should control the pot: check and see how he plays the turn. Plan: check-fold to a large, committed c-bet; I can call a very small probe if it looks like a pure bluff, otherwise give up and re-evaluate on a safe turn (king or blank).

I hold pocket sevens (7s 7d) on a flop of Ac 9c Jh. This is an underpair to the board, making my hand vulnerable but potentially still ahead in the hand. Minni checked to me after calling pre-flop, which suggests either a mediocre hand, a draw, or slow-play. My position as button and Minni's passive check indicates an opportunity for a continuation bet. A bet of 400 (roughly 53% of pot) applies pressure, takes control of the action, and either wins the pot immediately or gains valuable information about Minni's holding. If called, I can reassess on the turn. If Minni has an underpair or missed entirely, my bet wins. If Minni has an ace or strong hand, I want to find out now rather than face more aggression later. This is a standard value/pressure bet in position.

Flop: A♣ 9♣ J♥. I hold K♦7♥ — just king-high with no club or straight draw, so I’m usually behind to the button’s raising/c-betting range (Ax, broadways, club draws). Claude’s bet 400 into a 1150 pot is relatively small (a probe bet). Calling 400 to see the turn is cheap (calling makes the pot ~1550, ~26% immediate pot odds) and preserves pot control with a marginal hand. I won’t fold away a cheap chance to improve to top pair or get more info, but I won’t inflate the pot by raising with almost no equity. Plan: call the 400, and fold to any large turn shove/strong barrel unless I pair a King or pick up a meaningful draw.

Check. I have K♦7♥ — only king-high with no club or straight draw. Claude’s line (button open + small flop c-bet) is weighted toward Ax, broadways and club draws, and the turn pairing an Ace further crushes my equity and makes pure bluffs less likely. With the pot at 1,550 and deep stacks, I want pot control and to avoid getting committed with a marginal hand. I’ll check and fold to any meaningful bet; only consider calling a very tiny probe (small enough to give correct pot odds), but otherwise give up.

The turn Ah is a critical card that significantly weakens my hand. I hold 77, which is now an underpair to a two-ace board. Given Minni's check-call line on the flop followed by another turn check, they likely hold either a weak ace (which beats my hand), a mid-pair (which is a coin flip at best), or a missed draw. The Ah significantly increases the likelihood Minni has an ace in their holding, making additional aggression here unwise. By checking, I practice proper pot control: I've already invested 400 on the flop and can see the river card cheaply before committing further. If Minni shows strength on the river, I can make a more informed decision with complete information. This approach minimizes losses against Minni's likely-ahead holdings while preserving chips for more favorable spots.

Board: A♣ 9♣ J♥ A♥ Q♠. I hold K♦7♥ — no pair, no club, no straight. Claude opened and c-bet the flop (range heavy in Ax, broadways, Jx and club draws) and then checked the turn; his line and the paired aces on board make it very likely he has at least showdown-value hands that won’t fold to a river bluff. Pot is 1,550 and I have virtually no showdown equity or fold equity, so betting is an unnecessarily high-variance bluff with poor expected value. With deep stacks and many hands left, preserve chips: check and accept the likely loss at showdown. If Claude now bets a sizable amount on the river I will fold — I won’t call for thin value with K-high.

I hold pocket sevens (7s 7d) on a final board of Ac 9c Jh Ah Qs. This is the river with no more cards to come. My pair of sevens is an underpair to both the board's aces and vulnerable to most hands Minni would have called the flop with. Minni's line has been: call flop bet (400), check turn, check river. This sequence suggests either a weak/missed draw or a cautious hand that doesn't want to commit more chips. Betting here creates a dilemma: if Minni folds, I win the 1550 pot but could have won at showdown; if Minni calls, I'm almost certainly crushed by an ace, mid-pair, or queen. The river check is a showdown offer—Minni is done committing chips. Value betting is counterproductive when I'm likely behind. Checking allows me to see the showdown cheaply and win against any complete misses or weak hands Minni holds, while minimizing losses against stronger holdings. This is proper pot control on the river with a marginal hand.

I am on the button with K7o in a 3-handed game. This is a standard opening hand to put pressure on the blinds. Raising to 300 allows me to potentially take the pot down pre-flop or play the hand in position against either the small or big blind. Given the current stack depths and the tendency of both opponents to defend, a standard 3x open is appropriate here.

I have T♠4♦ in the small blind facing a button raise to 300 in a 3-handed game. This is a weak, unsuited hand with very poor post‑flop playability and is frequently dominated by the opener’s range. Calling 250 into the 450 pot requires roughly 35–36% equity to be profitable up front, which T4o won’t provide versus a typical BTN/BB raising range, and I’ll be out of position against both opponents. A meaningful 3‑bet would need to be polarized and sized larger to work as a bluff — T4o isn’t a hand to include in that range. With deep stacks but a poor holding and the big blind still to act (could 3‑bet or limp), the right play is to conserve chips for better spots. Fold.

With K5o (King-Five offsuit) in the big blind facing Flash's button raise, I'm in a difficult spot. While I'm getting 2.25:1 odds on a 200 chip call, K5o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position. Even though my 17,500 chip stack is healthy and I have many hands remaining (hand 86 of 150), this is not a profitable spot to defend. King-Five offsuit will miss the flop approximately 80% of the time and will be difficult to play post-flop from out of position. Flash has shown selective but reasonable button raising patterns, so his range is likely ahead of my holdings. I should preserve my chips for stronger spots where I have better equity and positional advantage. This is a disciplined fold.

I’m on the button with 9♠8♥ (off-suit, connected) in a 3-handed pot with blinds 50/100 (pot = 150, to call = 100).
