This is a good spot to open with a raise: I have position versus both blinds and a hand with good playability postflop (connectivity for straights, decent blockers). Claude has shown aggression in past hands and Flash has folded/defended selectively, so a small button raise will often pick up the blinds or keep ranges wide if called. I’ll make the minimum standard open to apply pressure without bloating the pot with a speculative offsuit connector — allows me to continue comfortably in position or fold to a significant 3-bet. If I face a heavy 3-bet I will likely fold; versus single callers I’ll play postflop aggressively from position.

Q4o is a weak hand from the small blind. While I have decent stack position with 17450 chips, calling 150 more out of position against Minni's raise is not profitable. Queen-four offsuit plays poorly post-flop and has marginal win probability. Additionally, Flash in the big blind could still reraise, putting me in an awkward spot. I should preserve my chip lead and fold weak hands, waiting for premium holdings to leverage my stack size. This is a standard fold.

I am in the Big Blind with King-Eight offsuit. Minni, on the button, has made a minimum raise to 200. With Claude folding in the Small Blind, I only need to call 100 more to see the flop. The pot is currently 350, giving me 3.5-to-1 odds on a call. K8o is a standard hand to defend against a wide button opening range, especially given the attractive pot odds and my position. I will call to see the flop.

On a flop of Ace-King-Nine with two hearts, I have flopped middle pair (Kings). This is a solid but vulnerable hand, as there is an overcard (the Ace) and many potential draws (flush and straight draws). In the Big Blind, standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) to see how they react. I intend to check-call a reasonable bet to evaluate the turn, as I have a piece of the board but cannot afford to play a massive pot out of position against a potentially stronger range.

I raised preflop and in position I’ve flopped middle pair (9♠8♠ -> pair of 9s with a weak 8 kicker) on K♥9♣A♥. Flash checked from the big blind, which often indicates a wide, sometimes weak calling range (missed broadways, small pairs, A‑x that slowplays, or floats). A half‑pot c-bet (~225 into 450) applies pressure to the missed parts of his range, gets value from worse pairs/draws, and protects my hand without bloating the pot with a marginal holding. Plan: call or fold turn as appropriate — fold to a large raise (represents A/K/sets), call single bets and re-evaluate if the turn improves my hand or the texture becomes safer.

I have flopped middle pair (Kings) on a board with an Ace. My opponent, Minni, has made a continuation bet of 225 into a 450 pot after raising from the button. While the Ace is a scary overcard, my hand is too strong to fold to a single bet, as Minni could be betting with a wide range of hands, including weaker Kings, draws (like hearts or a gutshot), or air. The pot odds are 3-to-1, meaning I only need about 25% equity to make a profitable call. I will call to see how the turn develops, keeping the pot manageable while I have a medium-strength showdown hand.

On the turn, the 8h significantly improves my hand to two pair (Kings and Eights). However, the board is now quite coordinated as the third heart has landed, completing potential flushes. Given I am out of position against Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent play. If Minni has a flush or a set of nines, I want to keep the pot manageable. If she has a strong Ace or is semi-bluffing with a single heart or a straight draw, checking allows her to continue betting, which provides me with value and the opportunity to see how she reacts to the board texture. My two pair is far too strong to fold to a standard bet, but leading out might fold out her bluffs or get raised by better hands.

I have 9♥8♠ and the turn (8♥) gives me two pair, 9s and 8s, on K♥ 9♣ A♥ 8♥. Heads-up vs Flash who flat-called my flop bet, his calling range is wide (lots of Ax, Kx, medium pairs and some draws). Most of those hands are behind my two pair (single A/K pairs, small pocket pairs, many draws). Hands that currently beat me (A9, K9, 99, 88, or sets) are relatively infrequent. Betting for value and protection is the right line — I want to charge any pair/draws and deny a free river card. I’ll size to ~2/3 pot to extract value from worse hands while making draws pay. Plan: if he folds I take it down; if he calls I re‑evaluate on the river and continue to extract value on safe rivers; if he check‑raises big, I’ll put him on a very strong holding (sets/two‑pair A9/K9) and fold to a large, polarized raise.

I have improved to two pair (Kings and Eights) on a board that now features three hearts, making a flush a distinct possibility for my opponent. While my hand is very strong, the presence of the Ace and the potential flush means I should proceed with caution. Minni's bet of 600 into a 900 pot (2/3 pot) is substantial. Raising here would likely only get called by hands that beat me, such as a completed flush, a set, or a better two pair (like Ace-Nine or Ace-King). By calling, I keep the pot manageable, control the size of my potential loss if I am behind, and keep Minni's range wide, allowing her to continue betting with bluffs or weaker hands on the river. I will call and re-evaluate on the final street.

The river brings a fourth heart to the board, making any single heart a flush. My two pair (Kings and Eights) is now very weak relative to the board texture. Betting would likely only result in being called by a flush or a better hand, and I cannot realistically represent a flush myself without a heart in hand. I will check to see how Minni acts and hope to reach a cheap showdown if she doesn't have a heart.

River brings 3♥, giving me 9♥8♠ on K♥ 9♣ A♥ 8♥ 3♥. I’ve turned two pair into a 5‑card heart flush (A K 9 8 3 hearts). That’s strong but not the nut: any single heart T♥/J♥/Q♥ in Flash’s hand now beats me. He checked the river after calling my turn bet — his line is consistent with a wide range (A‑x, K‑x, pocket 9s, some two‑heart combos that already had a flush) but also with medium strength hands that will call a small value bet rather than bet themselves.

Given the texture I want to extract value from Ax/Kx/9x and other medium pairs that will likely call a modest bet, while not pricing myself in against the few higher‑heart combos that dominate me. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) accomplishes that: it gets called by worse hands, makes bluffs pay, and avoids folding out the very hands I want to extract from. If Flash check‑raises big, I’ll assign him very strong holdings (better flush/full house) and fold.

Action: make a small value bet of 700 (≈1/3 pot) to maximize value against weaker holdings while keeping worse flushes and bluffs in the decision.

On a board with four hearts, any single heart in the opponent's hand completes a flush. Although I have Two Pair (Kings and Eights), this hand is extremely vulnerable on such a coordinated board. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, raising from the button pre-flop and betting both the flop and the turn. Her 600 bet on the turn (when the third heart appeared) and her 700 bet on the river (when the fourth heart appeared) suggest she either already had a flush or has improved to one. Given that I do not hold a heart myself, I have no 'blockers' to her potential flushes. While the pot odds of 4-to-1 are somewhat tempting, a call here would only be profitable if she were bluffing or value-betting a worse two pair/top pair without a heart, which is unlikely given the board texture and her betting line. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Calling 100 from the button with QhTs. Strong positional advantage allows me to play post-flop with control. QT is solid enough to contest the pot, and the minimal cost allows flexibility. Position and hand strength make this the optimal play against these aggressive opponents.

I am in the Small Blind with Qd 2c. The Button (Claude) has limped for 100. With a very weak hand like Q2 offsuit, there is no value in raising or even calling to play out of position against a player who has been relatively active. Folding here is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I'm in the big blind (100 posted) with Q♣5♠ facing a single limp from Claude. Q5o is a marginal, often-dominated hand out of position and doesn't warrant isolating with a small raise (the min-raise to 200 is tiny and will simply get called). With stacks deep and the pot only 250, it's better to see a flop cheaply and keep the pot small. Plan: check and evaluate the flop — continue only if I hit a Q/5 or pick up a strong draw; fold to significant aggression on dry boards.

I missed this dry flop with Q♣5♠ — no pair and only a backdoor club. Claude limped preflop, so his range is wide but contains many pairs and hands that will call a probe. Out of position on a dry 7♣-9♠-4♦ board I don’t want to build the pot with air. Checking controls the pot, keeps the pot small, and lets me fold to meaningful aggression. Plan: check and see how Claude reacts — call very small probes to realize equity but fold to medium/large bets; if he checks back I’ll take a free turn to reassess and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw.

Betting 150 (60% of pot) as a standard c-bet continuation bet. I have a strong draw with 8 outs (any K or 6 makes a straight), plus Queen-high. Minni's check indicates weakness, and from the button with position, I should take control of the pot. This bet size applies pressure while keeping the pot manageable, allowing me to play profitably postflop with both my drawing equity and overcard outs.

Claude limped preflop (wide, weak range) and now makes a small probe bet (150 into a 400 pot). I missed this dry 7♣-9♠-4♦ flop with Q♣5♠ — no pair and no realistic flush (with one club in hand and only two cards to come a nut flush is impossible). I have ~6 clean outs (three Qs, three 5s) to hit a pair by the river ≈24% equity. Pot odds: calling 150 to potentially win ~700 means I need ≈21% equity, so a call is +EV. Plan: call to realize equity and see a turn; fold to significant turn aggression if I don’t improve, and extract value if I pair the Q or 5 or pick up a useful draw. Raising here would be unnecessary and thin out of position versus a wide limp-call range.

Claude limp-called preflop and fired a small probe (150) on the flop — a wide, weak range. I called the flop to realize equity with Qc5s. The turn A♠ bricks my backdoor club (no realistic draw now) and I still have only ~6 clean outs (~24% to pair by the river). Pot is 550 and stacks are deep (I have 12,200). Out of position with a marginal hand and an Ace on board, I want to control the pot and avoid bloating it with air or getting exploited by a turn lead. Checking keeps the pot manageable, lets me fold cheaply to real aggression, and allows me to call small turn bets to realize equity. Plan: check — if Claude bets small (about ≤1/3 pot ≈180–200) I’ll call to see the river; fold to large turn bets (>~1/2 pot) and re-evaluate on the river if I pair or see a clear bluffing spot.

Checking the turn with Queen-high after the Ace comes. Minni has shown weakness by checking twice, but the Ace is a scary card that likely helped her range or improves her position. With only a gutshot as equity and minimal hand strength, betting risks building a pot with the worst hand. Checking preserves chips, keeps the pot manageable, and allows me to evaluate Minni's river action. This is solid bankroll management with a marginal hand in position.

I have Q♣5♠ with no pair on 7♣-9♠-4♦-A♠-8♠. Claude limped pre, made a small flop probe (150) and then checked the turn — a line consistent with a wide/weak range (air, small pairs, or missed draws). I have essentially no showdown value, so checking is passive and will lose most showdowns. A river bluff is appropriate to pick up the pot against his weak checking line. I’ll size to about half-pot (275): big enough to fold many unpaired hands and some weak pairs but small relative to my stack if he calls or raises. If Claude raises, I’ll release - I don’t want to get hero-called or raised off my bluff. Bet 275 to try to take it down now.

Folding Queen-high on the river. Minni's action pattern is clear: she checked the turn despite the Ace (showing weakness), then raised after the 8 completed the board. The river 8 is a particularly dangerous card that completes straights, creates two-pair combinations, and overall strengthens her range significantly. With three spades on board, flush possibilities are real. Queen-high requires approximately 25% equity to call, but my hand is likely beaten by two pair, flushes, or straights in her range. The pot odds (3:1) don't justify calling a river raise from a player who showed weakness on the turn but found aggression after the board completed. This is a clear fold.

On the button in a three-handed game, King-Ten offsuit is a strong hand that merits an opening raise. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my positional advantage over the blinds.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the small blind with 5♠9♣ — a weak, offsuit, poorly-connected hand that’s often dominated by a BTN opening range. Claude (BB) is still to act, so calling 250 into a 450 pot will likely create a multiway pot and leave me out of position postflop. Calling requires ~36% equity vs the open, which 9‑5o rarely has; 3‑betting to 500 as a bluff is also unattractive because I have no meaningful blockers or postflop playability. With 127 BBs effective I can wait for a better spot — fold and preserve stack.

I'm in the Big Blind with TcJs (Ten-Jack offsuit), which is a broadway hand with decent playability. Flash raised to 300 from the button, and Minni folded from the small blind, leaving this as a heads-up situation.

Key factors:
1. Pot odds: I'm getting approximately 2.25:1 to call 200 to win 450, which is favorable
2. Hand strength: TcJs has decent showdown value and can make straights; it's marginal but reasonable for calling position
3. Position: After the flop, I'll be in position against Flash, allowing me to play more accurately post-flop
4. Stack sizes: I have a healthy 17100 chips with no desperation, so I can outplay post-flop
5. Flash's tendencies: Flash has shown aggression throughout the tournament, but I have position to counter-attack

Calling allows me to see a flop in a heads-up spot with reasonable equity. If I flop well, I can potentially win the pot with aggression. If the flop is dry, I can fold to a continuation bet. This is preferable to folding a hand that has reasonable value, or raising with a marginal holding where I'm not getting value from worse hands.

I have TcJs on a flop of 8c 9h 6c in a heads-up pot. This is an interesting spot—I have a strong drawing hand with an open-ended straight draw (K or 7 completes a straight) plus two unpaired overcards, giving me roughly 8 outs to the straight plus additional equity. However, I currently have no made hand and am first to act post-flop. Checking is the correct play here for several reasons: (1) My hand has significant equity as a draw but no made strength, so I want to take a free card; (2) Flash just raised pre-flop to 300 from the button and will likely continuation bet, allowing me to respond based on their action and bet size; (3) Being out of position, checking keeps my range balanced and prevents me from overcommitting with a draw that, while profitable to see another card, may not warrant aggressive action immediately; (4) Flash's aggressive style means they'll likely put pressure on me on the flop, but I can call a reasonable bet with my drawing equity. This preserves chips while maintaining position information for future streets.

The flop of 8-9-6 is highly coordinated and significantly favors the Big Blind's calling range, which is likely to contain many middle-pair combinations, connectors like 7-8 or 6-7, and straight draws. While I have two overcards and a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a 7), I don't have a made hand yet. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, avoid a potential check-raise on a board that hits my opponent's range, and ensure I see a free turn card to realize my equity.

I hold Tc Js on a board of 8c 9h 6c As, heads-up against Flash in a 650-chip pot. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to K or 7) and two overcards, giving me roughly 32% equity. I'm out of position and my hand has no current made strength. Flash checked the flop, suggesting a moderate hand rather than a premium holding. Checking is correct here: it preserves chips with a draw that benefits from seeing another card, allows me to respond to Flash's action, and avoids overcommitting with speculative equity. If Flash bets, I have good pot odds to call with my straight draw; if they check, I see the river for free.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a classic scare card that significantly favors my range as the pre-flop raiser. After the flop checked through, Claude has checked the turn as well, showing a high probability of weakness. By betting now, I can represent the Ace and likely force a fold from his middle pairs (8x, 9x) or straight draws that might have called a flop bet. Even if called, I still have 4 outs to the nut straight (any 7) and potential overcard value with the King. I will bet 350 into the 650 pot to maximize my fold equity.

Heads-up vs Flash on the turn with Tc Js on a board of 8c 9h 6c As. I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs to K or 7) with roughly 32% equity against Flash's likely range. Pot odds: I'm getting 2.85:1 (1000 chips to call 350) which exceeds the ~2:1 odds needed to break even on an OESD. Flash's line (checked flop, bet turn after the Ace) suggests either a medium-strength hand or a hand that hit the Ace, but given stack sizes and position, calling is justified. One card to come means my draw has substantial equity, and if I miss on the river, I can reassess based on Flash's action. The pot is only 1000 at this point, so I'm not overcommitting relative to my 16900 stack.

River decision with Tc Js on 8c 9h 6c As 4c. I'm checking down to showdown with T-high. The river 4c was a blank that didn't complete my straight draw (needed K or 7). Flash's action progression (checked flop, bet turn after the Ace, now checked the river) suggests a medium-strength hand or weakness. With no made hand and only T-high, I have no credible bluffs and very little showdown value. Betting would be speculatively trying to win with nothing. Checking allows me to reach showdown against Flash's likely hand, which is standard practice on the river when out of position with a draw that missed. The pot of 1350 is modest relative to my 16550 stack, and there's no strategic advantage to forcing action with a busted draw.

The river is the 4 of clubs, which completes potential flush draws and straights. I represented the Ace on the turn with a bet of 350, but Claude called, suggesting he has a piece of the coordinated board (like a pair of 8s or 9s) or a draw that may have just completed. Claude has shown a tendency to be a sticky player in previous hands, calling down significantly. Since my King-high has no showdown value but the probability of a bluff succeeding against a calling station on this board is low, I will check back to minimize further losses.

Q♣8♣ on the button is a clear open-raising hand: suited, decent high-card, good postflop playability and lots of fold equity from late position. Limping here surrenders initiative and invites multiway pots; the min (200) is too small and often just gets called. With deep stacks (~127 BB) and two blinds to steal, I’ll open to a standard 3xBB sizing to 300 total. This gives good fold equity, keeps my range wide and playable, and preserves stack to play postflop. Plan: call small 3-bets in position or fold to large 3/4-bets; if called see flop and continue selectively (c-bet when I have equity or good texture, check/fold or float cautiously otherwise).

I hold pocket Kings (KK), one of the strongest possible hands in poker. From the small blind, I should definitely be aggressive here. Minni's raise to 300 from the button appears to be a standard raise (looking at the history, Minni raises frequently from late position). I have good stack depth at 16,500 chips. A raise to 900 (approximately 3x Minni's raise) accomplishes several goals: (1) builds the pot with a premium hand, (2) applies pressure to both opponents, (3) tests the strength of Minni's hand, and (4) gives me initiative heading to the flop. This sizing is standard and doesn't commit me too heavily, but demonstrates strength and discourages weak calls. With KK, I want to play a big pot, and this raise accomplishes that objective.

I am holding 6s 3s in the big blind. There has been an open-raise to 300 from the button (Minni) and a 3-bet to 900 from the small blind (Claude). While my hand is suited, it is extremely weak and I would be playing out of position against two aggressive opponents in a bloated pot. There is no reason to risk chips with such a marginal hand when facing significant pre-flop strength.

I opened to 300 on the button with Q♣8♣ and Claude (SB) 3‑bet to 900, so I need to call 600 more. With deep stacks (~124bb) and position, Q8s is a clear defend — good postflop playability and useful blockers. A 4‑bet here would be a polarized, high‑variance move I don’t want to make with this hand; folding surrenders too much equity. I’ll call to keep the pot manageable, see a flop in position, and play based on texture (c‑bet or stab when I have equity/draws; check/float or fold when I don’t).

Betting 1,200 with pocket Kings on a dry 7-2-8 board. This flop is favorable for my range after the pre-flop 3-bet. The bet (60% of pot) accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) extracts value from weaker pairs like QQ-TT and drawing hands like AK/AQ, (2) protects against potential draws if Minni has diamonds, (3) maintains consistent aggression post-raise, and (4) keeps reasonable stack depth (~14,450 remaining) for subsequent streets. Only AA, 77, or 88 beat me, which are relatively unlikely in Minni's calling range given the action. This sizing is standard and well-balanced.

I opened to 300 preflop and called Claude’s 3‑bet — on 7♥‑2♦‑8♦ I have Q♣8♣: middle pair with a good kicker and I’m in position. Claude’s 1,200 continuation bet represents strength but will include a lot of hands I beat or have decent equity against (floats, some 8x, weaker pairs, and bluffs), and there are two diamonds on board so raising to 2,400 would mainly be a protection raise that bloats the pot and prices out the very hands I want to extract value from. Calling 1,200 into a 3,100 pot requires ~28% equity; my hand has roughly that vs his c‑bet/3‑bet range, so I’ll call to realize equity and use position to control the pot on the turn. Plan: if a diamond hits and he barrels big I’ll be very cautious; on blank turns I’ll look to get value or pot‑control depending on his action.

I hold pocket Kings, which is an extremely strong hand that currently beats nearly all of Minni's possible holdings on this 7-2-8-T board. Analysis of Minni's range: Given her button raise to 300 and call of my 900 3-bet, then her flop call against my 1,200 bet, she likely holds medium pairs (QQ-TT), broadway cards (AK, AQ, KQ), or possibly a set (77/88, though less likely given the action). The Ten of diamonds on the turn is a relatively neutral card for our ranges - it doesn't dramatically improve Minni's likely holdings, though it does add some flush draw connectivity if she holds diamond holdings.

My strategy: Continue aggressive betting for value. With KK and over $14K in chips remaining, I should build the pot while my hand is strong. A disciplined bet of 2,000 (approximately 47% pot) accomplishes several objectives: (1) extracts value from weaker pairs and drawing hands, (2) maintains stack depth (~12,450 remaining) for the river, (3) applies pressure that may force suboptimal decisions, and (4) sets up a river decision with clean information about Minni's hand strength. This sizing is standard for continuation betting and doesn't overcommit with such a premium holding.

Minni's effective stack is only 10,600, meaning we're approaching a critical juncture where commitment becomes inevitable. Betting now with the best hand is the mathematically and strategically correct play.

Turn brings the T♦, the third diamond on board. Claude 3‑betted preflop, barreled the flop and now barrels a second time — a line that heavily favors made hands and completed diamond flushes (and also includes strong T/sets/two‑pair). The pot is 6,300 and it costs 2,000 to call, so I need roughly 24% equity to continue. My Q♣8♣ is just middle pair with no diamond blocker and has very little equity vs a range that contains many diamonds and stronger pairs. Raising as a bluff here is high variance and unlikely to fold the hands I’m behind to. I’ll fold and conserve chips for a better spot.

I'm holding 6d2s (six-deuce offsuit), which is the worst starting hand in poker. Despite being on the button with the largest stack at the table, this hand has no playable potential. The risk-reward simply doesn't justify entering the pot. Even accounting for my positional advantage and stack advantage over the blinds, 6-2 has minimal win rate and would create difficult postflop situations. Folding preserves equity for better opportunities ahead. With 59 hands remaining in the tournament, I should maintain disciplined hand selection and avoid marginal spots with garbage hands.

I am in the Small Blind with 8h 3h. Claude (Button) has folded, leaving only me and Minni (Big Blind). While 83s has some flush potential, it is a very weak hand overall. Minni has been an active and aggressive player throughout the tournament, frequently defending her blinds and playing aggressively post-flop. Given that I have a comfortable stack and there are still 60 hands remaining, there is no need to risk chips with a bottom-tier hand out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I'm on the Button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a 3-handed game, any Ace on the Button is a strong opening hand. I'll make a standard 3x raise to 300 to take control of the pot and put pressure on the blinds. My stack is healthy at over 100 big blinds, allowing me to play post-flop if called.

9♣2♦ is essentially the worst non-folding hand: offsuit, disconnected and with almost no postflop playability. Flash opened to 300 from the button; I’m the SB and already committed 50, so calling 250 into the 450 pot would require ~250/(450+250)=35.7% equity to break even. Versus a button open-range my actual equity is well below that and I’ll be out of position for the whole hand — plus Claude (BB) still gets to act and can squeeze or call. With a healthy 106 BB stack there’s no reason to gamble out of position with junk. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot.

Pocket kings is a premium starting hand (only behind AA). Flash raised to 300 from the button, which is a positional play that could represent a wide range. Minni folded from the small blind, leaving heads-up against Flash.

With KK and a significant chip lead (18,650 vs 10,300), I should apply aggressive pressure to extract value and win the pot now or get it in with a strong hand. A raise to 800 is approximately 2.7x Flash's raise, which is standard for premium pairs and achieves several goals:

1. Narrows Flash's range - differentiates my hand from a call
2. Builds the pot while maintaining fold equity - many hands will fold
3. Allows for strong value if Flash continues with weaker holdings
4. Puts Flash in a spot where they need a strong hand to continue given their remaining stack size (10,300)

This is textbook poker with a premium starting hand in a favorable situation.

I opened for 300 on the Button with Ace-Seven offsuit, and the Big Blind (Claude) 3-bet to 800. While I have position and the pot odds are roughly 2.3:1 to call, A7o is a very marginal hand to play in a 3-bet pot. It is easily dominated by Claude's likely 3-betting range (higher Aces like AJ-AK) and does not play well post-flop. Given that Claude is the chip leader and has shown significant aggression in previous hands, I will make a disciplined fold to preserve my 100BB stack for a better spot.

2♣5♠ is among the weakest starting hands — offsuit, unconnected, no backdoor flush potential or meaningful blockers. The pot is 150 and it costs 100 to play (a large price relative to the pot) into two remaining opponents (SB and BB), which makes my postflop equity poor and I’ll face likely resistance or multiway action. A min-raise to 200 as a steal is possible from the button, but against deep, somewhat active stacks and two defenders that often defend, it’s a high-variance bluff with little fold equity and poor playability if called. Preserve chips and wait for a much better spot — fold preflop.

JT suited is a strong hand from the small blind, especially in a heads-up situation against the big blind (Flash). With Minni already folded, I'm playing against only Flash who is relatively short-stacked at 10,200 chips. My stack of 19,050 is healthy and gives me excellent fold equity and position advantage post-flop. The small blind has already committed 50 chips, and a standard 2.5x raise to 250 total is appropriate to build the pot with a premium hand while maintaining good stack-to-pot ratios. This aggression is warranted given my hand strength and stack advantage.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. Claude has raised to 250 from the Small Blind. This means I only need to call 150 more to see the flop, giving me pot odds of approximately 2.3 to 1. Additionally, I will have position on Claude for the remainder of the hand. While Jack-Seven offsuit is a mediocre hand, the combination of favorable pot odds and positional advantage makes a call standard in a three-handed game.

Betting 325 on the flop with JhTh against an out-of-position Flash. I have strong equity with multiple straight and flush draws (8 straight outs + flush draws), plus overcards. Flash is short-stacked, making aggression profitable. This continuation bet applies pressure, extracts value from weaker holdings, and builds the pot with my equity advantage. The sizing is strong but not excessive given the pot size and stack depths.

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Seven offsuit. I called a pre-flop raise from Claude in the Small Blind. The flop comes King-high (Kd-8h-3d), and I have missed the board entirely with no pair and no real draw. Claude has made a continuation bet of 325 into a 500 pot. While I have position on Claude, I have no equity in this hand and calling would be a 'float' with pure air against an aggressive chip leader. Since I have nothing to work with and would be reliant on a pure bluff later, the most disciplined play is to fold and preserve my stack.

Pocket nines is a premium hand (top 5 starting hands). I'm in button position with only two opponents remaining. Standard play is to raise to define hand strength and build the pot. A 3x BB raise to 300 total is a reasonable sizing that's aggressive but not over-the-top. This gives me good positional advantage and forces both opponents to make a decision with worse hand ranges most of the time.

I have a premium hand, Ace-King suited, in the Small Blind. Claude opened from the Button to 300. AKs is a very strong hand that performs well when 3-betting, especially out of position, to gain the initiative and potentially win the pot pre-flop. A standard 3-bet size here is approximately 3 to 4 times the initial raise. I will raise to 1,000 to extract value and narrow the field.

Fold. Facing a small‑blind 3‑bet to 1000 after a button open, my A8 offsuit is a marginal defending hand — I’m likely dominated by the 3‑better’s value range (AK, AQ, big pairs) and can easily end up multi‑way if the button calls, which further destroys my equity. Calling 900 into a 1400 pot requires roughly 39% equity to break even; without a clear read that Flash is 3‑betting light (and with plenty of effective stack behind ~105bb), this call is not +EV. A minimal raise to 1700 would be an awkward small 4‑bet that bloats the pot with a medium hand and could be exploited. Better to preserve my stack and wait for a cleaner opportunity in position or with a stronger holding.

Calling Flash's re-raise with pocket nines. This is a premium hand that warrants seeing the flop at 2:1 pot odds. With position and a healthy stack, I can play this hand profitably postflop. The call allows me to gather information from the flop while maintaining flexibility in my decision-making.

I hold Ad Kd on a flop of 5c 3d Th. I was the pre-flop aggressor with a 3-bet to 1,000. This board is relatively dry, and while it doesn't hit my range perfectly, I still have two overcards and a backdoor diamond draw. Continuation betting here is standard to maintain the initiative and put pressure on Claude's range, which likely consists of medium pocket pairs, suited connectors, or other Broadway hands that missed this flop. A bet of roughly 35-40% of the pot is appropriate to fold out his weaker holdings while keeping the pot manageable if he has a hand like a 10 or a small pair.

I'm holding pocket 9s, a premium hand that's likely ahead on this dry flop (5-3-T). Flash's pre-flop aggression (raising to 1,000) and flop bet of 800 could represent a legitimate hand like TT+, AK/AQ, or could be a continuation bet with a weaker holding. My position and stack depth (18,350) allow me to play assertively here. The flop texture doesn't contain many coordinated cards that favor Flash's range. A raise to 2,000 accomplishes several goals: (1) charges any draw Flash might hold, (2) gets value from medium pairs or ace-high hands, (3) maintains fold equity against weaker holdings, (4) defines my hand strength without overcommitting. The pot odds (3,700 to call 800) strongly support staying in the hand, and raising puts me in control rather than playing passively. This aggressive play is appropriate given the premium nature of pocket 9s and my favorable position.

I hold Ace-King suited (Ad Kd) on a relatively dry flop of 5c-3d-Th. I was the pre-flop 3-bettor. Claude's raise to 2,000 on the flop after I continuation bet 800 is a show of significant strength, often representing a set (55, 33) or a strong top pair (AT). However, the pot currently stands at 4,900, and it costs me 1,200 to call. This gives me pot odds of approximately 1,200 / 6,100, which is about 19.6%. With two overcards (6 outs) and a backdoor nut flush draw, my equity against a range that includes JJ-QQ, AT, and sets is roughly 25-30%. Given these odds, calling is mathematically justified to see if I can improve on the turn, while also discouraging Claude from thinking he can easily push me off premium hands in 3-bet pots.

The turn brings the 3 of clubs, pairing the board. This card is unlikely to have changed the situation significantly, as I don't expect Claude to have many 3s in his range after calling a 3-bet pre-flop, though a set of 5s now becomes a full house. After calling his raise on the flop, I am in check-call or check-fold mode depending on his bet sizing. Leading out would be polarized and likely to be raised again by his value range (AT, JJ, QQ, 55). I will check to Claude to see if he checks behind with marginal showdown value or continues his aggression.

Flash's turn check after aggressive pre-flop and flop action is a clear weakness indicator. With pocket 9s making second pair on a board of 5c-3d-Th-3c, I have a solid holding that's likely ahead of most of Flash's checking range. Flash either missed a draw (AK/AQ), has a weak pair, or is checking a hand they don't want to invest further with. A value bet of 3200 (roughly half the current pot of 6100) extracts maximum value while avoiding overcommitment. This sizing is aggressive enough to win pots against air and draws, while being responsible enough to not overextend if Flash has made two pair or trips on the turn. My position and stack depth support this aggressive play.

I am in a difficult spot with Ace-King suited. After 3-betting pre-flop and continuation betting on a dry 5-3-T flop, Claude raised me. This raise on such a low, unconnected board is very strong, typically representing a set (55, TT) or an overpair (JJ, QQ). While I called the flop raise to see the turn, the turn card (a 3, pairing the board) doesn't improve my hand. Claude's follow-up bet of 3,200 into a 6,100 pot represents significant strength and commitment. I only have Ace-high, and even if I assume my 6 outs (Aces and Kings) are clean, I only have about 14% equity, which is well below the 25.6% pot odds I am getting. Given the high-roller tournament context and the strength shown by Claude in a 3-bet pot, I must conclude that I am beat. Folding preserves my remaining stack of 7,050 chips.

I am on the button with Jack-Ten offsuit (JTo), which is a strong opening hand in a three-handed game. My goal is to either win the blinds or play a pot in position. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to exert pressure on the blinds.

4♠9♦ (offsuit, disconnected) is a very weak hand to defend from the small blind. Pot is 450 and it costs me 250 to call (would need ≈35.7% equity). I’ll be out of position to the raiser (Button) and the Big Blind still can act (squeeze risk). Flash’s open likely contains many hands that dominate mine and my postflop playability is poor. 3-betting as a bluff is too risky here given stack dynamics and ranges. Fold and wait for a better spot.

Calling Flash's raise with K7o. Flash is short-stacked (6,750) and likely pushing a wide range from the button. Pot odds of 2.25:1 are reasonable, and my healthy stack allows me to play post-flop. K7o has adequate equity against a light pushing range and plays reasonably well with some flop development.

Continuation bet on a paired, uncoordinated flop where Flash checked. Flash's short stack (6,450 remaining) and preflop button raise suggest a wide range, and his flop check indicates weakness. K7o has decent showdown value (king-high) and should win frequently against a whiffed range. The bet applies pressure and capitalizes on his check. If he shoves, I'll have to evaluate pot odds at that point, but winning the pot now is the most likely outcome.

On a paired board of 3d-5d-3s, Claude has led out with a bet of 350 into a 650 pot after calling my pre-flop raise. My hand, Jack-Ten offsuit, has no direct connection to this board and is currently just Jack high. While it's possible Claude is bluffing or testing me with a small lead, he has shown significant aggression in recent hands (particularly Hand #94) and holds a massive chip lead. Given my mid-sized stack and the lack of equity or a clear plan for the turn if I don't improve, I choose to fold and wait for a more favorable situation rather than floating against a dominant and aggressive big stack.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed pot with K6 of diamonds — suited king with position is worth playing aggressively. Calling 100 is too passive in this spot and limping hands in 3-handed play surrenders initiative; the min-raise to 200 is undersized compared with the table’s common 300 opens. I’ll raise to a standard 300 to (1) seize position, (2) deny cheap odds to the blinds, and (3) isolate heads-up where K6s plays well postflop. Stacks are deep enough (I ~104 BB, BB ~66 BB, SB ~227 BB) to realize equity postflop, but I’ll be prepared to fold to a large 3-bet since K6s is often dominated.

5-6 offsuit is a weak hand that doesn't warrant a call from the small blind against Minni's raise to 300. While I have a healthy stack (22,750) and could theoretically play wider ranges, this particular hand has minimal equity and poor playability post-flop. Minni has shown she raises frequently from the button as a positional play, but calling with 56o would still result in negative expected value. The pot odds of 1.8:1 don't justify playing this weak holding. Better to fold and preserve chips for stronger opportunities.

I am in the Big Blind holding Qd 7h. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and Claude has folded. While I am getting decent pot odds to call (200 to win 450), Q7o is a weak hand that plays poorly out of position, especially against an active opponent like Minni. Given my current stack size and the stage of the tournament, there is no need to make a marginal defense here. I will fold and wait for a better spot.
Calling 100 from the button with Ad7d. Strong position advantage, good pot odds (1.5:1), and solid hand with flush draw potential. Healthy stack allows for optimal post-flop play against the blinds.

I am in the Small Blind with 3d 2s, one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Claude (Button) has limped in for 100. Even though it's only 50 more chips to call and see a flop, 32o is mathematically very poor and plays terribly post-flop. Given I am out of position against both Claude and Minni, and my stack is significantly smaller than Claude's, I should conserve my chips and avoid entering a pot with trash. I will fold.

I’m in the big blind with K♦6♦ (offsuit K6). Facing a lone limp from the button, I want to seize the initiative and isolate Claude’s wide limping range. A standard raise to 300 (total) applies pressure, gets value from worse Kx and medium pairs, and folds out speculative hands; the size is small relative to both stacks so it keeps postflop play manageable. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate (K6o is marginal vs a large reraise); if he calls I’ll play the flop aggressively on favorable textures.

Calling 200 more (300 total to match Minni's raise). Position on the button combined with excellent pot odds (2.25:1) makes this a profitable play. Ad7d has strong post-flop playability with a flush draw, pair potential, and ace-high value. Minni's aggressive pattern from the blinds suggests a wide opening range, and my positional advantage post-flop will be valuable for decision-making on all streets.

I was the preflop aggressor with K♥6♦ and the flop is 8♣-5♣-9♦. I have no pair and only a gutshot to a 7 plus pair outs (roughly 10 outs total: 3 Ks, 3 sixes, 4 sevens — ~21% to hit on the turn, ~38% by river). Claude’s limp-call preflop indicates a fairly wide range (broadways, small pairs, suited connectors, clubs). This texture is a good spot for a continuation bet to fold out unpaired overcards and charge club draws while keeping the pot manageable. I’ll make a modest c-bet of 250 into the 650 pot (~38%) to get some fold equity but preserve postflop flexibility. If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn and will fold to significant aggression unless I improve. If he check-raises big I’ll respect the raise and likely fold K6o OOP.

Calling 250 into a 900 pot with Ad7d on a 8c5c9d flop. This is a premium spot with ~17 live outs (OESD + flush draw) giving me approximately 40-45% equity. The 3.6:1 pot odds are excellent for my hand strength. Position on the button provides significant advantage for turn/river decisions. With a healthy stack of 22,450, calling allows optimal play of this strong draw while maintaining flexibility on future streets. Minni's aggression suggests a wide range, but my outs and positional advantage make this a clear mathematical call.

I have K6o with no pair on the 8c-5c-9d-4s runout. I was the preflop aggressor and c-bet the flop; Claude called, which keeps a fairly sticky range (pairs like 9/8/5, small pocket pairs, some club draws and broadway overcards). On the turn I have only 10 outs to improve to a pair or the 7-straight (3 Ks + 3 sixes + 4 sevens) — ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit the river. The pot is 1,150 and I face no bet, so a pure bluff here would need >~26% fold equity to be +EV; given Claude’s flop call and general tendency to call down wide, I expect him to call a modest turn bet too often. With very limited showdown equity and a single card to come, the least costly, highest EV line is to check and see the river. If Claude bets the river I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and the card (fold most medium/large bets with no improvement, call small bluffs/catchers; value-bet thinly if I pair the K/6 or hit the straight). For now: check and control the pot.

Minni showed weakness with a turn check after aggressive pre-flop and flop play. My Ad7d has excellent draw equity (~13 outs, ~28%) with both flush and straight possibilities (5-6-7-8-9 and flush draws). Betting ~400 exploits Minni's weakness, wins the pot against marginal holdings, and builds value when called. Button position + Minni's shorter stack (10,050) makes them fold-prone. This is a high-EV spot where aggression is justified both for fold equity and maintaining proper pot odds on future streets.

I checked the turn with K♣6♦ (Kh6d) to see a river because I only have ~10 clean outs (3 Ks + 3 sixes + 4 sevens ≈ 10/46 ≈ 21.7%). Claude’s small turn bet (400 into a 1,150 pot → pot becomes 1,550) gives me ~25.8% break-even equity (400/1,550). My pure raw equity is slightly below that, but his line (limp-call preflop, call on flop, small bet on the turn) indicates a wide, often-medium-strength range that includes missed club draws and many bluffs/underpairs. Raising here would bloat the pot OOP with a marginal hand and isn’t credible enough to get folds from better made hands; folding now is too weak given the cheap price to see one more card and the likelihood of catching some clean outs. I’ll call the 400 to realize equity and re-evaluate on the river — fold to sizable river bets if I don’t improve, value/raise thinly if I pair/complete the straight or improve to a good top pair.

I have K♥6♦ and missed — the river (8♣ 5♣ 9♦ 4♠ A♣) completes a lot of Claude’s reasonable continuation-range: any A‑x now pairs up and any two clubs in his hand now make a flush (three clubs on board). Claude limped, called my flop c‑bet and then bet the turn when checked to — a line consistent with a wide but often medium‑strength range (pairs, A‑x, and club draws). I have almost no showdown value and very poor blockers to the hands that now beat me, so bluffing is unlikely to fold enough of his calling range to be +EV. Pot control is the best line: check and give up to meaningful river aggression, only calling tiny bluffs. I’ll check and preserve chips rather than bloat the pot with K‑high.

River action with Ad7d on 8c5c9d4sAc. I have top pair with the best possible kicker (pair of Aces with 7). Minni has checked on both the turn and river, indicating weakness despite their aggressive pre-flop and flop play. Their check pattern suggests they either missed their draw, have a mediocre hand they're not willing to bet, or possibly have a weak pair. Given my hand strength and Minni's demonstrated weakness, checking is optimal here. A check accomplishes multiple objectives: (1) secures the showdown with the strongest hand in most scenarios, (2) avoids potential check-raises or value-bluffs from Minni, (3) protects my stack given the escalating blind levels (we're 97 of 150 hands). Betting risks getting check-raised or losing value if Minni folds immediately. With a strong hand and opponent weakness clearly indicated, taking the showdown with top pair is the highest-EV play.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven offsuit. In a three-handed game, this is a strong opening hand. Opening for a standard 3x raise is the appropriate move here to put pressure on the blinds and take control of the pot early in the hand, leveraging my position for the post-flop play.

I’m in the small blind with Ah4c facing a button open to 300. I’ve already posted 50, so it’s 250 to call (300 total). A4o has reasonable equity vs a wide button opening range and the Ace blocker is helpful. 3-betting with this marginal hand is thin — Claude (BB) is deep and likely to defend, which would force me to play a bigger pot out of position. Folding is too tight given the small price to see a flop. I’ll call 300 total to see the flop and proceed cautiously postflop: continue with top-pair or good draws, fold to significant aggression or large 3-bets.

I'm holding 2s5h, which is one of the worst possible starting hands in poker. Despite Flash being short-stacked with only 6,300 chips and the relatively small 200 to call, this hand has virtually no value. Deuces and fives are difficult to play postflop - they won't make strong pairs often, and even when they do, they're easily dominated. The pot odds of 3.5:1 (calling 200 into 700) don't compensate for the poor equity of this hand against Flash's raising range (which likely includes many hands that dominate 2-5o). With a healthy 23,650 stack, I'm in a position to be selective with hands. Folding garbage hands is how we build chips over time. This is a clear fold.

I have top pair (A♣? actually A♥4♣ — top pair, weak kicker) on a dry Ace-high flop A♦ 6♣ 2♣. Button opened wide preflop, so his range contains many worse A‑x hands, some pocket pairs and some two‑club combos. Betting gets value from worse aces and many broadway/medium pairs, and it charges hands with two clubs (the only hands that have a flop flush draw here) and other backdoor draws. I don’t want to check and allow free cards to equity heavy hands, but I also don’t want to bloat the pot OOP with a marginal kicker. I’ll make a half‑pot protection/value bet (≈350) to extract value and deny equity. Plan: if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if he raises large I’ll assume strong (set/two‑pair/better ace) and fold given my weak kicker.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace-Seven on an Ace-high board. Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 350 into the 700 pot. This type of lead often represents a draw (like the two clubs on board), a weak Ace, or a small pocket pair testing the waters. Since I have top pair in a three-handed game, my hand is quite strong, but raising here might drive away her bluffs or weaker hands that I currently beat, while only getting called by better Aces (A8-AK) or sets. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable and see how the turn develops while keeping her range as wide as possible.

Turn card 4♦ pairs my 4♣ — I’ve improved from a single Ace to two pair (A4). Opponent called my flop half‑pot bet, so his calling range is wide: worse A‑x, medium/small pairs, and the occasional set (66/22) or two‑club combo that would already have a flush. Two pair is a strong value hand here and I want to both extract from worse A’s/pairs and deny free cards that can beat me on the river. Betting ~2/3–3/4 pot accomplishes that balance. Pot is 1400, so I’ll bet 1000 to charge his calling range. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the river; if he check‑raises big I’ll assume a very strong holding (set or made flush) and proceed cautiously depending on sizing/reads (call small/medium raises, fold to massive overbets).

I have top pair with an Ace on an Ace-high board. Minni's line is quite aggressive, leading into me on the flop and then firing a large second barrel (about 71% of the pot) on the turn. While my kicker (7) is relatively weak, folding top pair in a three-handed tournament context after defending a pre-flop lead is too tight, especially since the board contains multiple draws (flush and gutshots like 3-5 or 5-7). Raising would likely only result in being called by better Aces (A8+) or two-pair/sets, while folding would be over-folding to aggression. Calling allows me to see a river, keep her bluffs in her range, and evaluate based on the final card and her subsequent action.

I have two pair (A4) on the river after the turn paired my 4. Villain (button) called my half‑pot flop bet and a sizeable turn bet, which looks like a fairly wide, calling range: worse A‑x, medium pocket pairs, and the occasional draw or slowplayed set. The 9♦ on the river does introduce a diamond‑flush possibility, but that requires villain to hold two diamonds (less common); the main hands that beat me are sets (66/22) or a completed diamond flush — both plausible but not dominant versus a wide calling line. Checking risks missing value from worse A’s and medium pairs that will call a river bet and villain has been passive this hand (unlikely to bluff big). I’ll bet for value to charge worse A‑x and pairs: ~2/3 pot is a good balance to extract calls while still folding out some pure air. Bet 2,200 into 3,400. If he raises very large (polarized), I’ll give credit to sets/flushes and fold; if he calls, I expect to be ahead most of the time.

In a three-handed game, Top Pair (Aces) is a very strong hand, even with a mediocre kicker like a 7. Minni's line is extremely aggressive, leading into the pre-flop raiser (a 'donk bet') on the flop and following up with significant bets on both the turn and the river. While this line can represent a monster (like a set of 6s, 2s, or a straight with 3-5), it is also a common line for missed draws. On this board, the club flush draw (which I partially block with the Ace of Clubs) and various straight draws (like 3-4, 4-5, 5-7) have all failed to complete. Given the pot odds (I need to be right about 28% of the time to break even) and Minni's history of aggressive barreling in previous hands (e.g., Hand #29, Hand #32, Hand #63), folding top pair here would be over-folding and potentially exploitable. I will call to see the showdown.

I’m on the button with Jd7c and it’s folded to me — SB Claude (23600) and BB Flash (2650) remain. With three players the button opens first preflop, but this is still a good spot to take the initiative and apply pressure, especially against a short-stacked BB. J7o is marginal but fine as an open in this spot for fold equity/isolation. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — standard, gives good fold equity and keeps the pot manageable. Plan: if Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold (out of position and dominated often); if Flash shoves his ~26BB I’ll call (pot odds ~2.3:1, shove range should be wide enough that J7 has usable equity); proceed cautiously on the flop if called.

I'm holding Td5h in the small blind facing Minni's raise to 300 from the button. This is a weak, unplayable hand with poor connectivity and no strong prospects. Key factors: (1) My hand has limited equity and playability—there are very few ways this hand wins consistently; (2) I'm out of position postflop, which is a significant disadvantage with a marginal hand; (3) While the pot odds are approximately 1.8:1 (450 to call 250), my hand doesn't have sufficient equity to justify the call; (4) Flash is short-stacked (2650), but whether they fold or go all-in doesn't change my hand's weakness; (5) My recent play has been disciplined, folding weak holdings from early positions. With better equity hands available going forward and my healthy stack, folding is the mathematically sound play here.

I am in the Big Blind with Kc 8c, a strong suited hand in 3-handed play. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. I need to call 200 more to see the flop. With pot odds of approximately 2.25:1, calling is well-justified. My stack is relatively short (26.5 BBs), making me look for opportunities to build the pot or take it down, but Kc 8c is a good hand to play post-flop. I will call to see the flop and evaluate from there.

I have flopped top pair with a King kicker on an 8-5-4 board. Since I am first to act against the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), checking is the standard move to allow her to continuation bet with her wide button opening range. Given my stack size of about 24 BBs, top pair is a very strong hand that I intend to play for a significant pot, but checking first allows me to gather information on her bet sizing or induce bluffs from her air.

I raised preflop from the button with Jd7c and got one caller from the short-stacked BB. Flop is 8s‑5d‑4d and Flash checked to me. I have very little showdown value (just J‑high) but have a 4‑out gutshot to a 6 and a diamond blocker (Jd) that reduces some two‑diamond combos in his range. Flash’s calling range preflop is wide (suited connectors, small/medium pairs, suited Aces, broadways) and he will fold a lot to a credible continuation bet. With the pot at 650 I’ll apply pressure but keep the size modest to preserve fold equity while limiting my downside if he shoves. Bet half‑pot (325) to fold unpaired overcards and charge draws. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check and re-evaluate; only barrel with real equity like a 6 or a favourable card). If he check‑raises or jams big I will generally fold this hand — it’s too weak to continue against heavy aggression on this drawy board.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker for an eight (King kicker). Minni's half-pot bet is consistent with a standard continuation bet on this board. Since the board has several potential draws (straight and flush possibilities), my hand needs protection, but calling is currently the best play to keep her range wide and induce further bluffs on the turn. Raising now might force out her air and leave me playing only against hands that beat me or have significant equity. Given my stack size, I am likely committed to this hand, but I will call to see how the turn develops.

On the turn, the Ace of Hearts is a significant scare card. As the pre-flop aggressor and button raiser, Minni's range is heavily weighted toward containing an Ace. My pair of eights has now been demoted to second pair. Since I am out of position, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see how Minni responds to the overcard. If she checks behind, I likely still have the best hand; if she bets, I will have to evaluate her sizing and the likelihood she is continuing a bluff or has actually improved to top pair.

Check. The turn Ace kills most of my equity — J7 has basically no showdown value now (only a 4‑out gutshot to a 6 and backdoor pair potential), while the Ah improves a lot of hands in Flash’s flop‑call range (Ax, A5/A4, some 8x/5x/4x) and makes getting stacked dangerous. The pot is 1,300 and Flash is short (~2,125), so any bet here risks a shove that I should usually fold to. I want pot control and to induce action from floats/draws on the river, so I’ll check and re‑evaluate on the river: call small bets that offer good odds against his wide turn/river bluff/call range, fold to large jams that very likely have me crushed, and only barrel myself if I pick up real equity.

On the river, the board has become significantly more dangerous with the six of diamonds completing a potential flush and several straight possibilities (any seven now makes a straight). While Minni's check on the turn might suggest she does not have an Ace, my pair of eights has diminished in relative strength given the texture of the board. There is little value in betting here, as most hands that would call me (Aces, flushes, straights, or better pairs) have me beaten, and I am unlikely to fold out better hands. Checking allows me to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate her aggression if she decides to bet. Given my relatively short stack, I need to preserve my remaining chips rather than turning a hand with decent showdown value into a bluff.

The river 6♦ just gave me a 7‑high straight (4‑5‑6‑7‑8) with J♦7♣. This is a very strong hand versus Flash’s likely calling range (Ax, single pair, missed draws), though it can still be beaten by a diamond flush (two diamonds in his hand) or a rare 9+7 combo making a higher straight. Flash’s line — calling my flop bet then checking turn and river — makes a rivered flush less likely (many flushes would bet for value), so I want to extract thin value without bloating the pot against the worst-case flush/9‑7 holdings. A small value bet (~1/3 pot) targets A‑x, one‑pair hands and missed draws that will call, while keeping the pot controlled if he raises/jams. I’ll bet 400 into the 1,300 pot. If he folds I scoop; if he calls I expect to be ahead most of the time; if he shoves I’m prepared to call given his short stack and wide shove range, though I remain cautious if the line strongly indicates a made flush.

I am faced with a 400-chip bet into a 1300-chip pot on the river. The board is 8s-5d-4d-Ah-6d. My pair of eights is now the third pair on the board, and the river card completed both potential straights (any 7) and flushes (any two diamonds). However, Minni's decision to check the turn (Ah) strongly suggests she does not have a high-ranking Ace. Her river sizing of roughly 30% pot is quite small, which could indicate a thin value bet with a hand like a 5, 6, or a small pocket pair, or a late-street bluff with missed broadway cards. Given that the pot odds are better than 5:1 (calling 400 to win a total pot of 2100), I only need to have the best hand approximately 19% of the time to make this a profitable call. In a three-handed game, my pair of eights is strong enough to justify a 'showdown' call at these odds.

Calling from button with Q7o. Position is paramount here - I'm last to act post-flop with the best positioning. Pot odds of 1.5:1 are reasonable, and against these stack sizes I can play exploitatively. Flash is desperately short and likely to push many hands; Minni has a medium stack. My button position allows me to leverage Q7 into a profitable spot despite its nominal weakness. Standard profitable call.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Eight offsuit and a stack of 16.75 BB. Claude (Button) has limped, which historically for him indicates a weak range (he raises with strong hands like AdKd). The price to call is only 50 chips, giving me 5:1 odds on a call. While K8o is not a premium hand, it performs well enough against a wide limping range to justify seeing a flop, especially in a three-handed game. I will call the 50 chips and see how the Big Blind and the flop develop.

I’m in the big blind with K♥5♦ facing two limpers (Claude on the button deep, Flash in SB short ~16bb). This is a multiway, out-of-position spot and K5o has poor equity and a weak kicker — not a hand I want to inflate the pot with preflop. Raising would either bloat an OOP pot against a deep caller or induce a shove from the short SB; neither outcome is desirable with this holding. I’ll check and see the flop cheaply, then decide based on board texture (continue only with clear equity or realizable fold equity).

In a limped pot with three players, the flop of Ad-7c-6s is quite coordinated but dominated by the Ace. As I hold only King-high (Kc 8d) and am out of position against both the Big Blind (Minni) and the Button (Claude), there is no merit in betting. My hand has very little equity and no real draw. I will check and hope to see a free turn card, or fold if there is significant betting action behind me.

I checked preflop with K5o to see a cheap flop; the board A♦7♣6♠ completely misses my hand — I have only K-high with no clean draws. This is a multiway, out-of-position spot: bluffing here has poor fold equity (Claude on the button is deep and calls a lot; the SB is short and could shove, which I don’t want to face with no equity). Best play is pot control: check and preserve chips. Plan: give it up to any meaningful bet and only continue if I improve (pair a K/5 or pick up a very favorable action from the short stack).

Holding Q7o with middle pair on 7c 6s Ad. Both opponents checked, indicating weakness. The ace on board is concerning and could beat my hand frequently. By checking, I see the turn for free and can reassess with better information. If the turn is favorable (Q, 7, or a safe card), I can then apply pressure. This is a standard float/control play with marginal hand strength.

In this limped pot, the board has become more coordinated with the Nine of Diamonds. I hold King-high and an open-ended straight draw (needing a 5 or a 10). Since everyone checked the flop, it is unlikely anyone has a strong Ace. However, with my current stack size and the small pot, there is no need to semi-bluff yet. Checking allows me to see the river for free and potentially realize my equity if I hit my straight or even a King, while keeping the pot small.

Turn: I still have K♠5♦ (K♥5♦) on A♦-7♣-6♠-9♦ — no pair, no real draw (flush impossible; only 6 outs: three Ks and three 5s). This is a multiway, out-of-position spot against a deep button and a short SB (~16bb). Everyone checked to me; bluffing here has very poor fold equity and a small bet risks a shove from Flash that I should fold to. Pot is tiny (300) relative to stacks. Best play is pot control: check and concede to any meaningful bet. If checked around to the river I can re-evaluate, otherwise I’ll fold to pressure.

Both opponents checked the turn, indicating weakness. The 9d didn't improve their holdings. I have a pair of sevens on a board with an ace—marginal strength, but ahead of their checking ranges. With position and healthy stack depth relative to the pot, I bet 150 chips (~50% of pot) for value. This applies pressure against weak holdings while remaining conservative enough to avoid getting exploited if called. Flash is critically short and may fold anything but his strongest hands; Minni will likely fold marginal holdings. Standard value bet with positional advantage.

In this limped pot, the turn card (9d) has given me an open-ended straight draw (needing a 5 or a 10 to complete the sequence 6-7-8-9). Claude has lead out for 150 into a 300 pot, giving me 3:1 pot odds. While my direct equity to hit the straight on the river is approximately 17.4% (8 outs), which is slightly below the 25% required by the pot odds, the implied odds are significant given Claude's large stack and the fact that an 8-high straight might be disguised. Furthermore, with everyone checking the flop, it is possible that my King-high still has some marginal showdown value if the board remains dry and the action checks down. I will call the 150 and look to realize my equity on the river.

I checked the turn with K♥5♦ to keep the pot small and only continue with real equity or a clear fold equity spot. Claude fires a small turn bet of 150 and Flash (short ~14.7bb) calls. I have no pair and essentially 6 outs (3 kings + 3 fives) — roughly 13% to improve on the river. The call would cost 150 to win ~750 (pot after call) so I need ≈20% equity to break even. My raw equity (~13%) is well below the required pot odds, and this is a multiway spot which further reduces the value of any pair I make. Raising is a poor option: it risks a shove from the short stack or bloating an OOP pot against a deeper caller with a marginal hand. Given the numbers and my earlier plan to give up to any meaningful bet, folding is the correct, chip-preserving play.

On the river, the King of Diamonds improves my hand to a pair of Kings. However, the board is highly coordinated, featuring three diamonds and several potential straights (any 5 or 10 completes a straight). Claude limped from the button and bet the turn after everyone checked the flop, which often suggests a marginal made hand or a draw. By checking, I can reach showdown as cheaply as possible or evaluate Claude's reaction. Leading out would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Aces, Flushes, or Straights), whereas checking allows Claude to potentially bluff with missed draws or check back weaker pairs that I now beat.

Flash is critically short (1,325 chips) and checked the river, indicating weakness. My pair of sevens is ahead of his likely checking range. The King on the river is concerning in theory but negated by Flash's passive play. I win the 600-chip pot by checking down, securing the chips without variance. This is optimal stack management against a desperate opponent showing weakness on the river.

I am currently the short stack with approximately 21 big blinds in a three-handed game. Holding Jack-Nine offsuit on the button is a standard opening hand in this configuration. Raising allows me to take control of the hand with position, potentially winning the blinds and antes immediately, or allowing me to play a pot in position against the blinds. Given my stack size, a standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate to exert pressure without overcommitting my stack before seeing a flop.

I have T9o in the small blind facing a button open to 300 from Flash (short stack ~17.7 BB) and Claude (BB) still to act. T9o is playable but out of position — it has good flop playability but isn’t a strong 3-bet candidate OOP. 3-betting gains no positional advantage and risks getting Claude (deep) involved or facing a shove from Flash; I’d rather keep the pot controllable and see a flop. The price to call is reasonable: I need to put in 250 more (300 total with my posted SB) into a 450 pot, which is cheap to realize the hand’s equity and implied odds. Plan: call to see the flop, continue when I hit or have good equity, and fold to large 3-bets or heavy postflop pressure when I miss.

With 4-2 offsuit, the worst starting hand in poker, I should fold. Despite Flash being short-stacked (1,775) and getting 3.5:1 pot odds on my 200 call, 4-2o has insufficient equity in this 3-way pot. Against Flash's raising range and Minni's calling range, my hand has roughly 25-30% equity at best, falling short of the ~33% equity needed to break even. The lack of straight potential, flush potential, or high card value makes this a clear fold. Preserving my 23,250 stack for better spots is the correct strategy.

Flop is 8c 6h 9h; I hold Tc 9s — top pair (nines) with a decent 10-kicker and a gutshot to a 7 (4 outs). Button (Flash) is a short, wide open-raiser, so his continuing range contains a lot of worse hands and draws (Ax, Kx, small pairs, suited connectors). Out of position I want to both get value and deny free cards to draws, so I’ll bet a constructive size (~½ pot) to charge heart/straight draws and get called by worse. Size keeps the pot controlled against a short-stack shove but still extracts from hands that will call. Plan: bet 400; if he jams I’ll evaluate calling based on his range and pot odds (I have reasonable equity vs a short shove), and if he shows large strength otherwise I’ll fold/reshape on later streets.

On a board of 8c-6h-9h, I have top pair with a Jack kicker. This board is extremely coordinated, offering several straight and flush possibilities. Minni's lead (donk bet) of 400 into a 700 pot suggests she either has a piece of the board or a strong draw. While my hand is currently strong, it is vulnerable to many turn cards. With my relatively short stack (approximately 17 big blinds remaining), I want to see how the turn develops. Calling keeps the pot manageable and allows me to see another card without committing the majority of my stack to a raise, which would likely isolate me against hands that have me beaten (like two pair or a straight).

I led the flop to charge hearts/straight draws and got a call. The turn (Jh) is a real scare — it’s an overcard and it’s the third heart on board (8c 6h 9h Jh). Out of position with a marginal top pair (T9), betting here often gets me shoved on by a short-stacked opponent and forces a tough fold/call decision for nearly the whole effective stack. Pot is already large (1,500) and Flash can commit ~1,375; calling that jam requires ~48% equity vs a shove-heavy, value-weighted range (Jx, two-pair/sets, two-heart combos), so I don’t want to bloat the pot now. I’ll check to control the pot and induce bluffs — call small turn bets to reach showdown but avoid getting committed unless I have a clear read that he’s shoving too light. If he shoves huge, I will fold this marginal top pair against a polarized shove range.

On this highly coordinated board (8c-6h-9h-Jh), I have improved to top two pair (Jacks and Nines). Minni donk-bet the flop but has checked the turn, which may indicate that the Jack is a scare card for her or that she is moving into check-call mode with a drawing hand. Given my very short stack of 1375 chips and the pot size of 1500 (SPR of 0.9), I cannot afford to check back and allow her to realize equity for free on such a wet board. Shoving is the most effective play to protect my hand against various straight and flush draws while extracting maximum value from Jx, smaller two pairs, or combo draws that are likely to call given the pot odds.

Preflop/Flop: I called the button’s open with T9o from the SB and c-bet the 8♣ 6♥ 9♥ flop to charge hearts/straights; villain (short on the button) called. Turn: J♥ is an overcard and completes many heart/straight combinations, so I checked to control the pot and induce. Flash then shoved all-in for 1,375. Math: pot = 2,875; a call of 1,375 would make the total pot 4,250, so I need ~1,375/4,250 ≈ 32.4% equity to break even. Range/read: Flash is a short-stack button opener who called the flop, so his turn shove is polarized — value hands (Jx that just paired, sets/pockets, and two‑heart flushes) plus a meaningful share of bluffs/marginal shoves (missed one-heart draws, some A‑highs, desperation shoves). Hand equity: T♣9♠ is a vulnerable top pair but not trivial — I have primary river outs to improve (≈3 tens + 2 nines + 4 sevens = ~9 outs → ~19–20% to hit on the river) and I also beat a good portion of his bluff/marginal shove combos without improving. Weighting a realistic polarized shove range, my overall equity is likely around the mid-to-high 30% range, which clears the ~32% break-even. The call is a relatively small fraction of my 13,875 stack (~10%), so it's a +EV, low-cost chance to win a decent pot and to punish a short-stack shove. Decision: call the all-in.

I'm on the button with T♠3♦ (marginal hand) facing only the blinds. With 125 BB and position, I should be aggressive and try to take the blinds/initiative rather than limp/passively call. A standard button open to ~3x the big blind (300) gives good fold equity versus both blinds (the BB is only ~41 BB and will often fold or isolate), keeps the pot manageable if called, and lets me play post‑flop in position. If either blind 3-bets large or the BB shoves, T3o is too weak to call for a big chunk of my stack and I will fold. If called, my plan is to c-bet on dry, favourable flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards. Raise to 300 to steal/attack from the button.

7-2 offsuit is the worst starting hand in poker with zero equity potential. I'm out of position against an aggressive raiser with limited chips to play with. Even though it's a small cost to fold (losing my 50 SB), continuing with this hand would put me in an untenable position post-flop. With a healthy 23,200 chip stack and many hands remaining, I should be selective and wait for playable holdings.

I am holding King of Clubs, Eight of Clubs (Kc 8c) in the Big Blind. Minni (Button) has raised to 300, and Claude (Small Blind) has folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop. My current stack is approximately 41 big blinds (4150 chips). Kc 8c is a strong suited hand that performs well in head-up pots and is well ahead of a standard button opening range. Given the pot odds (30.7%), this is a mandatory defense. I will call to see the flop rather than 3-betting, which would risk too much of my remaining stack pre-flop and likely push out hands I dominate.

I have completely missed this flop with King-high and no significant draws on an Ace-Queen high board. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop raiser's range (Minni). I am first to act since the Small Blind folded. Leading into the aggressor here would be ill-advised. I will check and most likely fold to any significant bet, as I have very little equity to continue in this pot.

I was the preflop aggressor from the button and missed this dry Q♣?Qh? — sorry Qh A♣ 4♠ — flop. Against one opponent (Flash) who only called pre, I can take the initiative with a C‑bet to fold lots of unpaired hands (Kx, Jx, small pairs, pure air) and deny equity. Flash is not desperate-short (≈3,950) but a modest c‑bet keeps the pot manageable while preserving fold equity. I’ll fire ~60% pot (400) to pressure his calling range; if he raises/shoves I will give up this hand — I have no pair or clean draw and should not get steered off a huge chunk of my stack.

I have King-high on an Ace-Queen high board. This board texture is excellent for the pre-flop raiser's (Minni) range, which includes many Aces and Queens. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor flush draw. Calling 400 into a 650 pot requires significant equity that I simply do not have here. Given my stack size and the probability that Minni has at least a pair or better, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for better spots.

Raising from the button with K8o to exploit the critical short stack situation. Flash has only 3,900 chips and is extremely vulnerable to fold pressure. With my substantial chip lead (23,200), applying pressure here is profitable in the long run. A raise to 250 gives me fold equity against both blinds while keeping my risk manageable. This is a standard button exploitation play against desperate opponents.

With 9s 5c in the Small Blind, I am facing a raise from the button. This hand is significantly below the range required to call or 3-bet, especially when out of position against a large stack like Claude. I will fold to preserve my stack.

Claude opened to 250 on the button and I’m in the big blind with T♦2♥ — a weak, marginal offsuit hand but cheap to defend. Pot is 400 and I need to call 150 (total commitment will be 250). 3-betting with T2o is a poor idea: no useful blockers and very little postflop playability OOP, so I won’t bloat the pot. Calling gets me decent pot odds (~27%) to see a flop against what is likely a fairly wide button opening range. Plan: play cautiously OOP — fold to heavy aggression on dry flops unless I pair up or pick up a strong draw; continue/raise only with meaningful equity (top pair, two pair, sets or strong draws). Therefore I’ll call to see the flop.

I have T♦2♥ on a 6♠5♠J♣ flop — no pair, no spade, and only remote backdoor outs. I’m out of position vs Claude (button) and called preflop cheaply, so leading here with pure air would bloat the pot and is a losing line. Check to control the pot and see his action: if Claude checks back I can realize my tiny equity for free; if he fires I’ll fold to a meaningful c-bet (may call a very small probe if the odds justify it). This keeps the pot small with a marginal hand and avoids unnecessary risk OOP.

Check. While my preflop raise was correct to exploit Flash's short stack, Flash has now folded preflop. I'm left in a heads-up flop situation with K8o against Minni's range. With no made hand, no real draws, and out of position, continuing the aggression is unprofitable. Checking allows me to see the turn with reasonable pot odds if Minni checks back, and avoids getting into an out-of-position bluffing war with a weak hand. This is disciplined play—I shouldn't compound the aggressive preflop with further marginal aggression when the dynamic has changed.

Check. I have T♦2♥ on 6♠5♠J♣7♦ — no pair, no spade, only six clean outs (three tens + three deuces) to improve to a pair by the river (~13% equity). Claude checked the flop and hasn't shown aggression, so betting the turn would be a pure bluff OOP with little fold equity and risk of a raise or a call that commits me OOP on the river. Pot control is the right line with this marginal holding: keep the pot small, see the river for free, and fold to meaningful aggression unless I actually pair or the river price is attractive.
