Disciplined check on the turn with K8o. While my preflop raise successfully isolated the short stack, I'm now heads-up with just king-high against Minni. The coordinated board (6s 5s Jc 7d) favors checked-back hands and draws over my non-made hand. Betting here would be a pure bluff that likely gets called by better holdings—Minni could have any pair, a draw, or stronger king. With a healthy chip lead and still another card to come, checking is the prudent play that keeps my river options open while avoiding unnecessary risk with a marginal bluff. I can reassess on the river based on Minni's action and the final board card.

I have T♦2♥ on 6♠5♠J♣7♦8♥ — missed everything and have no showdown value. Claude opened preflop but has checked flop, turn and river, showing passive line and low fold equity. A river bluff could fold out pure air, but against a preflop raiser who has been passive all streets it’s more likely he has some showdown pair (or will call a bluff), so betting is low EV and just bloats the pot with no real equity. Pot is small relative to my stack, so the prudent line is pot control: check and take the free showdown rather than bluffing into a player who’s shown passivity.

Betting 200 on the river with a made pair of eights. Minni's three-street checking pattern indicates weakness—she likely has either a weak pair, missed draw, or air. This is a value bet that extracts chips from her weaker holdings while maintaining a manageable risk profile. The sizing is substantial enough to look credible but modest enough that I'm not overcommitting with one pair.

I checked river with T♦2♥ to take the free showdown. Claude’s line (button open preflop, check-check on flop and turn, then a tiny river probe of 200 into ~550) looks like either a thin value bet (small pair like 7/8/6/5) or a small bluff (missed draws). Calling 200 to win a pot that will be ~950 requires ~21% equity (200/(750+200)=200/950 ≈ 21.1%). The call is cheap relative to my 12,600 stack (~1.6%) and will catch bluffs or very weak value hands; folding surrenders the pot with no information gain. Raising is wrong — I have no showdown value or good blockers and would just bloat the pot OOP. Therefore I’ll call the 200 and see showdown.

Holding Ace-Trey offsuit on the button in a three-handed game is a standard opening hand. I have roughly 39 big blinds, which gives me enough room to raise and play post-flop or fold to significant resistance. Opening for 3x (300) has been my standard sizing and maintains consistency while putting pressure on the blinds.

I have JJ in the small blind facing a button open to 300. This is a textbook 3-bet spot: jacks are strong but vulnerable OOP and in multiway pots, so I want to isolate the opener, gain fold equity, and extract value from worse pairs/Ax while charging draws. Calling is too passive and a tiny reraise to 500 is too weak. I'll 3-bet to 1,200 (total) — a standard size that's large enough to force decisions from speculative hands but leaves manageable stacks (Flash ~36bb) and keeps my range balanced. Pot math: current pot is 450; if Flash calls my 1,200 and BB folds the pot will be ~2,500 and I'll have plenty left to play postflop. I'll re-evaluate if faced with a large 4-bet/all-in, but raising now maximizes expected value and reduces multiway variance.

KT offsuit has solid equity against Minni's 3-bet range and excellent pot odds (1100:1600). With a healthy stack of 23,600, calling to see a flop is the optimal play. I'll have position post-flop and can make strategic decisions based on board texture and action.

I opened Ace-Trey offsuit from the button, which is a standard play. However, Minni's 3-bet from the small blind followed by Claude's cold-call from the big blind signals significant strength. A cold-call of a 3-bet usually indicates a very strong range (QQ, JJ, AK, or potentially a trap with AA/KK). My hand is frequently dominated in this spot. Even though I am getting 3:1 on a call, I would be playing a bloated pot out of position against two strong ranges with a weak Ace. With 36 big blinds remaining, I can afford to wait for a better spot rather than risking a large portion of my stack with a marginal hand.

I flopped trips — J♦ on a 5♥ A♣ J♦ board with Js Jc in my hand. This is a very strong value hand and Claude called my preflop 3-bet from the big blind, so his range contains many A-x, broadways and medium pocket pairs that will pay to see a flop. There are no meaningful two-card draws to fear (only one club on board), so protection is not the priority — extraction is. I want to charge Ax and pocket pairs while keeping their calling range wide, so I lead for a substantial but not overbearing size (~55% pot). Betting 1,500 chips accomplishes that: it prices Ax and most pairs to call and builds the pot for later streets. If Claude calls I’ll keep extracting on turn/river; if he check-raises big I’ll re-evaluate but trips is usually in great shape.

Calling with K♥T♣ on a 5♥ A♣ J♦ flop. I have 8 outs to the nut straight (any Queen) plus K-high, giving me roughly 25-30% equity. Pot odds of 2.8:1 are favorable for a gutshot draw. Minni's 3-bet pre-flop and flop bet indicate strength, but my drawing potential and position make continuation warranted. Stack depth at 22,500 allows for strategic play on future streets without overcommitment.

I hold JsJc — I flopped trips and led for value (1,500) to charge Ax and medium pairs; Claude called. The K♠ on the turn doesn't change the fact that very few hands beat me — mainly AA or KK (or quads) — while a large portion of Claude's calling range (A‑x, Kx, medium pairs) is behind trips and will happily call another value bet. There are essentially no meaningful draws to fear, so this is a pure extraction spot. With the pot at 5,700 I want a size that keeps worse hands and Kx/Ax paying to see more streets but still leaves room to maneuver; I size to ~60% pot. Bet 3,500: if he folds I scoop, if he calls I can continue to extract on the river; if he check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (it would represent the small subset of hands that beat me).

Calling with K♥T♣ on 5♥ A♣ J♦ K♠. The turn King significantly strengthened my hand from a pure gutshot draw to top pair. Pot odds of 2.63:1 require only ~27% equity, and top pair against Minni's likely aggressive 3-bet range provides good value. Her short stack (6,200 remaining) after betting 3,500 indicates commitment to the pot. While she likely has a strong hand, calling preserves stack flexibility for river play and provides appropriate risk-reward given the pot size and my hand strength.

I have trips (JsJc) on a very dry board: 5h Ac Jd Ks 2c. There are almost no completed draws and the only realistic hands that beat me are pocket Aces or pocket Kings; most of Claude’s calling range (Ax, Kx, medium pairs, broadways) is behind my trips and will happily call a value bet. I want to extract thin value from those worse hands while minimizing the chance of inducing a big shove from the small subset that beats me. A modest river bet of 3,000 (~25% of the pot) achieves that balance — it gets called by Ax/Kx and medium pairs but doesn’t overcommit me if Claude check‑raises big (in which case I’ll re‑evaluate).

River call with K♥T♣ on 5♥A♣J♦K♠2♣. Top pair with favorable pot odds of 5.23:1 (need only 16% equity). Minni's short stack (3,200 remaining) and aggressive betting pattern throughout the hand suggest she has a strong range, but my pair likely has sufficient equity. Top pair is a showdown hand on the river, and the pot odds fully justify calling with this strength. Stack remaining at 17,500 allows for this investment.

Three-handed on the button with T6o: this is a marginal hand but position and table size justify an open. Pot is 150 and a cold-call gives up initiative and allows the blinds to see cheap flops. I’ll open to 300 (about 3x the BB / consistent with my prior opens) to apply pressure and steal the blinds, while still leaving room to fold to a big 3-bet. Postflop plan: with a call I’ll use position to c-bet favorable, connected or ace-high boards and realize equity with my backdoor/straight draws; fold to large 3-bets or shoves unless I have a clear call with strong equity. Raising to 300 maximizes fold equity vs the short-stacked big blind and avoids the tiny min-raise that invites wide 3-bets from the deep small blind.

Q4o is a weak hand lacking sufficient equity to profitably call from out of position in the small blind. While Minni has been aggressive, this hand doesn't warrant defending. Folding preserves chips for better opportunities with stronger holdings. Expected equity against typical opening ranges is only ~30-35%, making this a profitable fold.

I am currently the short stack with 35 big blinds. Minni, the chip leader, has opened from the button. While I am getting a decent price to call (200 to win a total pot of 450), 9-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that plays poorly out of position. As the short stack, I need to be more selective with the hands I defend or use to contest pots, as I cannot afford to bleed chips on speculative calls. Folding preserves my stack for a better opportunity.

Raising A6o from the button is standard aggressive play. With healthy stacks and position advantage, I can apply pressure to both blinds. Flash's short stack makes this especially favorable - he'll either fold, call light, or shove. Minni will likely respect the raise given my tight image. A 300 total chip raise (3x BB) is appropriately sized to build the pot while maintaining fold equity.

I am holding a small pocket pair (2c 2h) in the Small Blind. In a 3-handed game, even small pairs have significant value against a Button's wide opening range. Claude has raised to 300 from the Button. While my stack of 3450 (34.5 BB) is the shortest at the table, it is still deep enough to call and look for a set on the flop, or potentially win at showdown if the board runs out favorably and the action is light. I will call the additional 250 to see a flop, being mindful of Minni in the Big Blind who might squeeze, but the price is too good to fold a pair at this stage of the tournament.

I’m in the big blind with 8♠7♥ facing Claude’s button open to 300 and Flash (SB) calling. Pot is 700 and I need 200 more — calling 200 means 300 total in the pot from me. 8‑7 off is marginal, and multi‑way its equity and playability shrink, but the pot odds are favorable (700:200 ≈ 3.5:1 — need ~22% equity). With a deep stack (≈220 BB) I can realize implied odds on connected flops and keep the pot under control. Raising OOP with this hand is thin (gives up positional disadvantage and can get me into tough spots vs Claude’s open and Flash’s short stack), while folding would be too tight given the price. I’ll call to see a flop and proceed cautiously — fold to big show of strength if I miss badly.

I have flopped bottom set (2c 2h on a Kc 2s 8s board), which is a powerhouse in a 3-way pot. My goal is to maximize value and potentially double up my relatively short stack. Being out of position as the Small Blind, the most standard and effective play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor (Claude) to induce a continuation bet. If someone bets, I will likely just call to keep their bluffs and weaker Kings in the pot, unless the bet is very large or there is significant action from Minni, in which case I might consider a raise to protect against the spade flush draw.

Flop Kc 2s 8s with 8h7s: I’ve flopped middle pair (eights) with a weak kicker and one spade. This is a marginal hand in a three‑way pot — Claude opened from the button (in position, wide/aggressive tendencies) and Flash (shorter stack) has checked. Priority is pot control and information. A small continuation‑bet here won’t fold many better hands and will commit me OOP into difficult decisions, while checking keeps the pot manageable and lets Claude show his intent. Plan: check and see Claude’s action. If Claude fires a small bet (~1/3 pot ≈300) I’ll call to realize equity; if he bets large or check‑raises I’ll give it up (or re‑evaluate only if the price is excellent). If both check to the turn I’ll re‑assess and either take a free card or bet for value/protection if I improve or see weakness.

Standard continuation bet from position. I raised pre-flop with A6o, and both players checked the flop (Kc2s8s), indicating weakness. With Flash short-stacked and Minni showing no strength, a 400-chip bet (45% pot) applies pressure while maintaining fold equity. This wins many pots immediately and keeps my aggression consistent. If called, I retain position and can make better decisions on the turn based on their response.

I have flopped bottom set with 2c 2h on a Kc 2s 8s board, which is an extremely strong hand in a 3-way pot. Claude has made a continuation bet of 400 into a pot of 900. While slow-playing is an option, the presence of a spade flush draw makes me lean towards raising to build the pot immediately and charge any draws. As the shortest stack at the table with 3200 chips, my goal is to maximize my chances of a full double-up. A check-raise here looks strong but is necessary to extract value from Claude's Kings or to force a decision from Minni if she has a draw or a mid-pair. I will raise to 1200, which is a standard 3x raise, leaving me with 2000 chips to commit on the turn.

I have 8♥7♠ on K♣ 2♠ 8♠ — middle pair with a very weak kicker. Pot is 2,500 and Flash (SB) has check‑raised Claude’s small c‑bet up to 1,200, leaving Flash with only ~800 behind; Claude still gets to act after me. Calling 1,200 into a 2,500 pot requires roughly 32% equity (1,200 / (2,500+1,200) ≈ 32%), but Flash’s action polarizes his range toward strong made hands (sets, two‑pair/K8/K2, or very strong Kx) or a disguised strong spade draw (he could have both spades). I’m out of position to both opponents, have poor kicker and very few clean outs (two remaining eights to make trips, a few runner outs), and I’m likely to face further pressure from Claude. Raising as a bluff OOP here is thin and prone to getting called by better hands. Given the math and reads, folding to this check‑raise is the best way to preserve stack and wait for a better spot.

Flash is short-stacked (2000 chips) and raised my flop bet aggressively, likely indicating desperation rather than a premium hand. With A6o on Kc2s8s, I have top pair and substantial equity against his wide short-stack range. The pot odds (3.125:1) are favorable, requiring only 24% equity to break even. I have significantly more equity than that across nearly all of Flash's likely holdings. My button position and hand strength justify the call to see the turn.

I have flopped bottom set (2c 2h) on a board of Kc-2s-8s-Qh. My check-raise on the flop was called by Claude, which strongly suggests he has a King, a strong draw (like a spade flush draw or JT), or a pocket pair that he's unwilling to fold. The Queen of hearts on the turn is a relatively safe card, though it does complete some unlikely two-pair combinations like KQ. With only 2,000 chips remaining and a pot of 3,300, the stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) is very low (~0.6). At this point, I am fully committed to the hand. Checking risks giving a free card to flush or straight draws. Therefore, the most effective play is to bet all-in immediately to extract maximum value from Kx hands and force draws to pay the maximum price to see the river.

Flash is all-in on the turn raise to 2000. With pot odds of 2.65:1, I only need 27% equity to break even. My A6o has strong equity against his wide short-stack range—he likely doesn't have KK or QQ given his checking/aggressive pattern. Ace-high with this board texture and his desperate all-in play makes this a profitable call. His range is likely wide (A2-A9 off pairs, any draw, weak broadway cards, or complete air), and I'm well ahead of most of those holdings.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven of diamonds, a strong hand in a three-handed game. My stack is 7300 chips (73 big blinds). Opening for a standard 3x raise is the correct play here to take control of the pot and leverage my position against the blinds.

I have pocket sixes (6d6h) in the small blind facing a button open to 300 with the big blind still to act. In 3-handed play a small pocket pair is best played either by calling to set-mine or by 3-betting to isolate the opener and take the initiative. Calling would let this become a multiway pot (which reduces set value). I'll 3-bet to a size that gets value/fold equity and often isolates the button but doesn't overcommit my large stack. Raising to 1,200 (≈4x the open) pressures many of the button's wide opening hands and charges speculative callers; it keeps the pot manageable relative to my 21,700 stack. If I face a large 4-bet/shove I’ll re-evaluate (and likely fold to a polarizing all-in), but otherwise I want to play heads-up or define ranges postflop with initiative.

Facing a 3-bet from Minni (1200) over Flash's raise from the button, I have A6c in the Big Blind. While A6 has some value, it's a weak hand that plays poorly out of position against two aggressive opponents. The 3-bet indicates strength or positional aggression, and I'd be investing 1100 chips into a pot where I lack fold equity and face difficult post-flop decisions. With better spots likely to come, folding preserves my stack and avoids marginal situations where I'm out of position.

I am on the button with Ace-Seven of diamonds, which is a strong starting hand in a three-handed game. Minni has shown a tendency to 3-bet from the blinds (seen in Hand #104). While her 4x raise sizing is significant, folding A7s here would be too exploitable and tight. Calling allows me to play the hand in position with a strong suited-Ace range. I will evaluate the flop and look to use my position to outplay her or realize my equity if I hit the board.

Flop is A♥ J♥ 5♣ and I hold 6♦6♥ — an underpair with very little showdown/real equity vs a 3‑bet caller’s continuing range (lots of A‑x, broadways, pairs). I want pot control out of position and to preserve my deep stack rather than bloating the pot with a hand that’s usually behind. Plan: check to evaluate and set‑mine; call a small C‑bet (roughly ≤1/3 pot ≈ 800) for the price to see turn, but fold to any sizable bet/raise (≈1/2 pot or larger) since my equity is poor and I don’t want to get committed without improving. If the opponent checks back I’ll re‑assess on the turn with the same pot‑control mindset.

On this Ace-high board, I have Top Pair with a weak kicker (7). Minni 3-bet pre-flop and then checked the flop, which is a bit unusual given her previous aggression. Her range in a 3-bet pot likely includes strong Aces (AK, AQ, AJ), big pairs (KK, QQ), and some broadway draws. By checking back here, I accomplish two things: I control the size of the pot with a marginal hand and I protect my range. If I bet and get raised, I'm in a very difficult spot with my stack size. Checking allows me to see the turn for free and potentially induce a bluff from her on later streets if she has a hand like KK or a missed broadway draw.

I still have an underpair (6♦6♥) on A♥ J♥ 5♣ T♦ — my set-outs on the river are only the two remaining sixes (~4% equity). The turn completed or improved many hands in Flash’s calling/continuing range (Tx, AT, JT, KQ straights, pocket pairs), so I want pot control and to avoid bloating the pot out of position. I’ll check to see what Flash does: call a small turn bet (~≤1/3 pot ≈800) to continue set‑mining, but fold to any sizable bet/raise (≈1/2 pot or larger or an all‑in). If Flash checks back I’ll re‑evaluate the river with the same pot‑control mindset.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (7) is weak. Minni 3-bet pre-flop, which usually indicates a range like AK, AQ, AJ, or high pocket pairs. Her checking both the flop and the turn is suspicious; it could be a trap with a hand that has me beat (like AJ or AT), or she might be pot-controlling a hand like KK or QQ. By checking back, I control the size of the pot and ensure I reach the river with a hand that has significant showdown value without risking a check-raise. This allows me to realize my equity and potentially catch a bluff or value-bet safely on the final street.

Board A♥ J♥ 5♣ T♦ 3♣ with 6♦6♥. I 3‑bet preflop to isolate and then pot‑controlled the hand — both flop and turn were checked. I only have two outs to improve and very little showdown equity versus the calling range that flats a 3‑bet (lots of A‑x, J‑x, Tx, and medium pairs). The pot is 2,500 and Flash still has ~6,100 behind; a river bet here is thin value and will either fold out only pure air or get called by hands that beat me. I’ll take the cheap showdown — check and preserve my stack. If Flash fires a sizable river bet I’ll give it up; only an unusually small block bet would make a call plausible.

I have top pair with an Ace, though my kicker (7) is relatively weak. Minni was the pre-flop 3-bettor but has checked on the flop, turn, and now the river. In a 3-bet pot, if she had a premium Ace (AK, AQ, AJ) or a set, she would almost certainly have bet for value on at least one of these streets. Her passive line suggests she is either giving up with a hand like KQ or is pot-controlling a medium pair like KK or QQ. While there is a small chance I could get value from those pocket pairs with a small bet, I also risk being check-raised by a trapped better Ace or a slow-played monster. Checking back is the most prudent play to ensure I win the pot and see the showdown with a hand that has significant value, especially since I've already controlled the pot size on previous streets.

I'm on the button with T♦3♦ and deep stacks (205bb). Versus only the blinds this is a clear spot to open a fairly wide range: I gain position, have fold equity, and T3s has postflop playability (backdoor flush/straight outs) so it's worth building the pot. Standard sizing here is ~3x the big blind to apply pressure without overcommitting — I'll open to 300. Plan: if I face a large, decisive 3-bet (a size representing a strong hand) I will fold; I will call a small-to-medium 3-bet to see a flop in position. Postflop I'll C-bet favorable, dry flops and play cautiously on coordinated/wet boards, leveraging position to realize equity and extract value when I hit.

9h-Jh is a suited connector offering straight and flush potential. With pot odds of 1.8:1 and a 250 call from small blind, I'm getting good value to see a flop. Minni's aggressive tendencies make his raising range wide from the button. Flash likely calls as well, creating a multiway pot where my hand plays well. I'll take this reasonable spot to see a flop rather than fold or over-commit with a speculative hand.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Eight offsuit. The Button raised to 3 blinds and the Small Blind called. I am getting excellent pot odds (200 to call into a 700 pot) and I close the pre-flop action. While A8o is a decent hand, 3-betting against two opponents from the blinds can put me in a difficult spot out of position if they both call, so I will choose to call and see the flop, preserving my stack and playing for pot control.

I have two pair (Jacks and Nines) plus a 10-out flush draw on the K-9-J flop. Acting first as small blind, I should bet to build the pot and extract value from my strong equity. A 450 bet (approximately 50% pot) is appropriately sized for a value bet that maintains good pot odds for opponents while establishing my hand strength and control.

I am currently holding Ace-high on a very coordinated board (Kh-9d-Jc) that likely hits both of my opponents' ranges significantly. Claude, who was the Small Blind, has led out with a bet of 450 into the pre-flop raiser. This 'donk bet' typically indicates a made hand or a strong draw on such a wet board. With only Ace-high and no significant draw, I am likely behind. Furthermore, Minni (the original raiser) has yet to act on this street, and a raise from her would force me to fold anyway. To preserve my stack and avoid a difficult out-of-position situation, folding is the most prudent play.

Claude made a small c-bet (450 into a 1,350 pot) on K♥ 9♦ J♣. I hold T♦3♦: no pair, but I have a Q gutshot to a 9‑T‑J‑Q‑K straight and small runner‑runner diamond possibilities. Calling 450 into an after‑call pot of 1,800 requires ≈25% equity to break even. My raw chances to improve to a meaningful hand by the river (hit a Q for the straight or pair my Ten) are roughly in the high teens to high twenties percentage-wise (Q by river ≈16.5%, pair of Tens by river ≈12.5%, plus tiny runner‑runner flush chance), so calling is about break‑even to slightly +EV versus a typical SB c‑bet range. I have position, so I prefer to float and use turn/river information rather than thinly raising with a flimsy hand. Plan: call 450 to see the turn; if I improve (Q/T/diamond) or Claude shows weakness I'll apply pressure, but I’ll fold or proceed cautiously to large turn aggression if I miss.

Two pair (Jacks and Nines) is a strong holding on the K-9-J-6 turn. The turn card is a blank that doesn't help Minni's likely range. I'm value betting to extract chips while my hand is strong, keeping him interested in the pot rather than scaring him away with an overbet. This bet size (approximately 40% of pot) is consistent with my flop aggression and maintains control of the hand into the river.

I have T♦3♦ on K♥ 9♦ J♣ 6♣. I missed the turn — my only clean outs are 4 Queens (to make the straight) and 3 Tens (to pair), ~7 outs total (~7/46 ≈ 15.2% to improve on the river). The turn bet is 700 into a 2500 pot, so calling requires ≈700/(2500+700) ≈ 21.9% equity. My raw equity (~15%) is well below the pot odds, and many of the hands I beat when I pair a Ten (e.g. Jx/Kx) will still beat me. Raising as a bluff is thin: I don’t block enough of Claude’s value combos and he’s shown willingness to barrel and call; a raise to the 1400 minimum would be risky and likely get called or shoved. I already planned to call the flop and fold to significant turn aggression if I missed. Given the math and opponent line, folding and preserving a deep stack for a better spot is the highest EV play here.

Raising K9o from button. This is a premium position with a playable hand. A 3x BB raise (300 total) applies pressure to both blinds, particularly the short-stacked Flash at 8,250. This is a standard aggressive play that wins the pot frequently preflop and gets value from weaker holdings when called. K9 is strong enough to continue if called, especially in position.

J2o is an extremely weak hand to play from the small blind against a button open. Calling would put me out of position with a marginal-at-best hand, and there's still the big blind to act behind me. Folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Facing a standard button open to 300 with 7♣3♦ in the big blind. Pot is 450 and it costs me 200 to call, so I need ~30.8% equity to break even. 7-3 offsuit has well under that equity versus a normal BTN raising range (roughly in the low-to-mid 20s at best) and plays terribly out of position. A 3-bet bluff is only attractive if Claude folds to 3-bets a lot — his history shows he defends and gets involved in multi-street pots, so bluffing OOP is too risky. Fold and wait for a better spot to invest chips.

I am on the button with Ten-Seven offsuit. While it is a 3-handed game and ranges are wider, T7o is still a weak hand to open with, especially with active opponents like Minni and Claude in the blinds who have shown willingness to defend and 3-bet. Given my current stack of 82.5 BBs, there is no need to force a steal with a marginal hand. I will wait for a better spot.

AdJd (suited A‑J) in the small blind heads‑up to the BB is a strong, value hand. Button folded so it’s just me vs Claude (BB) who has shown a mix of calls and occasional 3‑betting — he’ll call wide but can be aggressive. Pot is 150 and I only have 50 to call; min raise is 200. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to take the initiative, isolate the big blind, and extract value from worse A‑x and broadway hands while keeping the pot and SPR manageable. If Claude folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I plan to c‑bet favorable flops (A‑high, diamond draws) and proceed cautiously vs big resistance or a large 3‑bet (re‑evaluate/fold to very large 3‑bets OOP).

Td8h is too weak to defend with against Minni's aggressive raise. Out of position with a marginal hand against an aggressive opponent, while already behind in the chip count, this is a disciplinary fold. Preserving chips for premium hands is the right approach.

4♦3♥ is a very weak, offsuit, low connector with poor playability multiway. Calling 100 into a 150 pot requires ~40% equity (100/(150+100)) to be +EV immediately — far more than 4-3o will have versus two deep blind defenders who will defend widely. Raising to try to steal is thin here: both opponents are deep and capable callers/3-bettors, so a raise often puts us in a dominated and bloated pot out of position postflop. With ~40 hands left in the tournament and a big stack to preserve, avoid marginal multiway spots and wait for a much stronger holding or a clearer heads-up steal. Fold and pick a better spot.

Heads-up scenario with K9 suited in the small blind. I'm facing a call of 50 more chips into a 150-chip pot. K9 suited is a playable hand in heads-up play with good equity against the full range of hands the big blind might hold. The pot odds are favorable (3:1) and I have a decent stack position at 11,900 chips. Calling to see the flop is the correct strategy here - it's a marginal hand that plays well in position and benefits from seeing community cards to improve or make decisions post-flop.

I am in the big blind holding Ace-Eight offsuit. The button folded and the small blind (Claude) just called the blind. This 'limp' often signifies a wide, mediocre range or a hand that wants to see a cheap flop. While raising to take control is a viable option, checking allows me to see a flop for free with a hand that has good high-card value but can be difficult to play in a bloated pot if I don't hit an Ace. Given my current stack size relative to the others, I will take the low-variance route and check.

Checking is the optimal play with K9 of hearts on a 5h 4c 2c board. My hand is marginal - just king high with a backdoor flush draw. Flash showed weakness with a pre-flop check from the big blind. Betting would be too aggressive without a clear path to a win. By checking, I control the pot size, keep the option to call if Flash bets, and maintain the ability to improve on the turn. This is standard heads-up strategy: don't get out of line with marginal hands when opponent shows weakness, especially when pot control is important.

I have Ace-high and a gutshot straight draw to the wheel on a low board. Claude limped pre-flop and checked the flop, indicating a likely marginal hand. Checking behind allows me to see a turn for free, maintaining the pot's size while preserving my stack. I still have the best hand frequently here and plenty of equity to improve on the turn.

Betting 100 chips on the turn as a minimum bet to apply pressure on Flash who has shown extreme weakness with consecutive checks. My K9 of hearts has a strong flush draw (9 outs) plus king-high kicker, giving me solid equity. The pot is 200 chips and Flash's short stack (8,150 chips) makes this an ideal spot to win the pot immediately or improve on the river. A small bet tests Flash's commitment and likely wins the pot given their demonstrated weakness pattern.

On the turn, the 5 pairs, which is a significant card. Claude, who limped from the small blind and checked the flop, is now leading out for half the pot (100 into 200). This could represent a 5, but it could also be a standard probe bet with a small pair or a bluff on a board that looks like it didn't hit my big blind checking range. I have Ace-high, which still holds decent showdown value against bluffs, and I have a gutshot straight draw to a wheel (any Trey) as well as overcard outs with my Ace. Given the pot odds (3:1), I have a clear call to see how the river develops.

River with K-high against a hand that called my turn bet. The board runs out 5h 4c 2c 5s 7d. My K9 of hearts is likely behind any pair Flash might hold (5, 4, or 2). Checking to showdown is the correct play - it controls pot size and avoids spilling additional chips when behind. Flash demonstrated weakness early (checking flop) but strength late (calling turn), suggesting a marginal made hand that would beat K-high. No bluffing value on river; check for showdown.

On the river, the board is 5-4-2-5-7. I hold Ace-high (Ah 8s). Claude limped from the small blind and we checked the flop. He bet half the pot on the turn when the second 5 appeared, and I called. Now, Claude has checked the river. My Ace-high has reasonable showdown value in a limped pot against missed club draws (like Kc Qc or Jc Tc) or missed straight draws (like 6-3 or A-3). Betting here would likely only be called by a pair that has me beaten, and I am unlikely to fold out a better hand with a standard bet size. Therefore, checking behind to see if my Ace-high is good is the most profitable and lowest-risk play.

Calling K7o from the button in position. With a pot of 150 and only 100 to call, I have favorable odds to see a flop. Position is my primary edge here, and K-high is playable. Both blind opponents have shown aggression, but I'll have the last say post-flop. Raising would be unnecessarily aggressive with a marginal hand, while folding would waste position unnecessarily. Playing in-position makes my marginal hand more valuable.

I am in the Small Blind with a pocket pair of fives (5d 5s). Claude limped on the button. In a three-handed game, a pocket pair is a strong starting hand. Raising here allows me to take the initiative, potentially win the pot pre-flop, or at least thin the field and define the hand. A raise to 400 is a standard size to isolate the button limper and put pressure on the big blind.

I'm in the big blind with A♠J♥ facing a raise to 400 from the SB (Flash) after Claude limped/called. Three-handed AJo is strong and plays well as a 3-bet: it has good equity vs a wide raising range and dominates a lot of callers. I want to isolate the aggressive SB and charge speculative hands from Claude, while getting fold equity. I size to 1600 (total) — about 4x the 400 raise — which is large enough to discourage callers but leaves plenty of stack to maneuver postflop. Plan: proceed postflop for value and aggression if called; reassess if I face a big 4-bet shove (I will likely fold to a very large shove, call smaller 4-bets and continue with caution).

Facing a 3-bet to 1600 from Minni, I need to reassess K7o. While I was justified calling 100 from the button, this 3-bet changes everything. Minni's aggressive 3-betting pattern throughout the tournament suggests a strong range - likely QQ+, AK, AQ at minimum. K7o is a weak hand that's dominated by nearly all premium holdings in Minni's 3-bet range. I'd be calling 1500 more chips out of position with marginal equity (likely 20-30% against their range). The pot odds don't justify this investment with such a weak holding. Cut losses now rather than play a vulnerable hand out of position in a 3-bet pot. This was a marginal spot to open with, but a clear fold when facing aggression.

Minni's 3-bet to 1,600 is a very large raise (4x my initial raise). While 55 is a decent hand 3-handed, it plays poorly out of position in a bloated pot with a low stack-to-pot ratio. I am not getting the right price to set-mine (the 'rule of 20' for set mining suggests I should have 20x the bet in remaining stacks, but here 1,200 * 20 = 24,000, which is more than either of our stacks). Shoving 8,050 would be a massive over-bet for 55 and would likely only be called by better pairs or overcards like AK/AQ that are flipping with me. Folding is the disciplined play here to preserve my 80BB stack for a better spot.

In a 3-handed game, 98o on the Button is a standard opening hand. I have a healthy stack and want to apply pressure to the blinds to win the pot pre-flop or take control of the hand. A 3x raise is my standard opening size.

Facing a button open to 300 from the button while I’m in the small blind with Qc2d. Pot is 450 and calling costs 250 more (300 total) which leaves me out of position vs the button and often heads-up vs the big blind as well. Q2o is a very weak, easily-dominated hand with poor postflop playability and little equity against a typical opening range. A tiny 3-bet to 500 is a marginal bluff that the big blind can still call and would put me in a tough spot out of position. With a healthy stack and many hands left in the tournament, folding and preserving chips is the highest EV line here.

Calling 200 more (300 total commitment) with T9o in heads-up vs Flash. Pot odds of 3.3:1 support this decision. While T9o is marginal, it has straight potential and I'll have position post-flop. In this heads-up matchup against an aggressive button raiser with a shorter stack, blind defense at reasonable pot odds is mathematically sound. The hand can play well if it connects while my position advantage allows for flexibility on future streets.

I flopped middle pair (pair of tens) on a highly coordinated JKT board against an aggressive button raiser. While I have a made hand, Flash's range includes premium pairs, broadway holdings, and strong draws that dominate or significantly threaten my equity. With Flash short-stacked at 7,750, pot control is essential. Checking allows me to gather information about Flash's hand strength without overcommitting with a marginal holding on a dangerous flop. If Flash bets, I can evaluate based on their bet size and stack situation. This approach maintains flexibility and respects the coordinated nature of the board.

On a board of Jd-Kc-Tc, I have an open-ended straight draw with 8d-9h (needing a 7 or a Q). However, this board is extremely heavy and likely hits Claude's big blind defending range (which includes many Broadways and suited connectors). Since the board is so connected and contains a flush draw, a continuation bet is unlikely to generate much fold equity if Claude has any piece of the board. By checking back, I take a free card to try and realize my equity and improve to a straight without risking a check-raise from my opponent.

Checking on the turn with pair of tens on a highly coordinated JKT7 board. Flash has shown weakness with two checks preflop and on the flop. My hand is vulnerable to straights but currently ahead. Checking allows me to reach showdown with a marginal made hand while keeping the pot manageable and potentially inducing a river bluff from a short-stacked opponent showing uncertainty.

On the turn, the board is Jd-Kc-Tc-7c. Claude has checked for a second time. I have an open-ended straight draw (needing a 6 or a Q to complete a straight), but the board is now showing three clubs, which is quite dangerous. Given that I checked back the flop, I have signaled that I likely don't have a monster hand. If I bet now, it's a pure bluff into a board that connects very well with a Big Blind defending range. By checking, I take the free card to try and hit my straight on the river without risking a check-raise, while also keeping the pot small in case Claude is slow-playing a flush or a strong made hand.

Betting 400 on the river with two pair (Jacks and Tens with K kicker). Flash has demonstrated consistent weakness through checking the flop and turn on a coordinated board. As a short-stacked button raiser who failed to follow through with aggression, Flash likely holds weak broadway cards, missed draws, or small pairs that are all dominated by my strong two pair. A bet of 400 (roughly 60% pot) is appropriately sized to extract value from worse holdings while respecting the pot odds. Flash's short stack and weak play pattern suggest they're either folding marginal hands or calling with dominated holdings, making this the correct value-extraction spot.

On the river, the board has paired the Jack and completed a possible club flush from the turn. I have 9-high, having missed my straight draw (8-9 on J-K-T needs a Queen). Claude has led out for 400 into a 650 pot after checking the flop and turn. Since I have absolutely no showdown value and my checks on the flop and turn signaled a weak hand, Claude could be betting for value with a King, a Ten, or even a Seven, or he could be bluffing. Regardless, I cannot call with 9-high, and a bluff raise is too risky against an opponent who might have improved to trips or is holding a King. The most disciplined play is to fold.

Button vs only the blinds with 7♦T♠ (T7o). This is a marginal hand but highly playable in position and a good spot to open-steal. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3xBB) to apply pressure, pick up the blinds when they fold, and play the flop last if called. Size is big enough to generate fold equity but small enough to keep weaker hands in. If I face a large 3‑bet I’ll fold preflop; if called I’ll C‑bet favorable textures and pot‑control on dry/high boards.

K5o is too weak to continue from small blind against Minni's aggressive button raise. With poor position and a marginal hand, I'll preserve chips and wait for better opportunities. My stack of ~120 BB allows for selective play.

I am in the Big Blind with 9h 5c, which is a very weak hand. Minni raised from the button to 300. While the pot odds are roughly 3.25:1 to call, 95 offsuit plays very poorly post-flop, especially out of position. Given the tournament context and the need to preserve my stack for better opportunities, folding is the most prudent play.

Five-Jack offsuit is a weak, unplayable hand in this pre-flop spot. While I'm on the button with positional advantage, and the cost to call is only 100 chips (less than 1% of my stack), the hand itself is too marginal to justify entering the pot. Even in 3-handed play where ranges are wider, 5-J offsuit doesn't justify a call when there are still 35 hands remaining and I need to maximize my stack efficiently. I should preserve my chips for stronger opportunities. Folding is the disciplined play here.

I am in the Small Blind with Qc 4h, which is a very weak hand. The action folded to me, leaving only the Big Blind, Minni, who is the chip leader and has been consistently aggressive. While it only costs 50 more to see a flop, Q4o performs poorly post-flop and I would be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given my stack size relative to the leader, I prefer to preserve my chips for better starting hands rather than getting involved in a marginal spot against an active opponent.

In a 3-handed game, King-Seven offsuit is a standard opening hand from the button. My stack size of 7,600 (76 BB) allows for standard opening raises. I'm raising to 300 to stay consistent with the table's betting patterns and to apply pressure to the blinds, aiming to take the pot down pre-flop or play the hand with positional advantage.

I’m in the small blind with 8♣6♥ — a weak, offsuit one-gap hand — facing a standard button open to 3bb. Calling 250 more leaves me out of position against the raiser (and possibly the big blind) for marginal equity; pot odds (~35% break-even) don’t justify calling with a hand that’s often dominated and plays poorly OOP. A raise to isolate would be thin and risky postflop. With a healthy 20k+ stack I’ll conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Calling with K-T offsuit from the big blind against a button raise is strategically sound. The hand has decent equity against a broad range, I have favorable odds at 2.25:1, and I'll maintain positional advantage post-flop. Flash's shorter stack and button position suggest a potentially light raise, making this a profitable defense spot. My 11,850 stack is healthy enough to play post-flop poker.

Checking K-T on this J-9-3 two-heart flop from the big blind is strategically sound. I'm out of position with a marginal hand that has a gutshot draw and potential overcards. Checking allows me to see Flash's action rather than committing chips with weak strength. If Flash c-bets, I can evaluate the sizing and decide whether my equity justifies a call. If Flash checks back, I pick up a free card. This passive approach maximizes my positional flexibility on a flop where I lack clear strength.

On a flop of J-9-3 rainbow, I have King high. I was the pre-flop aggressor and my opponent checked to me. While I haven't connected with this board, it is a standard spot for a continuation bet to represent a Jack or a strong draw. A bet of roughly half the pot should be enough to test the strength of Claude's hand and potentially take the pot down immediately. If I am called, I still have two overcards to the middle of the board and some backdoor equity.

Calling 300 with K-T on a J-9-3 flop. My hand has solid equity (~35-40%) against Flash's c-bet range with overcards and a gutshot draw. Pot odds of 3.17:1 are favorable for a hand with 10-12 outs. Flash's shorter stack and button position suggest a potentially wide c-betting range. Calling allows me to see the turn with multiple improvement opportunities while maintaining positional flexibility.

Betting 600 on the turn is optimal here. Flash showed weakness by checking after his flop c-bet. With K-T I have a gutshot straight draw plus an overcard (6-7 outs), plus significant fold equity given Flash's weak action. A 50% pot bet applies appropriate pressure to capitalize on Flash's weakness while positioning well if called. My stack size (11,350) gives me room to apply pressure in this spot.

Claude's decision to lead into me on the turn after check-calling the flop is a strong signal. The 8s is a coordinated card that completes several potential draws Claude could have defended with, such as T7 for a straight, or improved hands like 98. My King-high has very little showdown value, and while I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Ten), the pot odds don't justify a call given the likelihood that I am significantly behind and may face a further bet on the river. Folding is the best choice to preserve my stack.

Button with KcTs in a 3-handed game — strong opportunity to open. Deep stacks (204BB effective vs Shortest 69BB) and position give us initiative; KTs plays well postflop and has good blocker value against premium Kx. Standard sizing to isolate the BB and build the pot: 3x the big blind to 300 is a conventional, readable raise here (min is 200 but 300 gets folds and value). Plan: if called, continuation bet favorable textures (K/T/Q/J high flops) and pot-control or fold to heavy action depending on opponent ranges and stack dynamics; if 3‑bet big from Claude/Flash re-evaluate (likely fold or flat against a light 3-bet, consider shove only vs very light 3-bet given stack depths).

Calling with AT from the small blind against a button raise is sound. The hand has good playability and equity against most opening ranges. By calling 250 to complete the small blind, I can see a flop and make decisions based on community cards. This maintains proper bankroll management while keeping my stack intact for better spots. The odds are favorable and AT plays well post-flop.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ace-Jack suited, which is a premium hand in a 3-handed game. Minni opened from the button and Claude called from the small blind. Both players have wide ranges in these positions. Since I am out of position for the rest of the hand and currently hold the shortest stack, I want to maximize my value and ideally thin the field or win the pot immediately. A 3-bet squeeze is the standard professional play here. I will raise to 1100 to put pressure on both opponents and build a pot for my strong hand.

3-handed on the button with KcTs (offsuit). I opened to 300, Claude flat-called and Flash 3-bet to 1100. KTo is marginal but playable in position and benefits from the multiway dynamic (Claude’s flat keeps weaker hands in and I’ll have position postflop). Calling keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize equity and use positional advantage to play flops with K/T connectivity, while avoiding committing chips with a marginal hand by 4-betting. Plan: call to see the flop (1100 total). On favourable K/T/Q/J high boards I can C-bet for value/bluff; on dry or heavily hostile boards I’ll control the pot or fold to strong aggression.

Calling with AT offsuit is justified by favorable pot odds (4.25:1). With approximately 35-40% equity in this 3-way pot, I'm getting proper odds to see a flop. AT has good postflop playability with two broadway cards. This is not a premium enough hand to 4-bet given that Minni has already called, and folding would forfeit my 250 chip investment unnecessarily. Calling allows me to play postflop efficiently and make better decisions based on the community cards while risking only 6.5% of my stack.

Check with AT on a dry 4h-6d-Jh flop. This is an out-of-position decision where I have an open-ended straight draw (8 outs with K or Q) but currently hold just ace-high in a 3-way pot. 

Checking is prudent here because:
1. **Position disadvantage**: I'm in the small blind and will act first on all future streets, so checking allows me to gather information from Minni and Flash before committing more chips
2. **Drawing hand management**: With 8 outs to the straight and good equity (~30-35%), I want to keep the pot manageable rather than bloat it out of position
3. **Information gathering**: By checking, I can gauge opponent aggression levels and hand strength before deciding whether to continue
4. **Pot control**: I've already invested 1,050 chips preflop; controlling further investment makes sense with a draw
5. **Flexibility**: This line keeps both bluffing and calling options open on future streets based on opponent action

The flop is relatively dry and doesn't hit either position's typical opening ranges heavily, so aggressive checks from out-of-position drawing hands are standard.

I have flopped Top Pair, Top Kicker (TPTK) with Ace-Jack on a relatively safe board, although there is a flush draw available. Given the pre-flop 3-bet and the current pot size (3300) relative to my remaining stack (4800), the Stack-to-Pot Ratio (SPR) is approximately 1.45. In this low SPR situation, I am fully committed to this hand with TPTK. I will lead out with a bet of 1200 (roughly 36% of the pot) to extract value from draws, middle pairs, and weaker Jacks, while setting up a comfortable all-in shove on the turn.

We have KcTs on the button facing the preflop 3-bettor's small continuation bet (1200 into ~3300 pre-bet, ~36% of the pot). We have one clean overcard (K) and some backdoor runner potential but no heart backdoor; our hand is marginal but playable in position. The bet size gives us ~21% pot odds to call, and calling keeps the pot controlled while using position to realize equity and gather information. Plan: call 1200 to see the turn; fold to significant turn/river aggression if we don't improve, or apply pressure / extract value if we pair the K (or get a favorable read on weakness).

Calling with AT on the 4h-6d-Jh flop is justified by favorable pot odds (4.75:1). With approximately 33% equity including 8 outs to an open-ended straight draw plus two Broadway cards, I'm receiving proper compensation for my out-of-position status. The call represents only ~10% of my stack, maintaining proper risk management. This drawing hand has sufficient equity to continue seeing cards, and the pot odds support the decision mathematically. The flop is relatively coordinated to my drawing range, and hitting the turn could lead to favorable situations on later streets.

Checking with AT on the turn is correct out-of-position play with a marginal drawing hand (~28% equity, 7 outs). I'm first to act and already have 2,250 invested. Checking controls the pot size, gathers information from opponents, and keeps multiple options open on the river. Betting a draw out of position is typically unprofitable against multiple opponents who've shown strength. This preserves my ability to make disciplined decisions on the river based on their turn actions.

The Queen of Hearts on the turn is a very problematic card for my hand. It is an overcard to my pair of Jacks and completes a potential heart flush. Since both Minni and Claude called my 3-bet pre-flop and my continuation bet on the flop, it is highly likely that at least one of them has either a Queen, a flush draw that just hit, or a strong draw. With my stack-to-pot ratio (SPR) now being very low (approximately 0.68), I need to be careful. Leading out again would likely commit me to the pot against hands that currently beat me. Checking is the most prudent play to see how the action develops. If there is significant betting behind me, I will have to consider folding my second pair, as my equity has dropped significantly on this board texture.

We missed the turn (Qh) and now the board is 4h 6d Jh Qh — three hearts on board and I have KcTs (no heart, no pair). Pot is 6,900, my stack 18,100; opponents: Claude 10,300, Flash 4,700 (the preflop 3-bettor and flop aggressor). Both checked the turn, which is ambiguous but not a clear sign of strength (if they had a made heart flush they'd usually bet for value; if they have a Queen or Jack they may slow down). I have no real showdown value and limited fold equity vs two opponents — a turn bluff is thin and exposes me to a shove from short-stacked Flash or a call from any Q/J/heart that beats me. I previously planned to fold to significant turn/river aggression when I didn’t improve; that still makes sense. Best line is pot control: check back, keep the pot manageable, and decide on the river (call small bets if I improve to K or T, fold to large aggression).

Checking the river with A-high is correct out-of-position play. I have the absolute worst hand (ace-high) on a highly coordinated board. The turn check from all three players, especially Flash who led the flop, indicates weakness or missed draws. Betting would be a bluff attempt that's unlikely to succeed and wastes chips. Checking keeps the pot manageable, potentially reaches showdown cheaply, and allows me to gather information if opponents decide to act. This preserves chips for better spots later in the tournament while maintaining proper bankroll discipline.

On this river, my hand has been further devalued. Having started with Top Pair/Top Kicker on the flop, the turn (Queen of Hearts) and river (King of Diamonds) have introduced two overcards and completed a potential heart flush. After the turn checked around, it's possible my opponents are also weak, but there is very little value in betting here; I am unlikely to get called by worse hands, and better hands are certainly not folding. Checking is the most logical choice to attempt to reach showdown cheaply or evaluate a small bet from Minni, though my hand is now essentially a bluff-catcher.

River pairs me up — KcTs makes top pair (Ks on the river). Board is 4h-6d-Jh-Qh-Kd with three hearts, so the hands that beat me are very strong (two‑heart flushes, two‑pair combos like KQ/QJ, sets). Both opponents checked the turn (when the third heart fell) and checked the river, which reads as weakness/marginal holdings more often than value-heavy lines. Against two opponents my thin value is limited and a bet will often fold out worse hands while only getting called by better (or induce a shove from the short stack that beats me). Pot control is the +EV line here — do not inflate the pot; take the free showdown. Action: check to show down my top pair.

I'm on the button with Th8h (Ten-Eight suited), a decent hand with straight and flush potential. The stacks are: Minni (big blind) has 18000, Flash (small blind) is short with 4650, and I have 17200. With only 100 to call, this is a profitable spot to raise. I'll raise to 600 to apply pressure and exploit my positional advantage. This amount is substantial enough to win the pot pre-flop or narrow the field while maintaining fold equity. Given the aggressive nature of this game and my position on the button, raising with suited connectors is standard value play.

I am in the Small Blind with Seven of Clubs and Eight of Spades, which is a weak, offsuit hand. Claude has opened for 600 from the Button, which is a 6x raise relative to the 100 big blind. Calling 550 chips out of position with such a marginal hand is not profitable, especially since it would commit more than 10% of my remaining stack (4650). I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Claude opened to 600 from the button and Flash folded, leaving me heads-up in the big blind with 4♠3♦. Calling costs 500 more (600 total) into a 750 pot, giving me ~2.5:1 pot odds (need ~29% equity) — a cheap defend. With deep stacks and a wide button opening range, 3-4o has enough playability (pairs, gutshots, straights) to justify a call; 3-betting OOP with this hand is thin and folding is too nitpicky given the price. I'll call 600 total, see the flop and proceed cautiously — fold to heavy action on dry, high-card flops unless I hit a pair or strong draw, and only commit further with clear equity or strong reads.

Check. I defended preflop as a cheap BB call, but on A♣–J♣–9♦ with 4♠3♦ I have no pair and no club; only a weak backdoor wheel (needs both a 2 and a 5) and two low pair-outs (3 or 4). Pot is 1,250 and Claude is on the button with a wide raising range, so he will C-bet a lot, but I’m OOP and have very little equity or fold equity. Best play is pot control: check and see what he does. Plan: fold to a standard/large C-bet, consider calling a very small probe to float and see a cheap turn, and only continue if I pair or pick up a real draw. No check-raise or lead — too thin and too risky OOP with this holding.

C-betting with strong drawing equity (OESD + flush draw). The flop (Jc9dAc) is dry for most hands, and Minni's check indicates potential weakness. My Th8h has approximately 8-12 straight outs plus flush outs, giving me roughly 32-40% equity. Betting 500 applies pressure while maintaining fold equity if Minni doesn't have a premium hand. Even if called, I have strong equity to improve on the turn/river. Position and aggressive image support this play.

I defended preflop as a cheap BB call, but on Jc–9d–Ac with 4♠3♦ I have nothing (no pair, no club, only 6 outs to a pair). Claude’s flop bet is 500 into a 1,250 pot (pot ≈1,750 after the bet) — break‑even equity to call is ~22% (500 / (1,750+500)). My chance to hit a pair by the river is only ~24%, and even when I pair it will often be second‑best against a button’s continuation range (lots of Ax, broadways, and value hands). I’m out of position and likely to face further aggression, so calling here turns into a marginally +EV but high‑variance line that loses value most of the time. Check‑raising or leading is far too thin. Fold and wait for a better spot OOP with more real equity or position.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit, which is a weak hand. With my stack being significantly smaller than both opponents and the hand offering little post-flop value, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chips for a more advantageous situation.

Heads-up vs Claude from the small blind with K♣5♥. Pot is 150 and I only need to put another 150 to make it 200 total (min-raise). K5o is marginal but perfectly playable heads-up — raising is preferable to completing and playing OOP. A min-raise to 200 takes the initiative, applies pressure to Claude’s wide defending range, and lets me c-bet favorable flops or control the pot on wet boards. If Claude 3-bets large I’ll let this one go; if he calls I’ll continuation-bet on dry/high-card flops and proceed cautiously on coordinated boards.

With 3h 2c in the Big Blind facing a min-raise to 200 from the Small Blind (Minni), I have favorable pot odds. The pot is 300 and I need to call just 100 more, giving me 3:1 odds. In heads-up play, Minni's raising range from the Small Blind is wide and loose - I've observed him raising frequently with marginal holdings throughout this tournament. Even with the worst hand, 3-2 has roughly 25%+ equity heads-up against a wide range, which is profitable given my pot odds. The stacks are relatively balanced (17,750 vs 17,300), so this is a standard heads-up call. Folding would give up too much equity and allow Minni to run over the table from the button.

I was the preflop aggressor (opened to 200) and we see a dry A♣-4♥-3♠ flop. Claude’s call preflop is wide heads‑up and includes many non‑Ace hands that missed this board, but also a decent number of Ax combos. As the aggressor I should continuation‑bet to fold the weaker portion of his range and to define his holdings. A half‑pot c‑bet (200 into 400) applies pressure while keeping the pot controllable — I’ll fold to significant strength (check‑raise/shove) and re‑evaluate if he simply calls (plan to proceed cautiously on the turn, bluff less vs aggression and only barrel with equity or a clear out‑card).

With 3-2 off-suit and a pair of threes on the flop (4h 3s Ac), I'm facing a 200 bet from Minni in a heads-up pot. The pot odds are favorable at 4:1, requiring only ~20% equity to call profitably. I have a made hand that wins against Minni's frequent air/marginal holdings from the small blind. My observation of Minni's loose raising patterns throughout the tournament supports calling with this hand and equity. Stack sizes are balanced, making this a standard heads-up continuation.

I opened from the SB and c‑bet the flop; Claude called. Turn is A♣‑4♥‑3♠‑6♣ and I hold K♣5♥ — just K‑high. The turn didn’t give me a pair or any realistic draw (flush is impossible with only one club in my hand and two on board), and Claude’s flat on the flop is weighted toward Ax, small pairs, and reasonable floats that generally beat or continue against a turn bluff. I have almost no equity and very little fold equity to a turn bet, so forcing the issue would be throwing chips away. Best play is pot control and to use position: check and see what Claude does. If he bets big I’ll fold; I may call a tiny probe only if the sizing is extremely small, but generally I concede the hand on a turn bet. If he checks back I’ll reevaluate on the river (only consider aggression if the river gives me real equity — pairing a K/5 or a safe blank with good blocker properties).

Minni has checked on the turn, which is a weakness indicator. I hold a pair of threes on a board of 4h 3s Ac 6c, which is a marginal hand. By checking back, I accomplish several goals: (1) I keep Minni engaged in the pot with weaker holdings - his check suggests he either missed his draw or has a weak hand, and a river bet might allow him to continue bluffing; (2) I avoid bloating the pot with a hand that's unlikely to win a large amount; (3) I get a free look at the river card, which could potentially help me or at least give me better information about Minni's hand strength; (4) If I bet and Minni raises, I'm likely behind and have wasted chips. Given the balanced stacks and that Minni just checked, checking through to the river is the prudent play. I'll be in position to make a final decision on the river with complete information.

I opened to 200 and c‑bet the A‑4‑3 flop; Claude called and we both checked the 6♣ turn. The river 9♠ is a blank — I hold K♣5♥ with no pair or real showdown value. Claude’s flop call then turn check is weighted toward Ax, small pairs, or sticky one‑pair hands that will not fold to a river bluff. Betting here would be a thin bluff with very little fold equity against his line, so the lowest variance, highest EV play is to check and concede the pot (and fold to a bet).

Minni has shown clear weakness by checking both the turn and river. While I hold a marginal pair of threes, betting would risk additional chips with a hand that's unlikely to win a larger pot. Checking allows me to reach showdown inexpensively with my made hand. My pair of threes is likely ahead vs. Minni's weak holdings based on his check-check pattern, and I avoid the risk of a raise that would put me in a difficult spot. This is prudent pot control with a marginal hand in a heads-up situation.

Three-handed, on the button I should be opening a much wider range. Q♣2♦ is weak but playable with position and good for stealing the blinds. I raise to 300 (≈3x BB) to apply pressure on the short-stacked big blind and avoid the passive trap of limping. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet with this hand, but if called use positional advantage to C‑bet dry boards and proceed cautiously on wet/connected boards. This sizing balances fold equity and pot control while keeping future decisions manageable.

3h 5h is a trash hand with no real value. I'm in the small blind, out of position to both opponents. Minni is aggressively raising from the button as usual, but that doesn't mean I should defend with junk. My stack is healthy at 18200, so I can afford to wait for better spots. Folding weak hands from out of position is a fundamental poker principle, especially late in a tournament. There's no reason to put additional chips at risk here.

I am in the Big Blind with Jh 8h, a solid suited one-gapper. Minni has raised from the Button, which is a wide range. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450, giving me roughly 2.25:1 odds. This hand is well within the standard defending range against a Button open. While I will be out of position for the rest of the hand, the suited nature and high-card value of the Jack make it a call rather than a fold or a 3-bet, especially given my current stack size relative to the blinds.

I have top pair on a relatively connected and wet board (8-5-6 with a flush draw). Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard play here to see how she reacts and to control the pot size with a medium-strength hand. I'll likely call a standard-sized continuation bet, but leading out here might force out hands I want to keep in or bloat the pot when I'm vulnerable to many turns.

I opened wide on the button with Qc2d and the flop (8♦-5♦-6♠) is not a dry board I should automatically C‑bet. With only Q♣2♦ I have no pair and only a backdoor diamond (need both turn+river) — effectively very little equity. The flop is connected and two‑toned (two diamonds), which hits a BTN‑open/BB‑call defending range (AXs, suited broadways, small pairs, 7x/9x, suited connectors) frequently and makes me vulnerable to check‑raises or being called by many better hands. Flash checked; with position and a large stack advantage I want pot control and to realize equity rather than barrel into a lot of resistance. Plan: check and fold to sizable aggression, call small probes to see a cheap turn, and then look to take the pot away on safe turn cards or bluff when I pick up equity or the turn is a clean card for my range.

I have improved to top two pair (Jacks and Eights) on the turn. After checking the flop and seeing Minni check back, her range is likely capped or contains mediocre strength hands and draws. I am now ahead of almost all of her calling range, including Jx, 8x, and various draws. I will lead out with a value bet of approximately 70% of the pot to extract value from these hands and to protect against potential draws on this semi-connected board.

I opened wide on the button with Qc2d and checked the flop (8d-5d-6s) to control the pot — that was the plan. On the turn (Jc) Flash bets 450 into a 1,100 pot. I have no pair and my backdoor diamond plan is dead (I needed both turn+river diamonds pre‑turn). My only realistic river outs are 3 queens + 3 deuces = 6 outs → ~6/46 ≈ 13% equity to improve to a pair on the river. To call 450 here I must market about 450/(1100+450+450) ≈ 22.5% equity to break even. My raw outs (13%) are well below the required equity, and versus a caller’s likely range (pairs, Jx, sets, strong draws) my true equity is even lower. Raising as a bluff is unattractive: Flash is short-stacked and may call/jam or have hands that don’t fold to a small raise, and I don’t have strong blockers. Given pot control, my lack of equity, and the unfavorable pot odds, folding is the +EV play.

I'm on the button with 2c7h, one of the weakest hands in poker. While position is valuable, 2-7 is fundamentally unsound. To call 100 chips more, I'd be investing into a pot with terrible equity against typical blind holdings. Flash is short-stacked (4,950) and likely to be aggressive, while Minni has a healthy stack. With 30 hands remaining in the tournament and my stack at 18,200, I should preserve chips by folding marginal situations. Even with positional advantage, this hand doesn't warrant even a minimal investment. Folding 2-7 is a disciplined decision that aligns with tight, value-oriented play.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Five offsuit. Since the Button has folded, it is just me and the Big Blind (Minni). Ace-high is a strong starting hand in a blind-vs-blind situation. Raising is a standard play here to attempt to take down the blinds or define the hand early. Given Minni's active play, I want to lead with a standard raise to 300 chips.

Call to 300 (add 200) with J♥6♥. Heads-up vs the small blind this is a wide opening range and J6s has reasonable heads-up equity (jack blocker + backdoor heart/straight potential). Calling keeps the pot controlled against a shorter-stacked opponent (Flash ~47BB) and lets me see a flop with a playable hand — a thin 3‑bet here would commit more with a marginal holding. Plan: continue aggressively only with meaningful equity (pair of J, heart/straight draws) and fold to heavy action on dry boards where I miss.

I have flopped top pair with an Ace on a board of Ace-Six-Four. As the pre-flop aggressor in a blind-vs-blind situation, I should lead out with a continuation bet to extract value from Minni's potential drawing hands, middle pairs, or weaker Aces. A half-pot bet of 300 chips is appropriate here to define where I stand and protect my hand.

Flash opened preflop and continuation-bet 300 into a ~900 pot. I have Jh6h — a made middle pair (sixes) with a decent kicker and only backdoor heart potential. The bet is small (~1/3 pot) so the pot odds to call are ~20% (300 to win ~1200); versus a wide SB opening/c-bet range my pair has enough equity to continue. I want to keep the pot controlled against a shorter-stacked, aggressive opponent and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand, so I’ll call and re-evaluate on the turn. If I improve (two-pair/heart/straight) or see a clear bluff, I’ll proceed; if I miss and face heavy turn action, I’ll fold.

The turn card (4s) completes a potential spade flush and pairs the board. While I still have top pair (Aces), my kicker is weak. Checking is the best move here to control the pot and gauge Minni's interest in the hand. If she has a flush or a 4, betting would be counter-productive. If she has a weaker hand, checking might induce a bluff or lead to a cheap showdown.

Board is 4c 6s As 4s and I hold 6h Jh — second pair with a decent kicker and backdoor heart. Flash opened wide from the SB and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn: that looks like pot-control/weakness or a trap. Pot is 1,200 and Flash is relatively short (≈4,400 effective). Betting here would bloat the pot and could induce a shove or check-raise that I don't want to face with a marginal hand. I prefer to keep the pot under control, see a cheap river, and re-evaluate (look to value-bet only if I improve or call small river bets; fold to large river aggression if I miss). Check to realize equity and avoid unnecessary risk.

I have top pair with an Ace, but my kicker (5) is very weak. The board is somewhat dangerous with a pair of fours and three spades, making a flush or trips possible for Minni. Since she checked the turn and now the river, she likely has a marginal hand or a missed draw. While I could bet for thin value against a six or a newly improved King, I risk being called or raised by a better Ace or a slow-played monster. Checking back allows me to reach showdown safely and likely win the pot with my pair of Aces.

Check. I’ve got second pair (6s) with a Jack kicker on a board of 4c 6s As 4s Kc. Flash opened wide from the SB, c-bet the flop then checked the turn and river — a lot of his checked range is A‑x, K‑x or medium hands controlling the pot, and there are also missed draws/bluffs. The river K improves many hands that beat me (Kx, Ax) and he’s short (≈4,400) so a value bet risks either a call by better or an all‑in shove that I don’t want to face with a marginal hand. Pot is only 1,200, so the +EV line is to check and realize equity; only call a small river bet from him but fold to large river aggression or a shove.

I am on the button with 7-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. While being on the button offers a positional advantage, my stack is the shortest at the table (56BB), and both opponents have been playing actively. There is no reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand.

I have 2♥9♣ in the small blind, heads-up vs Claude (BB). Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to call (min-raise to 200 available). 2-9 offsuit is a very weak, poorly connected hand with bad postflop playability and a high chance of being dominated. Calling 50 gives marginal pot odds but I'd be out of position and likely face aggression; raising to steal is irresponsible with this hand. Preserve stack and wait for a better spot — fold preflop.

Q9s on the button in a three-handed pot is worth opening. It has good postflop playability and equity, and raising lets me take the initiative and steal the blinds or isolate the short-stacked BB. Claude (SB) is capable of 3-betting, but a standard 3x sizing keeps pressure on Flash (BB, short) and preserves flexibility postflop. Plan: open to 300, c-bet favorable flops and re-evaluate if I face a big 3-bet (fold to large, consider calling or 4-betting vs smaller, polarized 3-bets).

Raising to 800 with JK offsuit from small blind against a button raise. This hand has solid equity against Minni's wide button-raising range. The raise applies good pressure, potentially isolates against Flash's short stack, and establishes strength. At 18,200 chips with good table position, this aggressive play is well-timed and properly sized to force decisions.

I am in the Big Blind with pocket fives. Minni opened from the button and Claude three-bet from the Small Blind. While pocket fives are a decent pair, they are very difficult to play post-flop when facing a raise and a three-bet from the two largest stacks at the table. To call the 700, I would be set-mining out of position without guaranteed implied odds, especially since Minni still has the option to four-bet. Given my stack size of 5,500 (55 BBs), I prefer to wait for a more favorable spot rather than committing a significant portion of my stack to a marginal situation where I am likely behind or flipping at best.

Claude 3-bet my 300 to 800 after I opened Qh9h on the button. Q9s is too weak to 4-bet for value and too vulnerable to a cold 4-bet — but it has excellent post‑flop playability and flush/straight potential in position. With ~158 BB effective the stacks are deep enough to realize implied odds. Calling 500 more into the ~1,200 pot gives ~29% pot‑odds (500 / (1,200+500)) which Q9s can justify against a standard 3‑bet range, and staying in position lets me apply pressure on many flops. Plan: call 500 (800 total) — c‑bet favorable flops (top pair/strong draws or dry boards where I can take it away), check and control the pot on others, fold to large, committed aggression without improvement, and re-evaluate if Claude shows clear weakness or makes a small, highly polarized 4‑bet.

On the K♣-T♠-4♦ flop with J♥K♠, I hold top pair with a decent Jack kicker. As the pre-flop aggressor from the small blind, I should continue my aggressive narrative with a solid continuation bet. This bet size (~35% of the pot) accomplishes multiple goals: (1) extracts value from weaker Kings and Ace-high holdings, (2) maintains the pressure I established pre-flop, (3) clarifies Minni's hand strength—they'll either fold marginal holdings or commit with stronger holdings. The board is relatively dry with limited connectedness, favorable for my range. My stack of 17,450 is healthy enough to apply meaningful pressure. A check would give Minni a free card and relinquish my positional advantage, so betting is clearly superior here.

I opened the button and flat‑called Claude’s 3‑bet preflop, so I’m in position with Qh9h on a Kc‑Ts‑4d flop. Claude bets 600 into a 2300 pot — calling costs 600 for ~20.7% pot odds (600 / (2300+600)). My hand is behind most of Claude’s value range but has meaningful equity: ~3 queens + 3 nines + 4 jacks ≈ 10 clean outs to improve (≈21% chance to hit the turn), plus a small backdoor heart flush possibility. Raising here would be too ambitious with a marginal hand against a likely strong 3‑bet/cbet range; folding gives up good price and positional advantage. Plan: call to see the turn, continue (or raise) if I pair or make the straight/flush draw; if I miss and Claude fires a big turn bet I’ll re‑evaluate and likely fold to heavy aggression to avoid committing with air.

Turn betting with top pair and good kicker. The 3♠ adds a third spade but doesn't dramatically change the situation. Minni's flop call suggests they have a King, a Ten, or a draw. My J♥K♠ is very likely the best hand. I should value bet here to extract chips from weaker holdings while maintaining my aggressive narrative. A bet of ~1,000 is appropriate sizing—not too large to shut out draws, but substantial enough to build the pot.

I’m on the button with Q♥9♥ after opening and flatting Claude’s 3‑bet. Board K♣‑T♠‑4♦‑3♠; Claude barrels the turn for 1,000 into a 3,900 pot. Calling costs 1,000, so pot odds ≈ 1,000 / 4,900 ≈ 20.4%. My clean outs to improve: 3 queens + 3 nines + 4 jacks = 10 outs → ~10/46 ≈ 21.7% to hit the river. My equity slightly exceeds the price, so a call is +EV. Claude’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, turn barrel) is weighted to strong Kx/overpairs/AK/AQ but he will also have some bluffs and missed broadway draws. Raising here with Q9 is too ambitious and folding lets go of positive equity and positional advantage. Plan: call 1,000 to see the river; if I pair or hit the straight I will value‑bet or extract; if I miss and face heavy river aggression I’ll fold unless the river bet/pot odds justify a call.

River value bet with top pair and strong kicker (K♥J♠). Minni's flop and turn calls suggest they likely hold a King, a Ten, or a weaker hand that I beat. The J♣3♣ river card doesn't dramatically change the situation. I've been the aggressor throughout - a value bet of 1,500 (31% of current pot) extracts value from weaker hands while remaining consistent with my established narrative. This sizing is large enough to be meaningful but not so large that only stronger hands call. My stack of 15,850 supports this bet, and with 27 hands remaining, accumulating chips through value extraction is critical.

River: Kc Ts 4d 3s 3c. I have Q♥9♥ — missed everything and have no pair or showdown blocker. Claude opened with a 3‑bet preflop and barreled flop/turn; that line is very weighted to value (Kx, Tx, overpairs, maybe a set/boat). Pot is 6,400 and his river bet is 1,500, so calling only gets ~19% pot odds (1,500 / 7,900). With no pair I beat only a tiny fraction of Claude’s realistic 3‑bet/barrel range (pure air or light bluffs), so his bluff frequency would need to be >~19% to make a call +EV — given his line and history that’s unlikely.
