Raising as a bluff (min raise to 3,000) is possible but risky: I don’t have the fold equity against a 3‑bettor who barrels multi‑street, and a call/re‑raise puts me in a spot I can’t profitably continue from. Fold and preserve my stack for a better spot in position.

Calling with Q-4o on the button at minimal cost. Button position provides significant post-flop advantage. While Q-4 is weak, the low price to call (100 chips) and positional leverage justify seeing the flop. I can fold unimproved hands while maintaining options when I hit. With Flash short-stacked at 5,450 and Minni's aggressive tendencies, position will allow me to make profitable decisions post-flop.

I am the short stack at the table with 5,450 chips. Claude limped on the button, indicating a wide range of hands. King-four offsuit is a marginal hand but worth completing the small blind for 50 more chips in a 3-way pot. This allows me to see a flop cheaply while maintaining pot control out of position.

Three-way limp into the big blind with A♣3♦ — a marginal, often-dominated ace in a multiway pot. Pot is only 300 and I’m out of position to the button postflop, so I want to keep the pot small and avoid bloating it with a hand that plays poorly multiway. Check to see a free flop. Plan: fold to heavy aggression unless I pair the Ace (or make a strong draw/top-two), call small c-bets selectively for pot control, and only raise with clear equity or a favourable board that lets me take the initiative.

In this 3-way limped pot, I have flopped top pair with a weak kicker (Kings, 4 kicker). The board is somewhat coordinated with a flush draw and straight possibilities (7c Kc Jd). Given that I am out of position and the pot is small, checking is the most prudent play. It allows me to see how Minni and Claude react to the board. If a bet occurs, I will most likely call to see a turn, but I want to keep the pot manageable given my weak kicker and status as the short stack.

Three-way limp pot with A♣3♦ — a marginal, often-dominated ace. The flop Kc‑7c‑Jd gives me only Ace‑high and a very thin backdoor club possibility; I have no pair and I'm out of position to the button. Pot is small (300) so I want to control the size and avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand. I check to see what the button does; plan is to fold to heavy aggression unless I pair the Ace or see a cheap turn, and only call small c‑bets for pot control rather than barreling or raising without clear equity.

Both opponents checked the flop, giving me a free look at the turn. I hold Q♥4♠ - queen high with a weak kicker on a board with K♣ J♦. This is a marginal holding at best. The checked-through flop suggests both opponents may have weak hands, but betting Q♥4♠ for value doesn't make sense as I'm unlikely to win with this holding. My four kicker is particularly vulnerable. I'm in position, so I can see the turn card for free and reassess. If a favorable card hits (another Queen, or a blank that doesn't help their likely holdings), I can apply pressure on later streets. Checking also allows me to control pot size with a weak hand - if the turn helps me, I can bet confidently; if it doesn't, I haven't compounded the problem. This is the prudent, positional play.

In this 3-way limped pot, I have top pair with a weak kicker. The flop was checked around, which suggests my King is likely the best hand, as someone would usually lead out with a King or a strong draw on such a coordinated board. The turn (2c) introduces a flush draw. While I could bet for protection/value, checking again is a viable strategy to keep the pot small and potentially induce a bluff from opponents who might perceive my checks as weakness. Since I am the short stack and out of position, I want to avoid being check-raised or bloating the pot with a marginal kicker.

Check. I have A♣3♦ on Kc‑7c‑Jd‑2c: currently only Ace‑high but I have a one‑card nut‑club flush draw (Ac + Kc + 7c + 2c = 4 clubs; 9 clubs remain ≈19.6% to hit on the river). Pot is tiny (300) and both opponents have shown weakness by checking the flop and turn. I want pot control and to see a free river — I’ll check and fold to any sizable turn bet. I’ll only call very small turn bets that give correct pot odds (roughly ≤ ~70 chips) to chase the nut flush; if the river bricks I’ll check/fold to aggression, and if a club lands I can bet for value or raise.

Checking the turn with Q♥4♠ on a K♣ J♦ 7♣ 2♣ board. Hand strength is marginal - queen-high with a weak kicker is unlikely to win without improvement. Both opponents checked through the flop and turn, suggesting weakness or a potential trap. Betting Q-high for value doesn't make sense; I'd only be good if opponents have absolutely nothing, which is unlikely given their board coverage (K, J, 7 are strong cards). By checking, I see the river for free and can reassess on the final street. This is disciplined bankroll management - not burning chips on marginal holdings without significant equity. If a favorable card comes, I can make a better-informed decision.

I have top pair with a weak kicker in a 3-way limped pot that has been checked all the way to the river. The board texture includes a completed flush draw (clubs) and various straight possibilities, but the extreme passivity suggests that my King is likely the best hand. Betting here might only get called by better Kings or perhaps a stubborn Jack, while checking allows me to induce a bluff from a missed draw or simply take the pot down at showdown without risking a check-raise on a coordinated board. Given my position as the short stack and the small size of the pot, checking is the most consistent and low-risk way to realize my equity.

River pairs my 3: A♣3♦ on 7♣ K♣ J♦ 2♣ 3♥ gives me a very weak pair (pair of threes) with an ace kicker. The board is high (K, J) and three clubs — if anyone had a two‑club starting hand they already have a flush, but both opponents played passively (checked flop and turn after limps), which makes a concealed nut flush or a strong value hand less likely. Pot is tiny (300), so a bet here would be thin value and just risks a check‑raise or losing more to any Kx/Jx/flush. Best line is pot control: check and take showdown. If either opponent now barrels a sizable bet I’ll assume strong value and fold this weak pair; I’ll only consider calling very small river bets.

River decision with Q♥4♠ on a K♣ J♦ 7♣ 2♣ 3♥ board. I have queen-high with a weak kicker - one of the weakest possible hands in this situation. Both opponents have checked all the way to the river, indicating either genuine weakness or they're not interested in building a pot. The board is heavily coordinated (K, J, 7) which likely helps opponents' ranges far more than my marginal hand. Betting Q-high for value makes no sense as I can only win against hands with ace-high or complete air - very unlikely holdings given the board texture. Flash is short-stacked (5,400 chips) and Minni is solid (13,650). The pot is 300. Going to showdown with a check allows me to realize my equity without burning additional chips. This is disciplined hand management - protecting my chips when I have minimal equity and no information advantage. Checking and going to showdown preserves my stack for profitable spots ahead.

I am on the button with Jack-Four offsuit. This is a very weak starting hand. While it's tempting to try and steal the blinds from the button, my stack of 5,700 chips (57 BBs) is the shortest at the table, and both Minni and Claude have been playing relatively active and aggressive games. J4o does not perform well against most defending ranges and is an easy fold in this spot to preserve my stack for a better opening opportunity.

Heads-up with the button (I’m the small blind), Q9o is a reasonable opening hand in this spot. Pot is only 150 and calling is cheap, but opening to 200 (2x BB) gives fold equity, isolates the big blind and lets me take the initiative postflop (I act last after the flop). Claude is a big stack who 3-bets sometimes, so I’ll size small-to-standard to avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand — plan is to fold to a strong 3-bet, but continue or c-bet small if he calls. Raise to 200 to pressure the blind and play the hand in position.

Folding A2o out of position. While the pot odds (3.5:1) are reasonable, A2 offsuit is too weak to play from the big blind against a raise when out of position. With a healthy stack and 25 hands remaining, I should be selective and fold marginal hands that will lead to difficult post-flop spots. A well-timed fold here preserves chips for stronger situations.

I have Qc3c on the button with two players to act (SB Claude deep, BB Flash ~56bb). This is a marginal hand but suited Q with a club blocker has decent playability in position and is a fine candidate for a steal. Pot is 150 with 100 to call; raising puts me in control and leverages my positional advantage. I’ll make a standard 3x-type open to 300 — large enough to apply pressure and isolate, but not so big that I bloat the pot against deep callers. If Claude 3-bets big I’ll fold Q3s (poor equity vs a strong range); if Flash calls or Claude calls I’ll use position to play postflop for folds or to realize my equity. Raise to 300.

A5o is a playable hand with decent post-flop potential (top pair, straight possibilities), but it's not premium. From the SB, calling the 250 additional bet allows me to see a flop while keeping the pot manageable. Minni's aggressive raise pattern suggests this could be a steal attempt, and I have position to react on later streets if needed. My healthy stack (20500) provides flexibility. Raising would require more hand strength, and folding would be too passive with an ace.

I am in the Big Blind with Qh 3s. The button (Minni) raised to 300 and the small blind (Claude) called. I need to put in 200 more to see a flop in a pot that would be 900. While the pot odds are 3.5 to 1, my hand is very weak and plays poorly out of position against two opponents who have shown interest. Minni has been quite active with raises, and Claude's call suggests a range that likely dominates my 3-kicker. Conserving my stack for better spots in the final 25 hands is a more prudent strategy than calling with a trash hand.

Flop check with marginal ace-high. Board (3d 4s Tc) is relatively uncoordinated. A5o is decent but not strong - just ace-high. By checking, I keep the pot manageable, gather information about my opponents' hands, and maintain flexibility on later streets if I improve. With a healthy stack and first to act, checking is the prudent play to control pot size and make better decisions once I see how Minni and Flash respond.

Flop is 3♦ 4♠ T♣; I hold Q♣3♣ — a small made pair (trey) with a decent queen kicker and I’m in position. I was the preflop aggressor so I have a range advantage and this board is fairly dry: many of Claude’s calling hands (overcards, Kx/Qx/Ax, some draws) will fold to pressure. A small c-bet extracts fold equity and protects my pair against free cards. I’ll make a modest bet of 300 (~1/3–1/2 pot) to take the pot down or keep it controllable if called. If Claude folds, great. If he calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check or pot-control on scary cards, fold to a large check-raise, value-bet bigger if I improve).

Calling 300 with A5o on a dry flop against aggressive opponent's likely wide c-betting range. Pot odds (25%) are favorable, I have showdown value and improvement potential, and my stack depth allows flexibility to re-evaluate on the turn. This is a balanced play that avoids both passive folding and overcommitting with marginal strength.

Checking with A5o on a paired, dry board (3d 4s Tc 3h). This is a marginal holding - just ace-high - and I benefit from controlling pot size and gathering information. By checking, I avoid overcommitting with a vulnerable hand, maintain flexibility for the river, and let Minni reveal the strength of her holdings. If she shows aggression, I can make a more informed decision on the river based on pot odds and dynamics.

I have 3♣Q♣ on 3♦4♠T♣3♥ — trips with a queen kicker. Claude called my flop c-bet, so his calling range likely contains T-x, medium pocket pairs, some Qx/overcards and occasional floats. Very few hands beat me now (pocket tens or pocket fours that flopped sets and therefore make full houses with the turn), so this is a clear value-betting spot. I want to extract value from Tx, worse pairs and Qx while denying a free river that could change equities. Betting about 800 (~60% of the 1300 pot) builds the pot when I’m likely ahead but leaves room to call a reasonable check-raise. Plan: if Claude calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river for more value; if he check-raises a small/medium size I’ll call to realize equity and get to showdown; if he jams a very large amount representing a polarized full house range I’ll fold to avoid losing a large chunk to the very few combos that beat me.

Facing 800 with A5o on paired turn board (3d4sTc3h). Pot odds require 27.6% equity but I only have ~17.4% (8 outs on river - 4 aces + 4 deuces for wheel). Mathematically negative EV with only one card remaining and marginal implied odds. Out of position against aggressive Minni. Better spots will come in remaining 24 hands. Fold preserves chips for stronger situations.

Folding 5s8c from the button. While I have positional advantage, this hand is too weak to justify involvement. With 24 hands remaining and a chip lead (19,950), I should focus on playing premium hands where my edge is clear. Calling 100 with a hand that plays poorly post-flop is unprofitable, and raising would be overvaluing a marginal hand. Preservation of chip stack in a comfortable position is optimal strategy.

I am in the Small Blind with King-Queen offsuit (KQo), which is a very strong hand three-handed. The Button (Claude) has folded, leaving only the Big Blind (Minni) in the hand. Since I have a premium hand and there is only one opponent left, I should enter the pot with a raise to take the initiative and build a pot against Minni, who has been quite active. A standard raise to 300 (3x the big blind) is appropriate here.

Facing a small‑blind open to 300 while I’m in the big blind with 4♣9♠. To call I must put in 200 into a 400 pot, so I need ~33% equity to justify a call. This is a weak, unsuited, low card hand with poor playability and I’ll be out of position the rest of the hand. 3‑betting as a bluff is thin here — I don’t have the hand or blockers to apply profitable pressure vs a single opponent in position. Preserve my deep stack and wait for a better spot, fold preflop.

I am on the button with K9o, which is a strong opening hand in 3-handed play. My current stack is the shortest among the three players (57BB), so I need to find profitable spots to accumulate chips before the tournament ends in 22 hands. Raising to 300 is consistent with the standard open size observed in previous hands and puts pressure on the blinds while allowing me to play the hand in position if either opponent calls.

Facing a standard open to 300 from the button with the big blind yet to act, my hand 3♦4♠ is junk. I’m out of position as the small blind and calling 250 more only for a limp-in multiway pot or a heads-up OOP spot where this hand has very little playability and is frequently dominated. Immediate pot odds (250 to win ~450) still force me to have ~35–40% equity vs an opening range — far more than 3‑4 offsuit has. A bluff 3‑bet/shove isn’t attractive: Flash only has 5,400 behind and the big blind can still come along, making it a high-variance play with poor block/coverage. Better to conserve chips for a decent spot in position or with a real hand. Fold.

Calling with pocket threes from the big blind in a heads-up situation. Flash is short-stacked (5,400 chips) and raised from the button, indicating a wide range. Minni folded from the small blind. 

Analysis:
- Pot odds: 450:200 = 2.25:1, which is reasonable for set mining against a wide range
- Hand strength: Pocket pair has decent equity heads-up against Flash's likely wide button-raising range
- Position: Big blind is marginally weak, but heads-up dynamics favor calling with a playable hand
- Stack considerations: Flash at 54 BBs is shoving frequently; if I flop a set, I can stack him
- Risk/reward: Small investment for a chance to crack a weaker range and potentially win a large pot

This is a profitable call with set-mining potential and decent equity in a heads-up scenario.

Checking on the flop with pocket threes in a heads-up situation against short-stacked Flash. The board (Qh 5d 9s) is relatively dry and my hand strength is marginal. By checking, I avoid committing more chips with a weak hand while keeping Flash in the pot. This allows me to gather information on the turn, potentially hit my set, and extract value when appropriate. Against a critical short-stack, this approach maintains flexibility and prevents being exploited by a bet.

I have flopped second pair (nines) with a King kicker on a relatively dry board of Q-9-5. Claude, the big blind and chip leader, has checked to me. In three-handed play, middle pair is a solid hand but vulnerable to overcards. By betting here, I can get value from weaker hands like a five, pocket pairs below nines, or straight draws (67, 78, JT), while also protecting my hand against random overcards. If I am raised, I can comfortably fold knowing I'm likely against a Queen or better. I'll bet roughly half the pot to maintain initiative and define Claude's range.

Calling 300 with pocket threes on Qh 5d 9s. Pot odds of 4.16:1 are excellent for a pocket pair with set-mining potential against a short-stacked opponent playing a wide button range. Flash's aggression likely means a high percentage of unpaired hands. I remain flexible to hit my set or make turn decisions, while the favorable odds justify the call. Against a critical short-stack, this is a more prudent approach than raising and committing more chips with just an underpair.

Checking the turn with pocket threes. The Jack brought a dangerous card for my hand, likely improving Flash's range significantly (broadway cards, overcards, potential pairs). With no improvement on the turn and marginal hand strength, betting would be speculatively expensive. Checking preserves chips, allows me to see the river if Flash checks back, and keeps Flash in the pot if they're willing to continue. Against a short-stacked opponent, this approach maintains flexibility for final decisions on the river.

The Jack of Hearts on the turn is a significant scare card that hits several parts of Claude's calling range, potentially giving him top pair with JT or two pair with J9. While I still hold second pair, the board is now quite coordinated with straight and flush draws. Betting here doesn't accomplish much as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands, and I risk being check-raised off my equity. Checking back is the best play to control the pot size and realize my showdown value against his range.

River value bet with two pair (9s and 3s with Q kicker). Flash has checked the turn and river, indicating weakness. This is a prime spot to extract value from weaker hands like Qx, Jx, or missed draws. The bet sizing of 500 is proportional to the pot (1,250) and respects Flash's 5,100 stack while getting called by worse holdings. Checking forfeits value against this weakly-played hand.

I have improved to trips (nines) on the river with a King kicker. The board is now Qh-5d-9s-Jh-9d. Claude's lead of 500 into a pot of 1250 (a 40% pot-sized bet) after checking the turn is an interesting development. This 'donk' bet could be a value bet with a Queen or a Nine, a blocker bet with a marginal hand like a Jack, or even a slow-played straight (KT or T8) or full house (J9, Q9, 59). While trips are very strong, the presence of various straights and full houses in Claude's big-blind defending range makes raising risky. If I raise, I am primarily targeting Qx or weaker 9x, but I risk being reraised by a hand that beats me. Given I am the short stack and the tournament is nearing its conclusion, calling is the most prudent play to maximize my return while controlling risk and realizing my significant showdown value.

Qd9d on the button in a 3-handed pot is a profitable open: suited, decent kicker and I have position on both opponents. Pot is 150 and it’s only 100 to call. I want initiative and to either take the pot preflop or play postflop in position, so I’ll open to a standard size that’s consistent with the table. Raising to 300 (≈3x BB) gives solid fold equity vs the BB, can isolate the SB, and keeps the pot manageable versus Flash’s ~67bb stack. Plan: call small 3-bets to play in position, fold to large 3-bets; if called, c-bet favorable textures and use position to realize equity.

This is a straightforward fold situation. I'm holding 7s5d, one of the weakest unpaired combinations in poker. From the small blind facing Minni's raise to 300, I have several significant disadvantages:

1. **Hand strength**: 7-5 offsuit is a weak hand with poor playability and equity against most raising ranges
2. **Position**: Small blind is the worst position at the table
3. **Stack efficiency**: While I have a healthy stack (18,800), investing chips into a marginal spot is -EV
4. **Opponent profile**: Minni has been aggressive throughout the session, but that doesn't change the fact that my hand has minimal value

7-5 is not a hand to defend the blind with, especially when facing an aggressive opponent. I should preserve my chips for better opportunities later in the tournament. We're at hand 129 of 150, so there's still plenty of time to accumulate chips with premium hands.

Clean fold here.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Seven offsuit (Ac 7d). Minni, the button, has raised to 300. Claude has folded the Small Blind. I need to call 200 more into a pot of 450. Ace-Seven offsuit is a strong enough hand to defend my big blind against a button open, as button ranges are typically wide. While I could consider a three-bet (re-raise), calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has decent high-card value and some playability without bloating the pot out of position against a player with a larger stack.

On this dry flop of 5h-2d-8s, my Ace-high has significant showdown value and is often the best hand if Minni has missed. However, being out of position, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see their reaction. Leading into the button here doesn't accomplish much, as I'd likely only get called by better hands or folds from hands I already beat. I will check and evaluate based on Minni's continuation bet.

I opened to 300 preflop with Qd9d to take the initiative and Flash called from the BB. Flop 5h 2d 8s is very dry and heavily favors the raiser’s range. I have two overcards and a tiny backdoor diamond possibility, but my primary weapon here is fold equity and position. A standard continuation bet about half pot should often take this down and charges any small pairs or draws that called preflop. I’ll bet 325 to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. If I face a large check-raise I’ll give up (my hand has limited showdown value); if called I’ll use position to reevaluate on the turn and barrel selectively when the turn helps my range (diamond/Q/9) or to fold to heavy resistance otherwise.

On this dry and disconnected flop of 5h-2d-8s, my Ace-high (Ac-7d) still holds significant value against a wide button opening range. Minni's continuation bet of 325 into a 650 pot is standard for an aggressor. Since the board doesn't heavily favor her range over mine and I have two overcards, I will call to see the turn. Folding would be too exploitable on such a dry texture, and raising is unnecessary as I want to keep her bluffs in and minimize risk while out of position.

The Turn card is the Jack of Hearts, which is an overcard to the flop but does not significantly change the texture for my Ace-high. Having checked and called on the flop, I should remain consistent and check again to the pre-flop aggressor. Leading out here would likely only be called by hands that have me beaten (like a Jack or a pocket pair), whereas checking allows Minni to continue with her bluffs or check back with weaker hands, preserving my stack and keeping the pot manageable out of position.

Flop c-bet succeeded in taking the initiative but Flash’s call keeps a fairly strong, broad calling range (pairs, broadways, some draws). The turn J♥ does not improve my Q♦9♦ (my backdoor diamond is dead unless the turn was a diamond) and actually helps Flash’s calling range (Jx, AJ, TJ, two-pair combos). My hand has very little showdown value and the turn is not one I planned to barrel on — my earlier plan was to continue only when the turn materially helps my range (diamond/Q/9). Betting here would be a thin bluff with mediocre fold equity and the risk of a check-raise I’d have to fold to. I’ll control the pot and realize equity by checking back, keeping options to bluff or value-bet a favorable river (Q or diamond) and folding to strong action on a blank river.

The board has paired on the river (5h-2d-8s-Jh-2s). My Ace-high (Ac-7d) has significant showdown value against a button opening range, especially after Minni checked back the turn. Her check on the turn suggests she likely doesn't have a Jack or better. By checking the river, I can induce bluffs from missed draws or simply take the hand to showdown against her weaker Ace-high or air hands. Betting here would likely only result in folds from hands I already beat and calls from hands that have me beaten (like an 8 or a 5), so checking is the most plus-EV play to realize my equity.

I have Q♦9♦ — queen‑high with no pair and no completed flush. I c‑bet the dry flop and was called, which narrows Flash’s range to many medium pairs (8x,5x), some broadways and other hands willing to call a single c‑bet. The J on the turn helped his calling range and both of us checked the turn; his river check after the board pairs (2s) suggests pot‑control or a marginal showdown hand. A river bluff here would be thin: Flash has shown willingness to call down and there’s limited fold equity against his likely range, so betting risks getting called by any pair or Jx. The pot (1,300) is small relative to my stack, so preserving chips and showing down is the higher EV line. I’ll check back and move on to the next hand.

I'm on the button with A2o, which is a weak hand in absolute terms, but position is critical in No-Limit Hold'em, especially in the late tournament stages (hand 130 of 150). The math is favorable: I only need to call 100 into a 150 chip pot, giving me 1.5:1 odds. Even weak hands can be profitable with positional advantage postflop.

Key factors supporting this call:
1. Excellent position (button) gives me last action and best information
2. Favorable pot odds (150 into 100)
3. Ace has showdown value and can make premium hands
4. Short-stacked Flash (7475) may check back flops, allowing me to control the pot
5. If I miss badly on the flop, I can fold to aggression while preserving my large stack
6. With 18800 chips (largest at table), I can afford to see flops selectively with positional advantage

This is a profitable spot despite holding a weak hand, because position trumps hand strength in many postflop scenarios.

I am in the Small Blind with Ace-Nine offsuit (A9o). Claude, the chip leader, has limped from the button for 100. This is generally a weak play from the button, or potentially a trap, but A9o is well ahead of a button limping range. I want to raise for value and to avoid playing a multi-way pot out of position against both the chip leader and the big blind. A standard raise to 400 total (4x the big blind) is appropriate here to take initiative and build a pot with a strong starting hand.

I have 8♥2♠ in the big blind — essentially the worst starting hand. Flash (SB) has raised to 400 after Claude limped, so I’d be calling 300 into a 600 pot and likely playing a multiway pot out of position. Calling requires ~25% equity (300 into a 900 pot) but 8-2o has negligible equity vs a raising range and plays terribly postflop. Raising as a bluff here is also unattractive: I don’t block strong hands, I have little fold equity multiway, and I’d risk a big chunk of my stack out of position. With a healthy stack (13,575) and 20 hands left in the tournament, the correct move is to conserve chips and wait for a better spot. Fold.

Folding A2o despite having position and pot odds. While the initial call to 100 was justified by odds and position, Flash's raise to 400 represents strength. A2o is too weak postflop with poor kicker value, and with hand 130 of 150 and a healthy large stack (18,700), there's no need to take marginal spots. Better opportunities will present themselves where stronger holdings can utilize my stack more effectively.

I am on the button with A7 suited. This is a very strong hand for a three-handed game. I want to take control of the pot and build it early. Raising to 300 is a standard open from the button that puts pressure on the blinds while allowing me to play in position post-flop.

Facing a standard 3x open to 300 from the button while I’m in the small blind with 5c3c (offsuit low suited connectors), this is an easy fold. The pot is 450 and I need 250 more to call, so I’m getting ~35.7% pot‑equity to break even (250/(450+250)). 5‑3 suited has far less equity vs a button’s opening range (and Claude in the big blind is a frequent caller, turning this into a multiway, which further lowers my win rate). I’m out of position and have no reason to 3‑bet bluff into two players, so conserve chips and wait for a better spot in position.

Calling with J8 suited from the big blind. Flash has been aggressive and is short-stacked. J8s has good potential (straight and flush possibilities) and I'm getting 2.25:1 pot odds. More importantly, I'll have position post-flop as the aggressor acts first, allowing me to control the pot size and extract value effectively. This is a standard play against a wide button-raising range.

Checking with J♥8♥ on 6♠T♥5♥. I have a strong 9-card flush draw (~35% equity). Being out of position against a short-stacked aggressive opponent, checking is the most flexible play. This allows Flash to bet his range and lets me call profitably with my draw while retaining optionality. If Flash checks, I can bet the turn. The modest pot size (650) means I can play patiently here rather than immediately betting out of position.

On the button with Ad7d, I have an overcard and a backdoor diamond flush draw. The flop (6s-Th-5h) is relatively coordinated but doesn't hit the Big Blind's typical calling range particularly hard, though they could have middle pairs or straight draws. Following my pre-flop raise, a continuation bet of around half the pot is a standard play here to maintain initiative and potentially fold out better hands or weak pairs. A bet of 325 into 650 is a balanced size.

Calling Flash's flop bet with J♥8♥ on 6♠T♥5♥. I have approximately 15 outs (9 flush outs + 6 straight outs), giving me roughly 54-60% equity. The pot odds of 3:1 (calling 325 into 975) make this a clear profitable call. I'm getting excellent value with my strong draw, and calling keeps flexibility for turn decisions against a short-stacked opponent.

Checking on the turn with J♥8♥ on 6♠T♥5♥6♦. I have approximately 15 outs (9 flush outs + ~6 straight outs with 7s and 9s) giving me roughly 32-33% equity to the river. The turn pair changes the dynamic. By checking, I allow Flash (short-stacked) to dictate the action while maintaining flexibility. If he bets, I can evaluate pot odds for a call or fold. If he checks, I get a free river attempt to complete my draw. This is more prudent than betting out of position as a draw against an aggressive short-stacked opponent.

The turn brings a second six, pairing the board. Claude's check-call on the flop (6s-Th-5h) suggests he likely has a marginal made hand like a Ten, a Five, or a straight/flush draw. My Ace-high still has some showdown value against his draws, and by checking back, I control the pot size and get to see a free river card. Betting again risks a check-raise from a Six or a Ten, and I'm unlikely to fold out a better hand at this point given how the board has developed.

River check with J♥8♥ on 6♠T♥5♥6♦4♦. My flush and straight draws completely missed. I have just jack-high with no showdown value. Flash checked the turn on the paired board, indicating weakness, but I'm out of position with no hand to bluff effectively. Checking allows Flash to determine the action while preserving chips. If Flash bets, I can evaluate pot odds for a marginal call, but betting J-high would be injudicious bluffing. Standard river play with a missed draw against an opponent who showed turn weakness is to check and let them have the decision.

On the river 4d, the board reads 6s-Th-5h-6d-4d. I have Ace-high with a Seven kicker. Claude has checked for a third time (flop check-call, turn check, river check). While my Ace-high is unlikely to be the best hand if Claude has even a weak pair (like a 4, 5, or a low pocket pair), it does have showdown value against missed heart draws or straight draws like 89 or 78. Betting here is unlikely to fold out a Ten or a marginal pair that has decided to go to showdown, and I risk being check-raised by a trapped hand. Checking back is the most prudent play to minimize losses and see if my Ace-high is sufficient to win the pot.

I’m on the button in a 3-handed pot with 8♠6♥. With deep stacks (≈135 BB) and position, this hand is marginal but playable — in short-handed play I should open a much wider range to take the initiative and steal the blinds. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) is appropriate here: it applies pressure to the small and big blind, keeps the pot manageable with a speculative hand, and is consistent with table sizing. Postflop I’ll proceed cautiously: c-bet favorable flops, but if I face a sizeable 3-bet preflop I’ll fold this hand rather than get stacked with a weak holding.

Folding 67s from the small blind. While 67s is normally playable, I'm out of position in what will likely be a 3-way pot with marginal implied odds. With only 18 hands remaining and a healthy stack, this is a good opportunity to be selective. I should wait for stronger holdings in late tournament spots rather than playing marginal speculative hands.

I am in the Big Blind holding Ten-Seven of diamonds. Minni has raised to 300 from the Button, and the Small Blind has folded. I need to call 200 more to see a flop in a pot that will be 650, giving me pot odds of 3.25:1. T7s is a solid hand to defend with in a three-handed game, as it has good playability and post-flop potential. Given the stack depths and the price, calling to see a flop is the most standard and effective play here.

On this flop of 8d-Ks-8h, I have 10-high with a backdoor flush draw on a paired board. Minni, as the pre-flop raiser from the button, will often have a range advantage here, including Kings and potentially some 8s. Since I am out of position and have very little equity against a standard continuing range, my primary goal is to reach a showdown cheaply or potentially represent a hand if the board develops favorably. Checking is the most standard and disciplined play here, as it allows me to see how Minni reacts to this dry, paired board without committing more chips with a weak holding.

I have trips (8♠ plus 8♦/8♥ on board) — a very strong hand in a 3-handed pot. Flash checked the flop, which can be weakness or a slow-play, but a value bet here will get called by hands like Kx, mid pairs (TT-JJ), and weaker eights/overcards that want a cheap turn. I’ll lead for value with a medium-to-large c-bet (~60% pot) to build the pot and charge any draws while keeping Kx and smaller pairs in the calling range. If I face a big check-raise I’ll re-evaluate (possible KK/88), but for now bet to extract.

On a board of 8d-Ks-8h, I have missed completely with my Ten-Seven of diamonds, holding only Ten-high. Minni, as the pre-flop raiser from the button, has a significant range advantage on this board, which includes many Kings and potentially some 8s. While she could be continuation betting with air, I have very little equity to improve on future streets and calling 400 into a pot of 1050 with no real draw or pair is not profitable in the long run. Preserving my stack for better spots in the remaining hands of the tournament is the best strategy here.

With 4-8 offsuit on the button, I'm holding a very weak hand. While position is valuable, this particular hand has poor playability. The hand lacks both high card strength and good drawing potential. Even though it's only 100 to call, the risk-reward isn't favorable. Both opponents have substantial stacks and have shown aggressive tendencies throughout the tournament. There's no compelling reason to play such a marginal hand when I can wait for stronger holdings in future spots. Folding preserves my stack and positions me well for better opportunities.

I am in the Small Blind with J7o, which is a marginal hand. With only 3 players remaining and Claude having already folded from the button, the action is on me. My options are to fold, call the remaining 50 chips, or raise to attack Minni's big blind. Given that Minni has been relatively active and aggressive, and J7o is not strong enough to profitably raise or call here against a potential defense or post-flop aggression, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot to maintain my stack.

I am on the button with 8s Ts, which is a strong suited one-gapper that plays well in 3-handed play. Since I am first to act, raising is the standard play to take control of the pot and potentially win the blinds. A 3x raise to 300 has been my consistent opening size and fits the table dynamic.

I’m in the small blind with J♦2♥ facing a standard button open to 300. This is a very weak, poorly-connected offsuit hand that will often be dominated by the opener’s range and plays terribly out of position. Calling 250 more into a 450 pot to see a flop OOP gives me poor equity and opens up multi-way complications if the big blind acts. A reraise would be an unnecessary bluff from a bad position and can get me isolated or committed in spots I don’t want. Preserve my 13,875 stack and fold to the raise.

3-9 offsuit is one of the weakest hands in poker with poor playability out of position. At hand 134 of 150 with a deep stack (17,925 chips, ~179 BB), I can afford to be selective. While defending the big blind is a principle, this particular holding is too weak to profitably play against Flash's button raise when out of position. Folding preserves my stack for better spots.

I'm on the button with pocket deuces (2♥2♦). With only two blinds to beat and deep stacks, I want to take the initiative and either steal the blinds or isolate one caller while preserving strong implied odds for set-mining. A standard open to 300 (about 3x the BB and consistent with table sizing) gives good fold equity and keeps postflop play in position. Plan: if called, play postflop for a set or use position to c-bet small on dry boards and pot-control on wet boards; call a small 3-bet to set-mine but fold to an oversized 3-bet/4-bet unless I improve.

AhTh is a premium hand (strong ace with flush draw potential). I'm in the small blind facing Minni's button raise to 300. I have a commanding 17,875 chip stack, and Flash is short (8,100). I should apply aggressive pressure with this strong holding. A raise to 850 total accomplishes several goals: (1) builds the pot with a strong hand, (2) tests Minni's hand strength and willingness to continue, (3) potentially takes it down pre-flop or gets me to a favorable heads-up or 3-way flop. This sizing is assertive but reasonable given the stage of tournament (hand 135/150) and stack dynamics. Flash being short-stacked makes isolation less critical, but the raise still provides good fold equity and establishes that I'm willing to play back at Minni's aggressive button raising.

I am in the big blind with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a strong starting hand in a 3-handed game. There has been an open from Minni (Button) to 300 and a 3-bet from Claude (Small Blind) to 850. While AJ is strong, a 3-bet from the small blind against a button open usually indicates a very strong range (AQs+, AK, TT+). However, in this tournament, Claude has been active. Folding feels too tight for this stack depth and hand strength. Calling is an option, but it puts me out of position against two players or specifically against a potential 4-better. 4-betting could work, but given the stacks, I would likely be committing myself or forced to fold to a shove. Given the current pot odds and the strength of AJ in 3-handed play, calling the 850 and seeing a flop is a reasonable middle-ground approach, although the range I'm facing is significant. I will call the 850 total.

Call to set-mine. Claude’s 3‑bet to 850 with the BB cold‑calling creates a multiway pot — this is not a spot to 4‑bet or isolate with pocket deuces. My remaining investment (550) is small relative to my 13.6k stack and offers good implied odds if I hit a set. Calling preserves position and keeps the pot controllable. Postflop plan: look for a set; on overcard‑heavy boards play cautiously (check/fold to big pressure); on dry/low boards use position to pot‑control or take small stabs. If Claude jams or 4‑bets to an unreasonably large sizing preflop I will fold.

Continuation betting top pair with a gutshot and backdoor flush draw on a relatively dry board. AhTh is premium and likely ahead after raising pre-flop. Betting 1,200 into the 2,550 pot (~47% pot) achieves multiple objectives: (1) builds pot with strongest hand, (2) extracts value from weaker holdings, (3) protects against draw completions, (4) maintains aggressive image. Out of position with two opponents, but my hand strength justifies aggression here. Sizing is standard and exploitable, but my hand merits it.

Pre-flop, I called a 3-bet with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a marginal call in a 3-way pot but acceptable given the odds. On the flop of 8c-Ks-2s, I have missed completely, holding only Ace high. The pre-flop aggressor (Claude) has led out for nearly half the pot (1200 into 2550). This board favors a 3-betting range which includes many Kings (AK, KQ, KJs). Additionally, Minni is still to act behind me. Continuing with Ace high against a lead into two opponents on a King-high board is negative expected value. I have no significant draws and only one overcard to the King, which might not even be clean if Claude has AK. I will fold and preserve my remaining 7350 chips.

I have flopped a set of deuces (2h2d on 8c Ks 2s) and am in position heads‑up vs Claude after he tossed out a small c‑bet (1200 into a ~3750 pot). This is a classic spot to build the pot and protect my hand — the K on board hits his range heavily and there are backdoor spade possibilities, so I don’t want to give free cards. I’ll raise for value and protection: a size of 3600 total (raising by 2400 over his bet) is large enough to charge Kx and two‑card spade/straight draws while not overbetting and folding out only the very weakest hands. If Claude shoves or fires a huge 3‑bet, I’ll re‑evaluate (but I’m usually calling big with a set). Plan if called: continue extracting value on later streets and deny equity to draws.

Calling 2400 to see the turn. With AhTh on 8c Ks 2s, I have Ace-high with 6 direct outs to make a pair. Pot odds of roughly 3.06:1 require only 24% equity, which I likely have against Minni's mixed range. While Minni's flop raise suggests strength (likely holds Kings or better in significant portion of range), I have sufficient showdown value and equity to call and keep the hand alive. Folding wastes my pre-flop investment and this street's action. Raising is unfavorable given my out-of-position, likely-behind situation. The call allows me to reassess on the turn card.

On the turn with Ah Th on 8c Ks 2s 5s, I have a strong flush draw with overcard equity (~15 outs) but I'm likely behind Minni's likely Kings or strong AK holding. Rather than committing more chips while behind, I check to control the pot, gain information about Minni's hand strength, and keep alive my many outs for the river. This allows me to reassess his turn action before investing further. The flush draw combined with overcards gives me sufficient equity to play the river if Minni bets, but checking first is the more disciplined approach given my likely-behind status pre-river.

I have a set of deuces on 8c Ks 2s 5s and Claude checked the turn. His range (3‑bet preflop, led the flop and then called my raise) contains lots of Kx and medium pairs plus some two‑spade combos and occasional pocket 5s/KK that can beat me. Checking lets him realize equity and potentially see a safe river, so I want to extract value and protect my hand. A firm value bet of ~4000 (~40% of the 9750 pot) charges Kx and worse pairs while not pot‑committing me if Claude check‑raises or shoves. If he calls I plan to extract more on the river when appropriate; if he jams or check‑raises very large I’ll re‑evaluate (but will often call with trips). Bet 4000 to get value and deny a free river.

Calling with Ah Th on 8c Ks 2s 5s. I have 11 outs (8 heart flush draws + 3 aces for top pair), providing ~24% equity which slightly exceeds the 22.5% pot odds required. As chip leader with 15 hands remaining in the tournament, marginal +EV spots should be taken, especially against short-stacked Minni who may have desperation/bluff hands. Even losing doesn't cripple my tournament position (would leave 9,475 chips). The call keeps the hand alive with multiple outs while maintaining tournament position.

Checking on the river with Ah Th (Ace-high) is the disciplined play. Minni's line suggests a strong holding (likely Kings or premium broadway). My hand is marginal and likely behind. Checking allows me to: (1) keep the pot under control, (2) see if Minni bets and evaluate the specific bet size/action, (3) potentially get to showdown cheaply, (4) avoid committing more chips with a likely-behind hand. With only 15 hands left, preserving chips with marginal holdings is critical. If Minni checks behind, I win the pot and move forward. If they bet, I can reassess based on sizing and stack-to-pot ratios.

I have a set of deuces on 8c Ks 2s 5s 7d — an extremely strong showdown hand. The only realistic hands that beat me are very narrow (pocket 5s/KK/full house combos or a two‑card spade flush), while Claude’s line (3‑bet preflop, lead/called on the flop, check‑call on the turn) reads largely like Kx/solid one‑pair hands or medium pairs that will happily call value on the river. The pot is huge (17,750) and I have 5,425 behind. I want to maximize value and get it all in now: shoving extracts the most from Kx and other calling hands, gives some fold equity, and keeps the decision simple against the small set of hands that beat me. I shove my remaining stack — if he folds I take the pot, if he calls I expect to be ahead vs most of his calling range.

Calling 5,425 on the river with AhTh on 8c Ks 2s 5s 7d. Pot odds of 4.27:1 require only ~19% equity to break even. Against Minni's likely range (weighted toward KK, KQ, top pairs), I estimate ~25% equity, making this a marginal +EV call. With 15 hands remaining in the tournament and my chip stack able to absorb a loss, this price is acceptable for a hand with decent showdown value and sufficient equity.

Short-stacked at 40 BB on the button with T9o. Folding hands constantly will result in being blinded out before reaching premium cards. T9o has reasonable playability in position with two broadway cards and potential for top pair or straights. Both opponents are deeper stacked - they may fold to post-flop aggression. Need to accumulate chips with calculated risk-taking rather than folding into the blind levels. Calling 100 more is the survival-focused play with hand that has equity and position advantage.

Claude limped from the button, and I am in the small blind with 8s 5s. The cost to call is only 50 chips into a pot of 250, giving me 5:1 pot odds. This is a very favorable price to see a flop with a suited hand that has some connectivity. I will call the remaining 50 chips to see the flop, as folding would be too tight and raising against the big stack (Minni) and a short stack (Claude) isn't ideal with this hand.

I have 7♠4♥ — a very weak, low-equity hand. Both the SB (Flash) and Button (Claude) limped, so this is a multiway pot and I’m out of position. I’ve already posted the big blind, the pot is small, and there’s no raise. I’ll check to see a cheap flop rather than bloating the pot with garbage. Plan: fold to significant aggression unless I hit a real pair/two-pair or a strong draw that’s cheap to continue. Preserve my large stack and wait for better spots.

The flop of 7h Qd Jh is quite coordinated and does not connect with my 8s 5s. In a three-way limped pot, it is highly likely that one of my opponents has hit a pair or has a strong draw. With no immediate draws and only 8-high, there is no merit in betting here. I will check and likely fold to any significant aggression, as I am out of position and have very little equity.

Board: Jh Qd 7h. I hold 7s4h — bottom pair with a weak kicker and a backdoor heart. This is a limp-pot (two callers) and I’m out of position to the button. Betting here has thin value (I’ll get value mainly from worse small pairs) and exposes me to raises from Qx/Jx or stronger hands; with two opponents the fold equity of a small c-bet is low. I want to keep the pot controlled and see a cheap turn so I can fold to real aggression or continue only if I improve or face a very small bet. Stack sizes (Claude ~3950, Flash ~7250) mean a raise could commit a lot — not a spot to build a big pot with a marginal pair. Action: check and plan to fold to significant aggression, call small bets to realize equity or re-evaluate on the turn if I improve.

Betting T9o on 7-Q-J flop after both opponents checked. Open-ended straight draw (8 outs) with two overcards gives strong equity. Position on button with weaker opponents allows post-flop aggression. Short stack requires chip accumulation with premium hands - this qualifies. Bet size of 225 applies pressure while keeping hand playable for all-in decisions on later streets. Expected fold equity from weakness shown by checking, plus actual equity if called.

On a flop of 7h-Qd-Jh, my hand (8s 5s) has missed completely. There are no straight draws or flush draws available for my specific cards, and with both a Jack and a Queen on the board, it is highly probable that one of my two opponents has connected with at least a pair or a strong draw. Claude has bet 225 into a pot of 300, and Minni is still to act behind me. Given I have zero equity and poor position, folding is the only logical play.

I have bottom pair (7s) with a terrible 4 kicker on J♥ Q♦ 7♥. Flash folded so it's heads‑up vs Claude, who bet a relatively small 225 into a 525 pot. Raising out of position with this marginal hand and weak kicker is unnecessary and often gets me committed to a losing spot against Jx/Qx or better pairs. Calling is cheap: I must call 225 to contest a final pot (if I call) of ~975, so break‑even equity is only ~23%, and against a limp‑then‑bet range I have enough equity to see a turn. Plan: call 225 to realize equity and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to sizable aggression if I don't improve (two‑pair/trips or a strong draw), otherwise proceed cautiously.

Board J♥ Q♦ 7♥ 9♣. I hold 7♠4♥ — bottom pair with a terrible 4 kicker, heads‑up vs Claude and out of position. The pot is already ~750 and I checked the flop and called a small flop stab; betting now would bloat the pot and polarize my range while getting called or raised by many hands that beat me (Qx, Jx, better pairs, or two‑pair/straight draws). I want to control the pot and realize my thin equity. Plan: check the turn, see Claude’s action, call only very small bets to see a cheap river, and fold to significant aggression unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or get a clear draw.

Turn bet with made hand and strong equity. Pair of nines represents the strongest holding likely shown by Minni's check. Open-ended straight draw provides significant backup equity if called (8 outs). Short stack requires applying pressure to win pots rather than checking down. Bet size of 400 applies meaningful pressure while remaining reasonable relative to pot and stack. If called, have ~50% equity vs most hands. If folded, significant pot win contributes to stack preservation.

I have 7♠4♥ — bottom pair with a terrible kicker, out of position vs Claude. He c-bet the flop (225) and barreled the turn (400), so his range includes a lot of Qx/Jx, some two‑pair/sets, and occasional turn‑improved straight/8‑10 type hands or bluffs. The pot is 1150 and I must call 400, so my break‑even equity is only ~26%. Bottom pair has enough showdown equity to justify a call here to realize equity and keep the pot from ballooning unnecessarily. Raising would be a thin bluff/polarizing line with very little fold equity and would commit me out of position with a marginal hand. Plan: call the 400, see the river, fold to large river aggression unless I improve to two‑pair/trips or get a very cheap river and can extract value/shove against obvious bluffs.

I have bottom pair (7♠) with a terrible 4 kicker on J♥ Q♦ 7♥ 9♣ 6♦. I checked the flop and called the turn to control the pot and realize equity; the river didn’t improve me. Claude’s line (flop bet 225, turn 400) is weighted toward Qx/Jx/9x and other hands that beat a single 7 — betting now would polarize my range, get called by better hands, and risk a raise into a very uncomfortable spot. There’s no river bet to force a decision, so I exercise pot control and go to showdown. If Claude jams or bets big I’d fold; if he bets a very small amount I could consider a call, but with no bet I check.

River showdown with pair of nines after Minni checks to me. This is a marginal made hand - beats air/missed draws but loses to any Q, J, or 7. Minni's river check indicates weakness. Betting here has poor risk-reward: either she folds small air (minimal gain) or calls/raises with better hand (chip loss). With critically short stack (33 BB), preserving chips and taking showdown with a made hand is more efficient than speculative value betting. Check allows me to see the result while protecting my stack for higher-equity situations ahead with premium cards.

I am on the button with King-Queen of diamonds, which is a premium starting hand in a 3-handed game. My stack is roughly 72 big blinds, and I have position on both opponents. Standard strategy dictates a raise here to build the pot and potentially take down the blinds. A standard raise to 300 has been consistent throughout this match.

I have Q♠T♣ in the small blind facing a standard button open to 300. QTo is playable three‑handed but marginal to 3-bet out of position — I’d prefer to keep the pot manageable and see a flop. I need to put 250 more (300 total including my SB 50) to call; that’s about a 35–36% break‑even equity which is reasonable versus a wide BTN opening. If the big blind folds I’ll have position vs the opener; if not I can proceed cautiously post‑flop and fold to heavy aggression when I miss. So I’ll call and see a flop.

Folding Q9o in a 3-way pot with a medium-short stack. This is a marginal hand that will struggle against Flash's button range and Minni's call. With limited chips (4,775), I should preserve my stack for premium hands where I have a clear equity advantage. The risk/reward profile is unfavorable here, and being out of position post-flop would put me in difficult spots with a weak holding.

Flop is 5h 9h 5d and I hold Q♠T♣ — I completely missed. This is a paired, relatively dry board with two hearts, so villain can have fives, a 9, or a lot of random overcards and bluffs. I’m out of position to the preflop opener, so I want to keep the pot small with a marginal hand. Leading here bloats a pot OOP and just gets me raised off my equity. Best line is to check and exercise pot control: give the button the chance to c-bet with air (which I can call small bets) or check back so I can see a free turn. I will check and fold to heavy aggression, call small probes, and re-evaluate on the turn if I pair up or pick up equity.

I am the pre-flop aggressor on the button with two overcards (Kd, Qd) on a paired board (5h, 9h, 5d). After Minni checks from the small blind, a standard continuation bet is the most appropriate play to maintain initiative and potentially fold out better hands or random air. A bet of 300 into a 700-chip pot is large enough to be effective but small enough to preserve my stack if I encounter resistance.

Flop bet is small (300 into a 1000 pot — ~23% break‑even equity). I completely missed with Q♠T♣ and I'm out of position, but this is a cheap probe bet from the button that can include lots of bluffs and thin value (5x, 9x, pocket pairs, hearts, overcards). Folding here surrenders good pot odds and is too tight; raising OOP with no equity is unnecessarily risky. I’ll call 300 to keep the pot controlled and see a turn, plan to fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and only continue aggressively if I pair Q/T or pick up a clear favorable card/blocker on the turn.

I completely missed (QsTc) on 5h‑9h‑5d‑2s and am out of position. I called the small flop c‑bet to keep the pot controlled and see a cheap turn; the 2♠ is a blank for me and didn’t give any meaningful equity. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and fold out only the weakest hands while getting value/raised by the hands that beat me (5x, 9x, sets, or some hearts). With a large stack advantage and no clear fold equity, pot control is the correct line — check back and evaluate the river. If Flash fires the river I’ll fold to a large value bet, call small probes, and only continue aggressively if I pair or pick up a favorable card/blocker.

On a paired board of 5h-9h-5d-2s, I have King-Queen high. After Minni called my flop continuation bet, her range likely includes pocket pairs, nines, or heart draws. By checking back on the turn, I control the size of the pot and take a free card to try and improve to top pair on the river. Checking also allows me to potentially win at showdown against her missed draws or lower high cards without risking more chips on a bluff that might be called by any pair.

I completely missed with Q♠T♣ and have no hearts or pair on 5h‑9h‑5d‑2s‑Kh. I pot‑controlled earlier and this river card (K♥) both pairs the board and completes many heart draws, improving Flash’s range to Kx and flushes in addition to the one‑pair hands that already bet the flop. Betting OOP here would bloat the pot and get called by most hands that beat me (5x, 9x, Kx, any flush) while folding out only the very weakest air — poor EV. With a large stack and no showdown value, the correct line is to keep the pot small: check and fold to a meaningful river bet (only calling a tiny probe). Therefore I’ll check and show down if Flash also checks.

I hit top pair (Kings) on the river with a Queen kicker. While this is a very strong hand in a three-handed game, the river card (King of Hearts) also completed the heart flush draw. Minni has checked three times post-flop (including this river check). While I likely have the best hand, a value bet might only be called by hands that beat me (such as a slow-played 5 or a completed flush) or folded by hands I already beat (like a 9 or a mid-pocket pair). Given the tournament stage and my stack size, checking back is the safest way to realize my showdown value and win the pot without risking a check-raise.

I’m on the button with A♦7♠ in a 3‑handed pot (pot 150, 100 to call). This is a late‑position hand with a blocker to big Aces and decent playability postflop, so I want to take the initiative. Standard open sizing at this table is about 3x the BB, so I’ll raise to 300 to maximize fold equity, isolate heads‑up where possible, and play the hand in position. If I get a single call I’ll proceed aggressively on favorable flops; if I face a large 3‑bet (strong sizing) I’ll fold A7o since it’s often dominated. Raising to 300 keeps the pot controlled while applying pressure to SB (stack 4,725) and BB (7,850).

Short stack push with JT. Broadway hand has good equity against Minni's wide button range. At ~47BB, this is squarely in push/fold territory. JT is too strong to fold and calling leaves awkward spot postflop. Shoving now maximizes fold equity against worse hands while having decent equity if called.

I am holding 3c 7d, which is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Minni opened with a raise from the button, and Claude has moved all-in (or put in a very large raise to 4725). Even though the pot odds might seem tempting if the stack sizes are fluctuating, 3-7 offsuit has almost no equity against the ranges of two opponents who have already committed chips. With only 13 hands remaining in the tournament and me sitting in second place, it is vital to preserve my stack rather than gambling on a bottom-tier hand. Folding is the only sensible play here.

Claude responded to my button open (300) with a massive 3‑bet to 4,725, Flash folded and it’s heads‑up. The pot is 5,125 and I must call 4,425 (i.e. I need ~46% equity to justify a call). A♦7♠ is marginal — it’s often dominated by the kinds of hands that make a sizing like this (AA/KK/QQ/AK/JJ). While my Ace is a blocker, it doesn’t give me nearly enough equity to call a near‑all‑in 3‑bet, and 4‑bet shoving here with A7o is too thin vs a polarized range. With three players left in the tournament and a healthy stack, preserving chips and waiting for a better spot is the correct play. I fold.

Folding 4-10 offsuit on the button. While I have positional advantage, this is a very weak hand with minimal equity against two opponents. At 52BB deep with 11 hands remaining in the tournament, I should preserve my stack for stronger hands. The risk-to-reward ratio is unfavorable—calling 100 to win 150 with a hand this weak is a mathematically poor spot. Waiting for premium holdings in remaining hands is the correct approach.

I am in the Small Blind with 6d 4c, a weak hand. The pot is 150 and it costs 50 more to call, or I could raise to 200 total. Minni is in the Big Blind and has a massive stack (26,875), making her quite dangerous to play against out of position. Given the late stage of the tournament (Hand 139/150) and my weak holdings, there is no reason to inflate the pot or attempt a marginal call. Folding is the standard disciplined play here.

I am on the button with Ten-Four suited. While suited cards have some playability, Ten-Four is quite weak for a standard opening range, even in a three-handed game. Claude in the Big Blind has a stack of about 50 big blinds, which is enough to put me in a difficult spot if he defends or 3-bets. Minni, the chip leader in the Small Blind, has been playing very aggressively. Given that we are near the end of the tournament (hand 140 of 150) and I have a healthy stack to maintain my position, I will choose to fold this hand and wait for a better spot rather than risking chips on a marginal steal attempt.

Qh2c is a weak, offsuit hand and I’m out of position against the big blind. There’s only 50 more to call into a 150 pot (good pot odds) and raising to try and steal risks a shove or a big 3-bet from Claude (stack 5075) that I don’t want to face with trash. I’ll take the low-variance line: call the 50 (total committed 100) to see a flop and proceed only if I hit or get a clear foldable opportunity; otherwise fold to strong aggression.

With K-5o in the big blind against Minni's small blind call, I check to see a flop. This marginal hand has some playability with the King, especially given Minni's wide calling range. By checking, I can evaluate the flop texture and respond accordingly. With a short stack near the end of the tournament, I should look to see flops with reasonable holdings while remaining prepared to fold if the situation warrants it.

Preflop I called 50 as a low-variance attempt to see a flop and planned to proceed only if I hit or had a clear foldable opportunity. The flop (7c 8c 3h) misses Qh2c entirely: I have no pair and only a remote backdoor club flush that needs both turn+river clubs (very low equity). With essentially air and a big stack advantage, I don’t want to bloat the pot or invest chips bluffing into a live big blind who can easily call or shove. I’ll check to control the pot and fold to significant aggression; I’ll consider calling a very small probe to see a turn if the price is attractive, but otherwise give up this hand and re-evaluate on the turn if I pick up equity.

K5 is a marginal hand with no current strength on this coordinated flop. Minni's flop check suggests weakness or a trap. Betting K-high out of position creates difficult situations - if called, I'm out-of-position with a weak hand; if raised, I fold. With a short stack near tournament end, controlling pot size with a check is superior strategy, allowing me to see the turn while managing risk.

I have Qh2c on 7c 8c 3h 3d — no pair and only a two-card backdoor club possibility (needs both turn+river clubs). The pot is tiny (200) relative to both stacks. Claude has shown a tendency to call down and is short enough that a bet could either be called too often or induce a shove I don’t want to call with air. A small probe may fold some hands, but against this opponent I expect low fold equity and don’t want to bloat the pot with essentially zero showdown equity. I’ll control the pot and see a river card: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re-evaluating only if I pick up real equity or a favorable price on a small probe.

With K5o (K♣5♣) against a paired board (7♣8♣3♥3♦), I have K-high with no made hand strength. The turn brought the 3♦, pairing the board but not improving my hand. Both players checked the flop, suggesting weakness from both opponents. Betting K-high from out of position risks either being called by a better hand or facing a raise that forces a difficult decision with my short stack (5,075 chips). With only ~10 hands remaining in the tournament and needing to preserve chips for stronger spots, checking to the river is the prudent play. This allows me to potentially win at showdown if Minni has nothing, while avoiding further investment in a marginal hand. If Minni bets the river, I can make a more informed decision based on pot odds and her action patterns.

The runout (7c 8c 3h 3d Tc) leaves me with Qh2c — no pair and only a four‑card club situation (no flush). I have essentially zero showdown value. The pot is tiny (200) relative to both stacks and Claude (BB, 5075) has shown a tendency to call down; my fold equity bluffing the river is low and I risk a shove/call that I don’t want to face with air. This matches my earlier plan to check and fold to meaningful aggression. I’ll conserve chips, check to showdown, and fold if Claude fires a significant bet.

With only 10 hands remaining and a short stack of 5,075 chips, I must preserve capital for premium situations. Minni checked the entire hand (flop, turn, river), indicating weakness. My K-high has showdown value against her likely-weak range. Betting would risk chips unnecessarily on a marginal bluff when I'm potentially ahead in the hand right now. Checking allows me to reach showdown, win the pot if she has nothing, and minimize losses if she does have something. This conservative approach maximizes my tournament equity in the critical final stretch.

Open-raise to 300 (3x BB). Pocket deuces are a small pair but on the button with two blinds to steal from and deep effective stacks this is a standard +EV play: it gives fold equity, respects my position, and still allows set-mining if called. At this table my 300 opens are well within the local sizing and will often isolate one opponent or take the blinds down. If I face a modest 3-bet I’ll consider calling for implied odds (effective stacks are ~50–77BB), but I will fold to a large reraise that prices out set-mining. Postflop I’ll look to extract value when I hit a set and play cautiously (check/fold or pot control) when I miss against heavy aggression.

I'm in the small blind with 9cJh against Minni's button raise to 300. My stack is 5225 chips (about 17 big blinds), which is relatively short. I need to call 250 more to see the flop with decent pot odds (250 to win 450 + my 50 already posted = 3-to-1 odds).

9J is a reasonable broadway hand with decent equity against a wide button raising range. Given my short stack situation and the hand strength, calling is better than folding. This allows me to:
1. See a flop with favorable odds
2. Potentially hit top pair or a strong draw
3. Make decisions post-flop based on the board texture

Raising would commit too many chips with a hand that plays better in position with information. Folding would be overly tight given the hand strength and pot odds available.

I am in the Big Blind holding King-Nine offsuit. Minni opened for 300 from the Button and Claude called from the Small Blind. The pot currently stands at 700, and it costs me 200 more to call. This gives me pot odds of 3.5-to-1, meaning I only need about 22% equity to make this a profitable call. K9o performs well enough against a Button opening range and a Small Blind flat-calling range to justify seeing a flop, especially given the excellent price. Raising (squeezing) is an option but unnecessarily risky given the chip leader (Minni) is involved and we are close to the end of the tournament. I will call.

Betting top pair (Jack-9) on a relatively dry flop with Js 8h 2h. Position-wise, I'm acting first but have shown strength pre-flop (calling two others). A 350-chip bet (roughly 40% of the 900-chip pot) is appropriate value betting sizing to get worse hands to fold while extracting value from weaker holdings. This maintains pressure without overcommitting given my short stack of 4975 chips.

On a flop of Jack-Eight-Deuce with two hearts, Claude has led out into two opponents. My King-high has very little showdown value and no significant draws on this board. Since I am caught between a bettor and the chip leader (Minni) who is yet to act, calling with only King-high is a losing play. I will fold and preserve my stack for the final few hands of the tournament.

I flopped a set (2c2d on Js 8h 2h) — very strong and relatively disguised. Flash folded so I’m heads-up with Claude (SB) who made a small c-bet (350 into a ~1250 pot), which is a wide, often weak/medium range (Jx, floats, some two‑heart combos, small pairs). I want to extract value and charge two‑heart flush draws while denying cheap turn cards. I’ll raise for value and protection — sized large enough to charge draws but not needlessly commit my huge stack: raise to 1600. If Claude shoves I’ll call (set is almost always best); if he calls I’ll continue extracting on safe turns and re-evaluate on a heart/jack. This line maximizes value vs his wide c-bet range and forces drawing hands to pay dearly.

Calling with top pair (J9) on a relatively dry flop against Minni's aggressive 3-bet. The pot odds are approximately 3.3:1 (1250 to win 4100), which justifies the call with top pair. My J9 has reasonable equity against Minni's likely range (which could include JK/JQ, smaller pairs, or semi-bluffs with heart draws). The flop is dry with limited coordinated cards, and calling preserves my short stack while keeping the hand alive. With 10 hands to play, I need to accumulate chips, and this is a reasonable spot to do so. The hand has decent showdown value and I'm not committing my entire stack.

With top pair on a dry board but vulnerable to Minni's strong flop aggression, checking is optimal given my short stack (3,375 chips). Minni's massive 1,600 flop raise into ~900 pot suggests a strong holding (likely a set). I've already committed significant chips. Checking:
1) Allows a free river card if Minni checks back
2) Preserves chips in case I'm beat
3) Gets strength information if Minni bets (easier decision then)
4) With only 10 hands left, survival and accumulation are key priorities

Top pair has showdown value but isn't strong enough to bet/fold, and my position as a short stack makes information gathering preferable to aggressive commitment right now.

I flopped a disguised set of deuces and raised the flop to charge a wide calling range; Claude called. The turn (4s) is a blank and he checks, so this is a perfect spot to continue extracting value and deny him a cheap river. Claude's calling range likely includes Jx, 8x, single small pairs and floats — hands that will call a sizable turn bet but fold to an overbet. I will bet for value and protection: ~60% pot (2500) to charge one-pair hands and get value from worse pairs/turned boats while still keeping worse hands in. If he shoves I will call with my set; if he calls I’ll reevaluate on the river.

Folding top pair J9 with a short stack and 9 hands remaining. Minni's aggressive flop 3-bet (1600 into 900) followed by a strong turn bet (2500 into 6600) indicates a premium holding. Her line is consistent with sets or top pair with a better kicker. While pot odds are close to breakeven, J9 is likely only ~30-35% equity against her range—barely profitable at best. Preserving my 3,375-chip stack allows me to find better equity spots (premium pairs, premium broadway) in the remaining 9 hands. This is better than committing 73% of my stack as a slight underdog in flip territory.

Folding 4sJd with a critical short stack. While position is excellent, this is a weak, off-suit hand that plays poorly post-flop. With only 34 BB and two active opponents, I should preserve my chips and wait for premium hands with better equity and fold equity. Range advantage doesn't overcome fundamental hand weakness in this spot.

I am in the Small Blind with pocket threes (3c, 3d). Claude has folded on the button, leaving me heads-up against Minni, the chip leader. A pocket pair is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. My strategy here is to raise to 300 to exert pressure on the Big Blind and potentially take down the pot immediately. While pocket threes are vulnerable to overcards on the flop, they hold significant equity against a random range. Given that there are only a few hands left in the tournament and I am safely in second place, I want to play aggressively but cautiously against the massive stack of Minni.

Heads-up vs Flash (SB) who opened to 300. I hold 6♠9♣ — a playable hand in heads-up with decent equity vs a wide SB opening range. The pot is ~400 and I need to call 200 more (making my total 300) — about 3:1 pot odds, so I only need ~25% equity to justify a call. Raising with this marginal hand OOP isn’t necessary; I’ll keep the pot manageable, call to see a flop and proceed cautiously (fold to a large 3-bet, otherwise evaluate flop for continuation or pot control).

On a board of 8h-6h-Ks, my pocket threes are now an underpair. As the pre-flop aggressor out of position against the massive chip leader Minni, I have to be cautious. This board hits a lot of a defending Big Blind's range, including many 8x, 6x, and flush draws. Betting here (C-betting) risks getting called or raised by a wider range because Minni can afford to gamble with her stack. Given there are only 8 hands remaining and I hold a significant lead over Claude for second place, I want to avoid unnecessary chip loss. I will check to see how Minni reacts; if she bets, I will likely fold, as my hand has very little equity to improve and is beat by almost any pair she holds.

I have middle pair (6s) on K♠-8♥-6♥ against a wide SB opening range in heads-up. Flash checked the flop, which often indicates a missed broadway or a draw; betting protects my pair against heart draws and charges any floats, while also getting value from worse one-pair hands and some backdoor draws. I’ll make a standard half-pot c-bet (300) to keep the pot manageable and deny free cards. If I face a large raise I’ll give up (fold); I’ll re-evaluate if called on the turn.

On a flop of 8h-6h-Ks, my pocket threes are now an underpair and highly vulnerable. I checked to Minni, the chip leader, who responded with a half-pot bet. Given the coordination of the board (flush and straight draws) and the high probability that her defending range includes a King, an Eight, a Six, or a superior pocket pair, my hand has very little equity. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant lead over Claude for second place, it is more important to preserve my stack than to make a speculative call out of position with a weak underpair. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with pocket sevens, which is a very strong hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy (72 BBs), while Claude is relatively short-stacked (32.75 BBs). I want to open for a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially thin the field or win the blinds. Pocket sevens play well post-flop, and I am in a good position to either pick up the pot now or navigate a flop against one or both opponents.

Fold. Facing a button open to 300 from Flash, I hold 7♠5♣ — a weak, disconnected offsuit hand. I’ve already posted the small blind, so calling 250 into a 450 pot would leave me out of position against the button with poor equity (I’d need roughly ~36% to make a call profitable), and there’s a realistic chance Claude (BB, ~32BB) could defend or shove and create a difficult spot. 3-betting as a bluff isn’t attractive with this holding — it’s a marginal hand with poor blockers and little post‑flop playability. With a big stack and many hands left, I’ll preserve chips and wait for a better spot.

With only 7 hands remaining and a critically short stack of 3275, I need to be extremely selective with my holdings. I'm holding 4d3c - the worst hand type in poker (offsuit unpaired weak cards with no connectivity). 

Flash raised to 300 from the button after Minni folded from the small blind. While Flash likely has a wide range from button position, 43o has minimal equity against even a very wide raising range. Even against 50% of hands, 43o would be a significant underdog.

More importantly, with my dwindling chip stack and only 7 hands left, I cannot afford to make speculative calls with trash hands. I need to preserve my chips for premium holdings like pocket pairs, high cards, or strong broadway combinations that give me a real chance to win pots.

This is a clear fold. The 200 I'd call is better spent waiting for a spot where I have actual equity and tournament viability.

Button with A♥6♦ (A6o) — no raises, only blinds posted. Open-raising is +EV here: I want to apply pressure, steal the blinds, and isolate the shorter SB (Claude, ~32bb) while playing in position versus Flash (BB, ~72bb). I’ll raise to 300 (≈3xBB), a standard table size that preserves fold equity and leaves room for post‑flop maneuvering. Postflop plan: continue‑bet frequently heads‑up and use position to probe; if both call I’ll play more cautiously and target favorable boards; fold to a large, crisp 3‑bet (especially from the deeper stack) and only proceed vs small squeezes when pot odds/implied odds justify it.

Calling with pocket deuces in the small blind against Minni's button raise.
