With 6 hands left in the tournament and a short stack of 3,225, I need to take reasonable spots to accumulate chips rather than fold down. Pocket deuces has decent equity against Minni's likely wide button-raising range. The 250 call commits only about 7.7% of my stack to see a flop with a hand that can win a pot or hit trips. After calling I'll have 2,975 remaining, giving me options on subsequent streets. The pot odds and endgame urgency support taking this spot.

I am in the Big Blind with Trey-Five of Spades. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, and the Small Blind (Claude) has called. While the pot odds are attractive (200 to call into a pot of 700), 3-5 suited is an extremely weak hand to play out of position in a multi-way pot. With the tournament nearing its end (hand 144 of 150) and Minni playing very aggressively as the chip leader, I choose to fold and preserve my 72.5 BB stack rather than risk chips in a low-equity situation where I am unlikely to realize my equity post-flop.

Pocket deuces completely miss this 4-7-8 flop. Out of position, short stack, and facing two opponents. Betting with no equity is burning chips. Checking preserves my stack, gathers information cheaply, and keeps the pot small - allowing me to fold to aggression without significant loss. Better to win pots in stronger situations in the remaining 6 hands.

I raised preflop with A6o and got a head‑up call from the shorter SB (Claude). Flop 4♥‑7♠‑8♦ is fairly dry but connected — I missed, I only have Ace‑high and a gutshot to a 5 (4 outs). Versus one defender I should continue‑bet frequently to fold out weaker Ax and small pairs and deny free cards to draws. Pot is 700, so I’ll bet ≈50–60% pot to apply pressure but not overcommit; 400 is a comfortable size that charges draws and gets value vs worse hands. If he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed with pot control (or fold to strong check‑raise/shove if he shows heavy aggression).

Folding pocket deuces on the 4h-7s-8d flop. With only 2 outs to trips against Minni's likely strong holdings, I'm getting bad odds in a 3-way pot. My deuces lack showdown value and with 2,975 chips and 6 hands remaining, I should preserve my stack for premium hands or better equity situations. Calling 400 leaves 2,575 and likely commits me to more chips if the turn is a blank. This is correct endgame chip management.

Premium hand (AK) from button with short stack. Aggressive raise to 400 total applies pressure on both blinds, takes down pot often, and gets value when called. AK plays well in all-in scenarios if needed. Position + hand strength + stack urgency all favor strong aggression here.

With Q6o in the Small Blind against a Button raise from Claude, who is the short stack (2575 chips), this hand is too weak to call or 3-bet. Claude's raise to 400 into his 2575 stack suggests he is willing to commit, and Q6o does not have enough equity against even a wide Button opening range. Additionally, Minni has a massive stack in the Big Blind and could easily squeeze or outplay me post-flop. I will fold and wait for a better spot in the final hands.

Claude (button) opened to 400 with ~25bb behind. I hold KQo in the big blind — a standard defend. Calling 300 more (400 total committed) is cheap relative to the 550 pot and keeps his opening range wide; 3-betting risks getting jammed or bloating an OOP pot unnecessarily. With my big stack advantage I can apply post‑flop pressure or fold to heavy action, and KQo has good equity versus a wide button opening range. I’ll call and evaluate on the flop.

I called preflop to keep his opening range wide and keep the pot small. Flop 4h‑3d‑2d is low and coordinated with two diamonds — Claude’s button opening range will include a lot of diamond draws, small pairs and wide broadways. Out of position with K♥Q♣ I don't have a draw but I have two clean overcards and decent equity vs a wide range. I want to charge draws and fold out small weak hands while keeping the pot manageable vs his 25bb stack, so I’ll make a small probe/continuation bet (~1/3 pot). Betting 300 accomplishes denial/price-setting and gives me information: if he folds I pick up the pot, if he calls I can double-barrel or re-evaluate on the turn, and if he jams I’ll re-evaluate based on his shove frequency (wide shoves get called, tight shoves can be folded).

Calling with AK on a dry 4-3-2 flop. I have approximately 40-50% equity against Minni's likely range. The modest bet sizing (300 into 1150) doesn't suggest an overly strong hand. My overcards and wheel draw potential (any 5 = straight) give me multiple ways to win. Given my short stack position, preserving chips while maintaining equity is sound. The call is appropriately sized relative to my stack and pot odds are favorable at ~4:1.

Turn 5♦ completes the diamond draw on a 4♥-3♦-2♦-5♦ board. I put in a small C‑bet on the flop and Claude called — his button calling range naturally includes a lot of diamond combos (A♦x♦, K♦x♦, Q♦x♦), small pairs and some broadways. Now that the flush is live I have no diamond, no pair and just two overcards (K♥Q♣). Betting here will mainly get called by hands that beat me (made flushes or diamonds) or force a shove from a short stack, which puts me in an undesirable, high‑risk decision for little extra EV. I prefer pot control: check and see what Claude does. If he bets a small probe I’m folding most of the time; if he checks back I’ll evaluate the river and look for a low‑risk bluff spot or a cheap showdown.

Betting for value with a wheel straight. Minni's turn check indicates a weak holding that likely didn't improve on the diamond-heavy board. A bet of 800 (roughly half pot) is aggressive enough to apply pressure while attractive enough to get called by marginal hands like paired board or draws. My short stack and strong hand warrant value extraction now rather than checking through.

Turn check was pot-control — I have K♥Q♣ with no diamond and two overcards only. Claude (button) called my flop C-bet and now bets 800 into ~2250 after the 5♦ — a small but meaningful probe that often represents a made hand (two diamonds -> turn flush, 6x straights, or strong small pairs/value hands) or a single-diamond semi-bluff that still has heavy equity vs me on the river. Calling 800 requires ~26% equity to break even, but I only have ~6 raw outs to pair (many of which are diamonds that would likely give his range the nut-flush), so my realistic equity is well below break-even. Raising to 1600 to force his short stack all‑in is a thin, high‑variance line: if he folds we scoop, but if he calls I’m up against many hands that dominate me. With a very deep overall stack I can pick better spots later — on this turn facing a small value/semibluff bet with no diamond and no pair, folding preserves equity. I fold.

I am on the button with Ace-Jack offsuit, which is a very strong starting hand in a three-handed game. My stack is healthy (72 BBs), but Claude is quite short-stacked (36 BBs). Raising to 300 is a standard opening size that allows me to build a pot, put pressure on the blinds, and maintain control of the hand. With only 5 hands remaining in the tournament, I need to maximize value with my premium holdings while being mindful of the short stack's potential to shove.

Qs9o (Qs9d) in the small blind vs a standard button open to 300 is a marginal, often-dominated holding. To call I must invest another 250 out of position against a wide but largely stronger raising range (AQ/ KQ/ Qx/ broadways) and risk getting multi-way action with Claude (BB) — or facing aggressive continuation/3-bets. With a 29k stack I don’t need to defend every blind; this is an easy fold to preserve chips for a better spot where I have position or a stronger hand. I’ll fold and wait for profitable opportunities.

Folding J7o out of position against Flash's button raise. While short-stacked, J7o lacks sufficient equity (~32%) to profitably call out of position. With only 4 hands remaining, I should preserve chips for premium holdings where I have better equity or positional advantage.

Button with A♦7♥ in a 3-handed pot — a very playable hand in position with strong stealing potential. Pot is 150 and it costs 100 to see the flop; facing two blinds (Claude ~35bb, Flash ~72bb), I want to take the initiative and deny good odds to callers while preserving flexibility against 3-bets. A standard 3x open in 3-max (to 300 total) gives solid fold equity and keeps the pot manageable versus possible shoves from Claude. Plan: if called, use positional advantage to c-bet appropriate flops and extract value on A-high boards; if 3-bet large/shoved, re-evaluate based on stack sizes and ranges (fold to very strong jams, call only with favorable pot odds/reads).

With Q8o from small blind against Minni's raise, this is a weak hand that doesn't warrant investing 250 more chips. I'm short-stacked at 3575 with only 4 hands remaining in the tournament. Q8 is far below the threshold of hands I should play from out of position without a strong reason. While I have decent pot odds (450 to 250), the hand quality is poor and I'll have better opportunities to push my remaining chips. Folding preserves 3325 chips for the final 3 hands where I may find premium holdings or favorable situations.

I am in the Big Blind with Ace-Four offsuit. The Button (Minni) has raised to 300, which is a standard open. Claude has folded the Small Blind. I have an Ace and 20.25 big blinds (7250 chips). This hand is strong enough to defend against a Button range, especially given the tournament context where I need to protect my stack and pick up pots. I can call here to see a flop and realize my equity, as raising might commit too much of my stack if I'm four-bet.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a paired board. Ace-high has some showdown value and I do not want to build a pot yet. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni acts on this flop.

I'm on the button with A♦7♥; I opened to 300 preflop and the BB called, so my range is wide and I have position. Flop is 6♠ Q♠ Q♥ — a paired Q-high board with two spades. Flash's check is ambiguous (could be a slow-played Q, a middle pair, a missed broadway/connector, or a spade draw). I have only Ace-high but decent blocker value to some Ax combos and good positional leverage. I want to use a small continuation bet to fold out missed hands and charge spade draws while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called. Size ~1/2 pot is enough to apply pressure without committing too much; if I'm check-raised or face serious aggression I will fold A7 (it's a vulnerable hand), and if called I'll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously (check-call versus small bets, fold to large barrels unless I improve). Betting also preserves fold equity and exploits the BB's passive check-range here.

I have Ace-high with the nut flush draw (As on a 6s Qs Qh board). Minni's bet of 325 into a 650 pot is a small continuation bet that could come from many hands in her wide button opening range. With my overcard and the nut flush draw, I have significant equity to continue. Calling is the best option to realize this equity without bloating the pot out of position, especially since I can represent a variety of hands on later streets if the flush completes.

On a board of 6s Qs Qh Ts, I have Ace-high and the nut flush draw with the Ace of Spades. Since I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor, checking is the most prudent action. This allows me to see how Minni proceeds and gives me the opportunity to realize my equity by calling a bet or seeing a free river card. I have significant equity with the nut draw, and I do not want to turn my hand into a bluff here by leading out.

Flop c-bet was called on Q-Q-6 with two spades; turn brings T♠ making three spades on board. I hold A♦7♥ — no pair and no spade blocker — so I have very little showdown value and relatively weak bluff equity versus a caller who can plausibly have Qx, a spade flush, or a ten/medium pair. Flash’s check on the turn is ambiguous (weakness or a trap), but betting here would be a thin bluff that risks a check-raise or large call from better hands. Pot control and position are important: I can realize my equity by checking and see a free river, then bluff or fold based on the river texture and opponent action. Plan: check the turn to avoid bloating the pot with air; if Flash fires a small/medium bet I’ll fold to large, consider a call only to river if size is tiny or the line looks like a missed draw, and look to take a cheap river stab on a safe card.

I have Ace-high (As 4d) on a board of 6s Qs Qh Ts Kc. I had the nut flush draw on the turn, but the river was the King of Clubs, so the flush did not complete. Minni checked back the turn after betting the flop, which often indicates a medium-strength hand or a draw that she is willing to take to showdown. My Ace-high has some showdown value against her total air or lower missed draws (like 98, 87, etc.). Leading out as a bluff doesn't make much sense given my check-call line, so I will check and hope to see a cheap showdown. If she bets, I'll likely have to fold since the King is a card that hits a reasonable portion of a button opening range that would check the turn.

I have A♦7♥ on Kc / Qs Qh Ts 6s — no pair and no spade. Pot is 1,300 and Flash (BB) called my flop c-bet, then checked turn and checked the river after I checked the turn. His line is consistent with a range of Qx/medium pairs, missed spade draws, or a cautious Kx — hands that will often call or fold very rarely to a river bluff. I have poor blocker coverage to K and no showdown value worth risking chips to fold out only very weak hands. Betting here would be a thin bluff with low fold equity and a decent chance of being called by worse or check‑raised by better. With stacks deep relative to the pot and only three hands left in the tournament, preserving chips and avoiding a marginal bluff is the highest EV play. Action: check to the showdown and accept that my Ace‑high will lose to many of Flash’s calling/standpat hands.

Raising with premium pocket pair from button. QQ is a strong hand that should be played aggressively. A 3x BB raise to 300 builds the pot while maintaining good stack preservation. Position and hand strength both favor aggression here in the final hands of the tournament.

I am in the Small Blind with Jd 7d, which is a decent suited connector. Claude, the button, has raised to 300. He is short-stacked with only 3275 chips. Calling here allows me to see a flop and potentially outplay him post-flop, or hit a strong draw. Folding is too tight given the suited potential, and raising might commit too much of my stack if Minni (the big stack) wakes up with a hand or if Claude jams. I will call the additional 250.

Pot is 700; I need to call 200 (pot odds ≈22%). I hold 6♠2♣ — the worst offsuit holding. Facing a BTN open and a small‑blind call, this is a multiway pot where my hand has almost no showdown value and very poor playability postflop. My equity vs two calling ranges is well below the break‑even threshold, and a 3‑bet bluff here with 6‑2o is unnecessary and high variance. With a healthy stack, I should conserve chips for better spots — fold.

I am out of position against the pre-flop aggressor on a relatively dry board. My hand, Jack-high with a backdoor flush draw, hasn't improved much. Standard play here is to check to the raiser and see how he reacts. Claude has a relatively short stack, so I need to be careful about committing chips without a solid draw or pair.

Betting 450 with QQ on a dry flop after Flash checks. This continues my aggressive pre-flop line, builds the pot with a strong hand, and applies pressure. Sizing is about 65% of the current pot, which is standard for a continuation bet in this spot. The board (3-10-6) doesn't help most SB calling ranges, and Flash's check indicates weakness. With limited hands remaining in the tournament, accumulating chips with premium hands is crucial.

I am out of position on a dry 10-high flop. My hand, Jack-high, has no direct value and only very weak backdoor potential. Claude is a short-stacked player who is likely to commit his remaining chips if he has any piece of the board or a high pocket pair. Calling here would require me to likely face another bet on the turn with very little equity. Since there are only a few hands left in the tournament, preserving my stack to secure my second-place position is more important than chasing a marginal spot with Jack-high. I will fold.

I am on the button with Qd3d. My stack is 70.75 big blinds (7075 chips). Claude, the Big Blind, is short-stacked with only 3875 chips (38.75 BB), while Minni is the chip leader. In a 3-handed game, the button should be opening a wide range. While Qd3d is at the bottom of a typical opening range, the presence of a short stack in the big blind who may be playing conservatively or can be put under pressure makes an open viable. However, since I am guaranteed to play another hand (Hand 150) and want to protect my stack while staying competitive, a standard 3x raise is appropriate here to take the blinds or play in position.

Facing an unopened raise to 300 from the button while I’m in the small blind with 9♠4♦ — a weak, unsuited, disconnected hand — I’m out of position versus at least one active opponent (the big blind). Calling 250 to play a likely multiway pot OOP gives me poor equity and little post‑flop maneuverability; a small 3‑bet won’t credibly isolate and a shove is unnecessary risk with a comfortable stack. With two shorter stacks behind me and no strong hand to proceed, the +EV play is to preserve my deep stack and fold now.

Defending big blind in heads-up with favorable pot odds. 7-8 connector has sufficient equity against Flash's button raise range. Only need 31% equity with 2.25:1 odds. Standard big blind defense play in final hands of tournament.

Betting 375 on the flop with 7-8 on a 5-9-9 board. I have an open-ended straight draw with 8 outs and reasonable equity (~32%). Flash's check suggests weakness. In this heads-up final hand scenario, applying pressure is important given my chip disadvantage. A moderate bet accomplishes multiple goals: generates fold equity, builds the pot if ahead, and maintains my equity when called. This bet size is reasonable relative to my remaining stack while keeping me in tournament contention.

I raised pre-flop with Qd3d and Claude called from the big blind. The flop comes 5c-9h-9d, and Claude leads out with a bet of 375. While I have two overcards and a backdoor diamond draw, this 'donk bet' on a paired board from a short stack often indicates a made hand like a 5, a pocket pair, or occasionally trip 9s. Since we are at Hand 149 of 150, I want to preserve my stack to secure my position in the tournament. Q-high is unlikely to be the best hand here, and I don't have enough equity to justify calling and potentially facing another bet on the turn with only one hand remaining in the session.

Pocket eights on the button in a 3-handed pot is a strong, playable hand with great positional advantage. I want to take the initiative, build the pot, and earn fold equity against the blinds while keeping the size reasonable against two fairly deep, sometimes-aggressive opponents. A standard 3x BB open (300 total) achieves that: it isolates and prices out very weak hands, lets me play postflop in position, and keeps options open (c-bet on favorable flops, or proceed cautiously if I face a big 3-bet). If either blind 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and stack depth; if I get called I’ll use position to gain information and extract value on appropriate runouts.

Calling with A6o from the small blind. Minni has been aggressive from the button throughout the session with a dominant stack. The pot odds of 1.8:1 justify seeing a flop with an ace. I'm the shortest stack (4,275), so I need to play hands that can win pots. Against Minni's typical button raise range, this ace has reasonable equity, and I can evaluate further post-flop. If I hit an ace, I likely have the best hand; if I miss, I can fold to continuation bets without being pot-committed.

I am holding King-Deuce suited in the Big Blind on the final hand of the tournament. The Button (Minni) raised to 300 and the Small Blind (Claude) called. I need to put in 200 more chips to see the flop in a pot that will be 900. These pot odds (22.2%) are excellent for a suited King. While I cannot catch the leader (Minni), I want to maximize my final stack and defend against the short-stack (Claude). Calling is the most sensible play to realize my equity.

Checking with A6o on Qs 7d 9c. This is a marginal holding facing two opponents when I'm the shortest stack. Checking allows me to see if Minni c-bets (highly likely given her aggression pattern throughout the session), and then make an informed decision about whether my ace is good. If both opponents check, I get a free card to improve. Betting here with a weak kicker doesn't make sense - I'd either get called by better aces or pairs, or win a small pot. Checking maximizes my information and keeps me disciplined with my short stack.

This is the final hand of the tournament. I have King-high with a backdoor club draw on a board of Queen-Seven-Nine. I am out of position against the chip leader (Minni) and in a pot with the short stack (Claude). Since I have no made hand and limited equity, leading out into two players would be an unnecessary risk. My goal is to maximize my final stack. Claude has already checked, so I will check as well and see how Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, reacts.

I’m the preflop raiser on the button with pocket eights in a 3‑way pot. The flop Qs‑7d‑9c is fairly dry and both opponents checked, showing weakness/missed boards. A small continuation bet applies pressure and achieves two goals: takes the pot down now when they are weak, and gets value from worse pairs/marginal holdings (9x, 7x, KJ, A9, etc.) without bloating the pot against two callers. I size modest (~1/3 pot = 300) to maximize fold equity vs two players while keeping worse hands in to extract value. If I get called I’ll use position to re‑evaluate on the turn; if I face a large raise I’ll assume I’m likely behind and fold/reassess based on sizing.

Calling the 300 chip bet on the flop with A6o on Qs 7d 9c. My ace is likely the best hand currently - both Minni (heavy aggression throughout) and Flash (who checked) likely have weaker holdings. The pot odds are excellent at 5:1 (calling 300 to win 1,500), and with my short stack of 4,025, I need to play hands that can win significant pots. An ace on this board is a strong holding. Flash's check suggests weakness, and Minni's bet could easily be a standard c-bet from her aggressive button range. Calling allows me to see the turn card while keeping the pot manageable. If I miss on the turn, I can reassess, but currently my ace has good equity and the math supports the call.

On the final hand of the tournament, I am in the Big Blind with King-high and a backdoor club draw on a Queen-Nine-Seven rainbow board. Minni (the chip leader) has continued with a 1/3 pot bet, and Claude (the short stack) has called. Claude's call is particularly concerning as he is relatively short-stacked and unlikely to call without a pair or a strong draw (like Jack-Ten). With only three outs to a King (which might not even be the best hand if someone has a Queen) and no immediate draws, my equity in this three-way pot is likely below the 16.6% pot odds offered. Since my goal is to maximize my final stack over these 150 hands, I will fold and preserve my remaining 6,175 chips rather than chasing a marginal hand with low probability of success.

The turn card (7h) paired the board, and my As6d is now just ace-high with a weak kicker facing a highly aggressive opponent with a dominant stack. While I was justified calling the flop bet at 5:1 odds with top card leverage, the turn pairing dramatically weakens my hand's equity. Against Minni's wide c-betting range, I could easily be behind queens, nines, sevens, or stronger cards. My stack is critically short at 3,725 chips—any bet I face will likely pot-commit me. Checking here allows me to: (1) see if Minni continues her aggression pattern, (2) keep options open if she checks and I get a free river, or (3) fold if she bets and I determine my ace-high isn't good. With a weak holding and limited resources, there's no value in taking the lead out of position.

Check. Claude called my flop bet and the turn paired (7), which heavily favors his calling range (Qx, 9x, and especially any 7x now turned into trips). My pocket eights have only two clean outs to a full house on the river (~4% to improve), so I’m almost always behind to the hands that continued on the flop. Betting here would build the pot for very little value and invite a shove or raise from a short-stacked opponent with a hand that already beats me. Pot‑control and seeing the river cheaply is the higher EV line — check and re‑evaluate on the river (call small bluffs, fold to large aggression unless the river changes the board dramatically).

River showdown decision with As6d on Qs 7d 9c 7h Kh. Minni's turn check signals weakness—she would value bet legitimate holdings on a paired board. My Ace-high has meaningful showdown value against her likely air/weak range. Betting accomplishes nothing: I lose to pairs, win against air in both cases. Checking guarantees I see showdown while preserving my critically short stack. This is the optimal risk/reward in the final hand.

Check. I opened preflop with 8♣8♠ and c‑bet the Q‑7‑9 flop; Claude called. The turn paired the 7 so his continuing range (Qx, 9x, single 7s that can become trips/quads, and some medium pairs) improved or became much more comfortable checking for pot control. The river K♣/K♥ is a very scary card — it leaves very few worse hands that would call a river bet and a lot of hands that already beat my pair of eights. Betting here would be a thin value attempt at best and a bluff at worst, but Claude is likely to call small bets with many of the hands that beat me and can jam his short stack if he’s strong. There’s little fold equity and a high chance of getting called or check‑raised into a costly decision. Pot control + showdown is the higher EV line: check and fold to significant aggression, show down if he checks back.
